Terminate by mowing or grazing before it bolts in spring, then incorporate the residue with light tillage. In colder zones repeated hard freezes may winterkill the crop, easing termination.

plant_note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost; produces a large, soil-penetrating taproot that winter-kills in cold climates, leaving a decomposed channel.
directions: Broadcast seed at 8–10 lb/acre or drill at 6–7 lb/acre and cover to 1/4 in depth; forage radish emerges quickly and develops a taproot up to 18 in deep that fractures compacted subsoil and scavenges nitrates before they leach. In USDA zones 5 and colder the taproot reliably winter-kills, leaving a hollow channel that improves spring drainage and air porosity. Where winters are mild, mow the rosette before it bolts in spring to avoid a weedy seed bank. The decomposing roots have a pungent sulfur smell for 1–2 weeks after freeze-kill — this is normal.

plant_note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost; forage turnip produces heavy leaf canopy that smothers weeds and a fleshy taproot that winterkills to release nutrients.
directions: Broadcast or drill seed at 1–2 lb/acre and cover to 1/4 in depth; forage turnip is faster-maturing and less prone to overwintering than oilseed radish, making it a reliable fall cover where a quick canopy is needed. It scavenges residual nitrogen and suppresses winter annual weeds under its broad leaves. The bulbous root winter-kills in hard frost zones and decomposes rapidly, releasing stored nutrients at planting time. In mild climates it may overwinter and bolt in spring — mow before flowering to prevent volunteer seedlings in subsequent crops.

plant_note: Wait until soil is thoroughly warm before sowing this heat-loving annual.
directions: Broadcast at roughly 0.5-1 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover about 0.5 inch deep in warm, firm soil. Foxtail millet is exceptionally drought tolerant and matures quickly, making it ideal for filling short summer windows between cash crops. Its fibrous roots improve soil tilth while the dense canopy shades out weeds. Mow before the seed heads harden to prevent reseeding, then incorporate or leave the residue as surface mulch. In cold regions frost will terminate any remaining plants at season's end.

plant_note: Sow after last frost in hot, dry climates; exceptional drought tolerance makes this the best legume cover for arid Southwest summers.
directions: Direct-sow inoculated seed 1 in deep at 20–30 lb/acre into warm, well-drained soil after all frost danger has passed; guar is native to arid Asia and thrives in sandy, alkaline soils with limited irrigation. It fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules and produces significant biomass even in drought conditions, making it invaluable in the semi-arid Southwest and high plains. Terminate by mowing at early pod formation before seed fully hardens; the dense residue builds organic matter in soils chronically short of it. Avoid waterlogged sites — standing water causes rapid root rot.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first frost, often mixed with winter rye which gives it something to climb.
directions: Sow seed about 1/2 in deep, ideally inoculated with the correct rhizobia and paired with a grass like rye for support. It overwinters and fixes a large amount of nitrogen by spring bloom. Terminate at early flowering for maximum nitrogen and easy breakdown.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant warm-season cover-crop legumes.
directions: Broadcast or drill inoculated seed at 40–50 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 25 sq ft), 1/2 in deep in warm, well-drained soil; cowpeas germinate and grow rapidly, fixing 100–150 lb of nitrogen per acre while suppressing summer weeds with their spreading vines. The crop tolerates sandy, infertile, and slightly acidic soils where most legumes struggle. Terminate by mowing at early pod stage before seeds mature to prevent volunteer plants; large amounts of vine biomass decompose quickly in warm weather. Pairs well with sorghum-sudangrass for a combined legume-grass summer cover.

plant_note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost when soil reaches at least 60 F.
directions: Broadcast at about 0.5-1 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a moist seedbed and rake lightly to cover 0.25-0.5 inch deep. Japanese millet germinates fast in warm soil and tolerates temporarily wet or flooded ground better than most summer cover crops. It produces abundant biomass in as little as six to eight weeks and can be used to smother summer weeds in fallow beds. Terminate by mowing before seed set to avoid volunteers, then incorporate residue or leave as mulch. Frost will kill the crop, providing free termination ahead of fall or spring plantings.

plant_note: Sow in spring; slow to establish but forms a long-lived rhizomatous stand.
directions: Inoculate with the specific kura clover rhizobia strain and broadcast at about 0.2-0.4 lb per 1,000 sq ft into a firm, weed-free seedbed no more than 0.25 inch deep. Kura clover establishes slowly during its first year because it invests heavily in rhizomes, but once established it forms a persistent living mulch. Its spreading roots fix nitrogen and provide erosion control and pollinator forage for many years. Manage rather than fully terminate it by mowing or suppressing with strip tillage so it regrows between cash crops. For complete removal, repeated tillage or cultivation is required because of its vigorous rhizomes.

plant_note: Sow after last frost in warm soil; an exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant warm-season legume cover from Africa.
directions: Plant inoculated seed 1 in deep after soil temperatures exceed 65°F; lablab vines grow vigorously and can climb trellises or sprawl as a smothering ground cover. It fixes up to 200 lb N/acre equivalent under good conditions and tolerates low-fertility and mildly saline soils where cowpea struggles. Terminate by mowing or chopping at early pod set; raw mature pods contain toxic lectins, so the foliage should not be eaten without cooking. In frost-free zones it can be managed as a short-lived perennial ground cover.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost; oats winter-kill below about 10°F, leaving a ready-to-plant mat of residue in spring without tillage.
directions: Broadcast seed at 50–80 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 30 sq ft) and rake to 1/2 in depth; oats establish quickly and produce substantial biomass before being killed by hard freezes. Their winter-killed residue lies flat as a weed-suppressing mulch that is easy to plant or transplant through in spring without additional termination work. Often mixed with field peas or hairy vetch to add nitrogen to the primarily carbonaceous grass residue. In mild-winter regions (zones 8–9), oats may overwinter and require mowing to terminate before spring planting.

plant_note: Sow six to eight weeks before frost so plants form a strong rosette to overwinter.
directions: Broadcast at about 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover 0.25-0.5 inch deep in a firm seedbed. Winter-type oilseed rape develops a deep taproot that breaks up compacted subsoil and scavenges nitrogen and sulfur. Overwintering plants bolt and flower in spring, providing early forage for pollinators if allowed to bloom. Terminate by mowing at early flowering before pods set, then incorporate the residue with tillage. Allow two to three weeks of breakdown before planting the following crop to avoid nitrogen tie-up.

plant_note: Sow after last frost in warm soil; the most heat- and drought-tolerant warm-season cover grass, thriving in Gulf Coast summers.
directions: Drill or broadcast seed 1/2 in deep into warm soil; pearl millet germinates and establishes faster than sorghum-sudangrass in sandy, low-fertility soils. It produces large amounts of biomass for smothering weeds and building organic matter, and tolerate short dry spells without wilting like corn or sorghum can. Mow or terminate before seed heads mature to prevent unwanted reseeding. Mix with cowpeas for a combined nitrogen-fixing and biomass cover.

plant_note: Plant rhizome sprigs in late spring once soil is warm; establishment is slow the first season but the stand fills in by year 2.
directions: Plant rhizome sections 2–3 in deep in well-drained, sandy or loamy soil; perennial peanut tolerates poor, acidic soils typical of Gulf Coast landscapes. Establishment is slow the first year but the low, dense mat fixes nitrogen and crowds out weeds without mowing once established. It can be used as a lawn alternative, orchard under-planting, or living mulch in vegetable corridors. In zones 8–9 the top may freeze back in hard winters but the rhizomes resprout reliably from below ground.

plant_note: Sow in late summer to early fall; perennial ryegrass establishes fastest of all cool-season grasses and forms dense sod for erosion control.
directions: Broadcast seed at 20–30 lb/acre and rake lightly to 1/4 in depth; perennial ryegrass germinates in 5–7 days at optimal temperatures and establishes the fastest ground cover of any cool-season grass, making it excellent for emergency erosion control or orchard middles. It can be used as a short-rotation annual cover in cold winters (zones 3–5) or managed as a persistent sod in mild climates. Terminate before planting vegetables by aggressive mowing followed by solarization or herbicide; perennial ryegrass regrows readily from crowns and requires thorough kill. Mix with red or white clover for a nitrogen-fixing living mulch under fruit trees.

plant_note: Sow 2–6 weeks before last spring frost, or in early fall in mild climates; flowers arrive in about 6 weeks and are among the best for attracting bees.
directions: Broadcast tiny seed at 3–5 lb per acre (a light dusting per 100 sq ft) on the surface of a firm, moist seedbed without covering, as seed needs light to germinate; rake in very lightly if birds are a problem. Phacelia establishes quickly in cool soil, outcompeting many weeds with dense, ferny foliage that also conserves soil moisture. The blue-purple flowers are exceptional for beneficial insects and bees — allow partial bloom before mowing to maximize pollinator benefit. Terminate by mowing just as the last flowers fade; it decomposes rapidly and leaves no allelopathic residue.

plant_note: Sow in fall in mild-winter regions; it is less cold hardy than hairy vetch.
directions: Inoculate with vetch-pea rhizobia and broadcast at about 1-2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, raking to cover 1 inch deep. Purple vetch is a fast-growing annual legume best suited to mild-winter climates where it fixes nitrogen through the cool season. It climbs readily, so pairing it with a cereal companion improves standability and biomass. Terminate by mowing or crimping at early bloom before viable seed forms to prevent weedy volunteers. Incorporate the succulent residue or leave it as a nitrogen-rich mulch two to three weeks before planting.

plant_note: Sow in late summer to early fall for quick fall cover that scavenges nutrients.
directions: Broadcast at about 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover 0.25 inch deep in a moist seedbed. This fast-growing brassica establishes quickly, forming a fibrous root mat that takes up excess nitrogen and reduces leaching over winter. It is less cold hardy than oilseed rape and often winterkills in colder regions, providing free termination. Where it survives, mow at early flowering and incorporate the residue two to three weeks before planting. The rapid canopy closure also smothers fall-germinating weeds.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or frost-seed in late winter; short-lived perennial that fixes nitrogen and blooms its second year.
directions: Broadcast inoculated seed onto a prepared bed at 8–10 lb per acre, raking to 1/4 in depth; red clover tolerates slightly acidic soils and moderate shade better than most legume cover crops. It overwinters in zones 4 and warmer and puts on vigorous growth in early spring, often topping 2 ft before bloom. Terminate at early flower stage — before the first florets open to brown — to capture maximum fixed nitrogen of 80–150 lb per acre. A second flush of growth often follows mowing, allowing a second cut of biomass before final incorporation.

plant_note: Sow in early spring; the deep taproot exploits subsoil moisture and breaks compaction.
directions: Sow seed 1-1.5 inches deep at about 0.5-1 lb per 1,000 sq ft into warm, well-drained soil after the ground can be worked. Safflower develops a strong taproot that penetrates several feet, breaking up compacted subsoil and drawing up moisture and nutrients from depth. It is highly drought tolerant, making it a useful soil-building cover for dry sites and rotations. Terminate by mowing before the spiny flower heads mature, since the bracts become sharp and seed can volunteer. Incorporate the residue or leave it as surface mulch to protect the soil and improve organic matter.

plant_note: Sow in spring into well-drained, calcareous soil; not tolerant of wet ground.
directions: Inoculate with the specific sainfoin rhizobia and drill or broadcast at about 1-2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, covering 0.5-1 inch deep in well-drained soil. Sainfoin is a deep-rooted perennial legume that fixes nitrogen, thrives on dry alkaline soils, and produces showy pink blooms that are excellent for pollinators. Its non-bloating forage and deep taproot make it a durable soil-building crop where drainage is good. Manage by mowing after the first flush of bloom to encourage regrowth, or terminate mature stands with tillage before planting a following crop. It performs poorly on acidic or waterlogged sites, so match it to suitable ground.

scientific_name: Sorghum bicolor × Sorghum bicolor var. sudanense
plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; produces massive biomass for weed suppression and soil building.
directions: Broadcast or drill seed 3/4 in deep into warm soil; sorghum-sudangrass hybrids germinate and grow aggressively, reaching 6–8 feet before termination. The dense canopy suppresses even aggressive perennial weeds, and allelopathic root exudates further inhibit weed germination. Mow to 4–6 in to encourage regrowth and additional tillering before final termination; allow residue 2–3 weeks to partially break down before transplanting into it. Avoid planting in soybean rotations immediately after, as the sorghum-family residue can briefly stunt legume establishment.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked for a fast summer smother crop.
directions: Broadcast or drill at roughly 2-3 lb per 1,000 sq ft into a firm seedbed and rake to cover about 1 inch deep. Spring oats germinate quickly in cool soil and produce dense biomass that outcompetes weeds and captures nitrogen. Unlike winter oats, spring oats will not survive hard freezes, which makes winterkill a convenient no-till termination in cold climates. In mild areas terminate by mowing before seed heads form, then incorporate or leave residue as surface mulch. Follow with a nitrogen-fixing legume to balance the carbon added by the grass residue.

plant_note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost in mild-winter climates; uniquely reseeds itself by burying its own seed pods underground.
directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 15–20 lb/acre onto a well-prepared seedbed in late summer to early fall; subterranean clover tolerates poorly drained and acidic soils better than most annual clovers. The prostrate stems root at nodes and the spent flower heads push seed capsules into the soil surface, allowing the stand to self-reseed year after year without replanting. It fixes moderate nitrogen and forms a dense mat that suppresses winter and spring weeds. Mow or graze lightly in late winter to stimulate pod development, then allow to dry before any tillage.

plant_note: Sow after last frost once soil is consistently warm; a standout warm-season nitrogen fixer and nematode suppressor for zone 9–11.
directions: Broadcast or drill seed 1/2 in deep into warm, well-drained soil after danger of frost has passed. Sunn hemp grows rapidly — up to 6 feet in two months — and fixes 100–200 lb of nitrogen per acre while suppressing root-knot nematodes through root exudates. Terminate by mowing at first bloom (roughly 60–70 days) before seed set, since mature plants become woody and slow to decompose. Do not plant where livestock can graze, as the foliage contains alkaloids toxic to most grazing animals.

plant_note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost; the deep taproot breaks up compacted soil and winter-kills in cold zones.
directions: Broadcast seed about 1/2 in deep in late summer; the thick taproot drills deep and shatters compaction. In cold-winter areas it dies and rots in place, leaving channels and released nutrients. Where it survives, mow before it flowers to prevent seeding.

plant_note: Sow 4–6 weeks before first frost in fall; combines the cold-hardiness of rye with the palatability and residue quality of wheat.
directions: Drill or broadcast seed at 60–90 lb/acre and cover to 1 in depth in fall; triticale is a wheat-rye hybrid that is nearly as cold-hardy as cereal rye but produces softer, lower-lignin residue that breaks down faster in spring. It scavenges soil nitrogen over winter and produces abundant biomass for weed suppression and organic matter. Terminate in early spring at the jointing stage (before the seed head emerges from the boot) by rolling, flail-mowing, or tillage; residue breaks down within 3–4 weeks in warm spring conditions. Pairs well with hairy vetch to combine nitrogen fixation with grass biomass.

plant_note: Sow after last frost; velvet bean is the most aggressive warm-season smothering cover legume, capable of blanketing 100+ sq ft per plant.
directions: Plant large seeds 1–2 in deep at 40–60 lb/acre (or 12 in apart in garden rows) after the soil is consistently warm; velvet bean vines scramble aggressively over weeds, fixing up to 400 lb N/acre equivalent while out-competing even nutsedge and Bermuda grass. All plant parts carry stinging trichomes — wear long sleeves and gloves when handling. Terminate by mowing or machete-chopping at flowering, then incorporate for a massive nitrogen and organic-matter boost. The hard, shiny seeds require scarification or a 24-hour soak before planting to improve germination.

plant_note: Frost-seed onto bare or thin ground in late winter to early spring; the freeze-thaw cycle works seed into the soil surface naturally.
directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 1/4–1/2 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a lightly raked surface; the tiny seed needs good soil contact but should not be covered more than 1/4 in. White clover spreads by stolons to form a dense living mulch that fixes nitrogen and feeds beneficial insects with nectar-rich flowers. Mow periodically to prevent flowering from shading out adjacent crops, or allow it to bloom freely when used as a pollinator corridor. In annual vegetable beds, turn the stand under in early spring and wait 2–3 weeks before planting so the nitrogen flush is available to the next crop.

plant_note: Plant in late summer for fast fall biomass; it winterkills in most climates.
directions: Broadcast at roughly 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a firm, moist seedbed and rake to a shallow 0.25 inch depth. White mustard germinates rapidly in cool soil and is often grown to suppress cyst nematodes and weeds. It grows quickly but is less winter hardy than brown mustard, so it typically winterkills once temperatures drop into the low twenties. Terminate by mowing at early bloom and incorporating the residue, or simply let frost kill it and leave the residue as protective mulch. Follow with a spring vegetable once residue has broken down.

plant_note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; a fast-growing brassica smother crop with biofumigant properties.
directions: Broadcast seed at 8–10 lb per acre (about 1/4 oz per 100 sq ft) onto a firm seedbed, raking to 1/4 in depth; mustard germinates in cold soil within days and establishes an aggressive canopy that smothers weeds. When chopped and incorporated at early flower stage, the glucosinolates in the tissue break down into biofumigant compounds that suppress soil-borne pathogens and weed seeds — a technique called biofumigation. Mow and immediately till to a 4–6 in depth, then water and tarp the bed for 2 weeks to trap fumigant gases. Terminate before it sets seed, as mustard reseeds abundantly and can become a weed.

plant_note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, or in late summer 6–8 weeks before frost; a biennial that fixes up to 150 lb N/acre in its second year.
directions: Scarify or inoculate seed with Rhizobium meliloti, then broadcast at 10–15 lb/acre and cover lightly; white sweet clover tolerates alkaline, poor soils where other clovers fail. The plant forms a taproot in year one and overwinters to bloom prolifically in year two, fixing enormous nitrogen and feeding pollinators. Terminate in year two at early bud stage before stems become woody; chop and incorporate so the high-coumarin stems have several weeks to break down before planting. Avoid using freshly mowed green residue as mulch around brassicas, as coumarin can temporarily inhibit germination.

plant_note: Plant in early to mid fall so plants establish crowns before winter dormancy.
directions: Drill or broadcast at 2-3 lb per 1,000 sq ft about 1 inch deep into a firm, well-drained seedbed. Winter barley tolerates drought and saline soils better than other cereals and releases allelopathic compounds that suppress weeds. It is less cold hardy than rye or wheat, so it performs best in USDA zones 6 and warmer. Terminate in spring by mowing or crimping at the boot stage before pollen shed, then incorporate or leave as mulch. Barley residue breaks down faster than rye, allowing earlier planting of the succeeding crop.

plant_note: Sow from 6 weeks before to just after first frost; the most cold-hardy cover crop, germinating in near-freezing soil.
directions: Broadcast seed at about 1/2 in depth in fall; it germinates in cold soil when little else will. It scavenges leftover nitrogen and smothers weeds over winter, then puts on fast spring growth. Mow and turn under 2–3 weeks before planting, as its residue briefly suppresses small seeds.

plant_note: Drill or broadcast 4-6 weeks before hard freeze so plants tiller before dormancy.
directions: Sow at 60-120 lb per acre or about 2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, drilling 1-1.5 inches deep or broadcasting onto a firm, moist seedbed. Winter wheat overwinters as a rosette, resumes growth in spring, and scavenges residual soil nitrogen while suppressing weeds. It is easier to terminate than cereal rye, making it a good choice for gardeners. Terminate in spring by mowing at the boot to early-flowering stage, then incorporate with tillage or crimp and leave residue as mulch. Allow two to three weeks of decomposition before planting the following crop.

plant_note: Sow in early fall; it matures earlier than common hairy vetch.
directions: Inoculate seed and drill or broadcast at about 1-2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, covering to 1 inch deep in a firm seedbed. Woolly pod vetch is an early-maturing relative of hairy vetch that fixes nitrogen and produces dense sprawling growth. Its earlier flowering makes it easier to terminate ahead of spring planting than standard hairy vetch. Mow or roller-crimp at early bloom for effective no-till kill, then plant into the residue mat. Because it can reseed, terminate before pods harden to limit volunteers in future crops.

plant_note: Direct-sow in early spring or late summer; behaves like white sweet clover but is slightly shorter and prefers drier soils.
directions: Scarify hard-coated seed before sowing to improve germination, and inoculate with Sinorhizobium meliloti for reliable nitrogen fixation in new plantings. Broadcast at 10–12 lb/acre and press lightly into a firm seedbed; yellow sweet clover is more drought-tolerant than most clovers and establishes well on thin, calcareous soils. It overwinters as a rosette and bolts to bloom in spring of the second year; terminate at early bud for maximum N contribution. Allow 3–4 weeks between incorporation and planting to let allelopathic coumarin compounds dissipate.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained germination mix; seedlings grow quickly and should be potted on before becoming root-bound. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil as soon as the risk of hard frost has passed; osteospermum thrives in cool weather and slows during peak summer heat. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage continuous bloom and pinch stem tips to promote branching early in the season. In zones 9–11 it may survive as a short-lived perennial; take stem cuttings in late summer to perpetuate favorite selections.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after nights stay reliably above 50°F.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in a warm starting mix and keep evenly moist until germination. Transplant after all frost risk is gone, spacing larger varieties 12–18 in apart. Deadhead large pompom blooms as they fade to redirect energy to new buds. Tall varieties may need staking in exposed, windy sites.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow dust-fine seed on the surface of moist starting mix without covering — light is essential for germination. Ageratum produces fluffy, powder-puff blooms that attract butterflies and beneficial insects. Shear plants back lightly when flowering slows or plants become leggy to promote a fresh flush of bloom. Consistent moisture is important, as dry conditions cause the plants to stop flowering.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Start seeds on the surface of a moist, fine-textured germination mix at 70–75°F; seeds need light to germinate so do not cover. Transplant into full sun after last frost, spacing 12–18 in apart in well-drained soil enriched with compost. Angelonia is heat- and drought-tolerant once established and rarely needs deadheading, as spent florets drop cleanly. Pinch stem tips early to encourage branching and a fuller plant shape.

plant_note: Succession sow every few weeks for continuous bloom.
directions: Sow annual candytuft directly in early spring, making successive sowings every few weeks for a long season of bloom. The flat clusters of white, pink, and purple flowers are fragrant and attract bees and butterflies. Grow in full sun and average well-drained soil, thinning seedlings to prevent crowding. Shear plants after flowering to keep them tidy and encourage a second flush.

plant_note: Sow directly; resents transplanting due to taproot.
directions: Direct sow annual mallow after the last frost, as it forms a taproot and resents transplanting. The large satiny hibiscus-like blooms in shades of pink and white appear all summer on bushy plants. Provide full sun and moderately fertile, well-drained soil, spacing generously for good air circulation. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and deadhead to keep the display fresh.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; transplant is also possible starting 6–8 weeks early indoors.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep and cover lightly, as darkness can aid germination. Annual phlox prefers cool growing conditions and puts on its best show in spring and early summer. Deadhead spent clusters regularly to prevent seed set and keep flowers coming. In hot climates, plants may slow in midsummer heat; cut back and they will often rebloom as temperatures cool in fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has completely passed.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix, barely pressing in, as light aids germination. Grow on under bright light to prevent tall, weak seedlings before transplanting. Remove spent spikes at their base to encourage a continuous succession of new flower stems. Annual salvia blooms from early summer until the first hard frost.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix; refrigerate seeds for 5–7 days before sowing to improve germination rates. Transplant after the last frost date into well-drained soil, as verbena is very susceptible to root rot in wet conditions. Shear plants back by one-third in midsummer if they become leggy to stimulate a fresh flush of bloom. Verbena thrives in heat and is drought tolerant once established.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant only when nights are consistently above 55°F.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm, moist starting mix and maintain soil temperature above 75°F for best germination. Annual vinca is self-cleaning and requires no deadheading, shedding spent blooms on its own. It excels in summer heat and humidity and is highly drought tolerant. Avoid planting in cold or wet soil, as chilling causes the foliage to yellow and growth to stall.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in spring as soon as soil is workable, or in fall for early establishment.
directions: Plant bulbs scales-down at a depth of 3 times the bulb's height, typically 6–8 in deep, in well-drained, humus-rich soil. Lilies prefer cool roots but sunny tops; mulch the soil surface and underplant with low perennials to shade the root zone. Remove only spent flowers, never the stem and leaves, so the plant can photosynthesize and recharge the bulb. Red lily beetle is a serious pest in many regions; inspect plants regularly and remove beetles and larvae by hand.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in moist, organically rich soil with adequate moisture.
directions: Plant crowns so the top of the rhizome sits 1–2 in below the soil surface in consistently moist, humus-rich soil — astilbe does not tolerate drought and will brown and decline quickly without adequate moisture. The feathery plumes in shades from white through pink to deep red are spectacular in shade and semi-shade gardens from late spring through summer. Leave the russet dried plumes standing through fall and winter for ornamental interest. Divide every 3–4 years in spring when flowering declines or the crown heaves above the soil surface.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in mild-winter regions.
directions: Sow this California native directly where it is to grow, as it dislikes root disturbance. Press seed lightly into cool, moist soil and expect germination within a few weeks. The low mounding plants bear sky-blue flowers with white centers and thrive in the cool weather of spring. Provide light afternoon shade in hot climates and keep soil consistently moist to prolong bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; seeds germinate in cool soil and young plants are frost hardy. For fall bloom, sow again in late summer.
directions: Direct sow seed 1/4 in deep in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked. Bachelor buttons resent transplanting due to a taproot and grow best when started in place. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong blooming, or allow a few to set seed for natural reseeding. Plants decline in summer heat; a second sowing in late summer yields a flush of fall bloom in cool climates.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow outdoors in early spring; blooms appear the second year from seed.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly, just barely covering them, since light aids germination; thin or transplant to 12–18 in spacing. Balloon flowers are late to emerge in spring — mark their location to avoid accidentally digging the fleshy taproot. Deadhead spent flowers to extend the blooming period through summer, and avoid dividing since the taproot resents disturbance. Plants are long-lived and improve in beauty over many years with minimal care.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes in late summer to early fall so roots establish before frost; divisions planted after midsummer bloom by the following spring.
directions: Set rhizomes horizontally with the top surface exposed to the sun — never bury them, as iris borers and rot thrive under covered rhizomes. Plant in late summer after bloom, with the fan of leaves pointing in the direction you want growth to spread. Divide every 3–4 years when clumps become overcrowded, discarding the woody center and replanting healthy outer fans. Good drainage is essential; soggy soil causes rhizome rot.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with good air circulation.
directions: Plant in moist but well-drained soil rich in organic matter; good air circulation around plants is essential to minimize powdery mildew, their primary disease issue. The shaggy, two-lipped flowers are magnets for hummingbirds, bees, and butterflies from midsummer into fall. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering and cut plants back by one-third after the first flush to encourage a second wave. Divide every 2–3 years in spring because the spreading rhizomes exhaust the soil in the clump's center.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date; seeds need cool temperatures to germinate and benefit from cold stratification.
directions: Refrigerate seeds for one week before sowing; then direct sow on the soil surface in early spring since seeds need light and cool temperatures to germinate. Thin to 9–12 in apart — crowded plants are prone to powdery mildew. The tall, architectural stems bear small white flowers nestled inside large, shell-shaped green calyces that remain ornamental for months and dry beautifully. Cut stems for fresh or dried arrangements before calyces begin to brown.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; does not transplant well — sow in situ.
directions: Sow seeds directly where they are to grow, pressing them onto moist soil — they need light to germinate; thin to 9–12 in apart. Ammi majus produces flat, lacy white umbels similar to Queen Anne's Lace but with cleaner, more refined flowers that are highly valued by florists. The feathery foliage and long stems make it an ideal filler in bouquets with a vase life of 7–10 days. Successive sowings 3–4 weeks apart extend the harvest period; plants bolt in summer heat so succession-sow in spring only.

plant_note: Direct sow on or near last frost date; seeds need light to germinate so press onto soil surface.
directions: Press seeds onto the soil surface — they need light to germinate and should not be covered. Plants often bloom their first year from an early start, flowering freely into fall. Deadhead to prolong blooming or allow seeds to self-sow for a naturalized planting. Rudbeckia fulgida varieties are longer-lived perennials; divide every 3 years to refresh the planting.

plant_note: Start indoors 6-8 weeks early for earlier bloom.
directions: Start black-eyed Susan vine indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost for the longest season of bloom. The twining stems carry cheerful orange, yellow, or white flowers each with a dark central eye. Provide a trellis, fence, or container support in full sun to partial shade with rich, evenly moist soil. Feed lightly and keep well watered, and the vine will flower nonstop until frost.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; do not cover seeds, as light aids germination.
directions: Press seeds onto the surface of moist growing medium and provide warmth (70–75°F) and bright light without covering; transplant after frost into sharply drained, even poor soil since gaillardia thrives on neglect. Deadhead spent blooms continuously to keep the bold red and yellow flowers coming from early summer through frost. Avoid clay soils and overwatering — wet conditions in winter are the primary cause of plant loss. Divide or replace plants every 2–3 years, as they tend to be short-lived perennials.

plant_note: Plant bare-root crowns in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked; dormant division in fall is also successful.
directions: Plant crowns 1–2 in deep in cool, humus-rich, consistently moist but well-drained soil in partial shade. Bleeding heart goes dormant by midsummer in warm climates — interplant with hostas, ferns, or other shade-lovers to fill the gap left when the foliage yellows and dies back. Divide clumps every 4–5 years in early spring just as new growth emerges; the fleshy roots are brittle, so handle with care. All parts of the plant are mildly toxic if ingested.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; division every 2–3 years maintains the intense blue color.
directions: Plant in full sun and very well-drained, lean to average soil; blue fescue is a cool-season grass that goes semi-dormant in the heat of summer and performs best in cool, dry climates. The tight, spiky mounds of intensely silvery-blue foliage are striking as edging or mass plantings; tan flower spikes appear in early summer. Divide every 2–3 years in early spring, discarding the dead center and replanting the vigorous outer portions — this maintains the best blue coloration. Avoid heavy, wet clay soils and humid conditions which promote crown rot.

plant_note: Sow in place; needs darkness to germinate, cover seed.
directions: Sow blue lace flower directly where it is to grow, covering seed completely since it needs darkness to germinate. The powder-blue umbels resemble a colored Queen Anne's lace and are cherished by florists for airy arrangements. Provide full sun, well-drained soil, and support for the slender stems in exposed sites. Keep evenly moist during establishment and stagger sowings for a longer cutting season.

plant_note: Direct sow in very early spring or fall; seeds germinate in cool soil and seedlings tolerate frost.
directions: Surface sow by scattering seed on bare ground and pressing lightly without covering, as light aids germination. Breadseed poppies resent root disturbance and must be sown in place. Thin to 6–12 in when seedlings are 2 in tall. The ornamental seedpods are striking after petals drop and are valuable dried; harvest when the cap at the top turns from green to tan.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier for bloom the first season.
directions: Press seeds onto moist soil surface or cover very lightly; they need light to germinate and germinate readily in 7–14 days. Unlike Rudbeckia hirta, R. triloba is a true biennial-to-short-lived-perennial that produces abundant small golden-yellow flowers with a dark center from midsummer through hard frost. Plant in full sun to partial shade in average garden soil; plants self-sow prolifically and naturalize easily in meadow and prairie plantings. Deadhead some stems to extend the season and let others set seed to perpetuate the colony.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in the refrigerator, then start indoors; or direct sow in fall for natural stratification and spring emergence.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds by mixing with moist sand in a sealed bag and refrigerating for 30 days before sowing at soil surface — seeds need light and should not be covered. Butterfly weed has a deep taproot that makes it drought tolerant once established but very sensitive to transplanting; direct sowing or using deep individual cells causes the least disturbance. First-year plants rarely bloom, investing energy into root development; blooms appear reliably from the second year onward in vivid orange clusters that are among the most attractive nectar sources for monarchs and other butterflies. Leave seedheads through winter to self-sow and feed birds.

plant_note: Plant tubers after all frost danger passes and soil is reliably above 70°F; cold soil causes rot.
directions: Set tubers knobby-side up, 2 in deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil; for a head start, pot tubers indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost date in a warm (75–80°F) location. Caladiums are prized for spectacular heart-shaped foliage in combinations of red, pink, white, and green rather than for their flowers. They excel in shady spots where little else provides vibrant color and thrive in humid conditions. In fall, before temperatures drop below 50°F, dig tubers, allow to cure for 2 weeks, and store in dry vermiculite at 60–65°F.

plant_note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost in cool soil; young plants tolerate frost and prefer cool weather.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in cool, well-drained soil in early spring. Calendula prefers cool weather and will slow or stop blooming when summer heat arrives, often rebounding in fall. Deadhead consistently to prevent seed set and extend the blooming period. The ray petals are edible and can be used as a garnish or in salads.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seeds need cool soil to germinate and tolerate light frost.
directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface and rake lightly to cover 1/16–1/8 in deep. California poppies hate transplanting due to a delicate taproot, so sow where plants are to grow. They thrive in poor, dry soils and need little or no supplemental water once established. Deadhead to extend bloom, or let plants self-sow freely for a naturalized planting.

plant_note: Surface sow; compact form good for edging.
directions: Surface sow dwarf calliopsis where it is to grow, pressing seed into contact with the soil since light aids germination. This compact selection forms neat mounds smothered in bicolor yellow-and-red daisies loved by pollinators. It tolerates poor, dry soil and full sun and is ideal for edging, containers, and the front of borders. Deadhead or shear lightly to encourage continued bloom into fall.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–6 in deep in moist, fertile soil; camassia tolerates seasonally wet conditions better than most bulbs.
directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 in deep in organically rich, moisture-retentive soil; unlike many spring bulbs, camassia tolerates and even prefers seasonally moist or wet ground. Star-shaped flowers in blue, violet, or creamy white appear on tall spikes in late spring, bridging the gap between early bulbs and summer perennials. Allow foliage to die back naturally after blooming; bulbs naturalize readily in meadow or prairie-style plantings. Deer and rodents avoid camassia bulbs.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; climbs by twining petioles.
directions: Direct sow canary creeper after the last frost or start indoors a few weeks early in short-season areas. This relative of nasturtium climbs by twining leaf stalks and bears fringed, bright yellow flowers resembling tiny canaries. Provide a trellis or netting in full sun to partial shade with moist, moderately fertile soil. Keep well watered in summer heat and it will bloom until frost.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes after the last frost when soil is warm; or start indoors in pots 4–6 weeks early.
directions: Plant rhizomes 2–4 in deep with the growing eyes pointing up after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed; starting in pots indoors 4–6 weeks early extends the season in short-summer regions. Cannas are heavy feeders — apply a balanced slow-release fertilizer at planting and a monthly liquid feed during the growing season. Deadhead by removing the entire spent flower spike back to a side shoot to keep the display going. In zones below 8, dig rhizomes after frost, allow to dry for a day, then pack in barely moist peat or newspaper at 50–55°F for winter storage.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; surface-sow without covering as seeds need light to germinate.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist mix and provide light at 65–70°F; seedlings emerge in 2–3 weeks and grow slowly their first season. Plant in consistently moist to wet soil — rain gardens, pond edges, and low-lying areas suit this plant perfectly. Brilliant red spikes appear in midsummer to early fall and are hummingbird magnets. Individual plants are short-lived (2–3 years), so allow self-sowing or divide offsets each spring to maintain the colony.

plant_note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; plants establish quickly and bloom the first season.
directions: Plant in lean, well-drained soil — catmint thrives in conditions that would challenge many perennials and blooms heavily from spring into early summer. Shear the entire plant back by one-half after the first flowering flush to refresh the mounding habit and trigger a second wave of lavender-blue flowers in late summer. The silvery-green aromatic foliage is deer-resistant and provides a reliable edging throughout the season. Divide in spring every 3–4 years or propagate from cuttings taken in late spring.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant only after soil is warm, as cold temperatures cause stunted plants.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix and lightly press in — seed needs light and warmth to germinate. Do not rush transplanting into cold soil, as chilling causes plants to stall and go prematurely to seed. Deadhead spent plumes on crested and feathered types to extend the display. Celosia makes an excellent dried flower; harvest spikes just as they reach full color.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall promptly; they dry out quickly.
directions: Plant checkered fritillary bulbs in fall as soon as possible, since the delicate bulbs dry out quickly, setting them about four inches deep. The nodding bell flowers display a distinctive checkerboard pattern in purple or white in mid-spring. It prefers moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade and naturalizes well in damp meadows and woodland edges. Handle the fragile bulbs gently at planting and let foliage die back to ensure return.

plant_note: Start indoors 8 weeks early; surface sow for light.
directions: Surface sow this unique deep-red rudbeckia indoors eight weeks before the last frost, as light aids germination. Cherry Brandy produces rich burgundy daisies with dark centers, prized as long-lasting cut flowers. Transplant to full sun and average well-drained soil after frost danger passes. Deadhead regularly to keep new blooms coming, and treat it as an annual though it may overwinter in mild zones.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix at 65–70°F. Rotate planting locations each year, as China asters are prone to Fusarium wilt when grown in the same soil repeatedly. Avoid overhead watering and ensure good air circulation to reduce disease pressure. Harvest cut flowers when petals are just beginning to open fully for maximum vase life.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant into the garden 2–4 weeks before last frost, as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; seedlings grow quickly and can be transplanted while still small. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral-to-slightly-alkaline soil; heavy clay and overwet conditions lead to crown rot. Deadhead spent flowers continuously to prevent early seed set and extend the blooming season well into summer. Plants often decline in intense summer heat but may rebloom in fall if sheared back and kept watered.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; can also sow in fall in mild winter climates.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in early spring directly where they are to grow; cover seeds lightly as they germinate better in darkness. The clusters of tiny, intensely blue flowers resemble true forget-me-nots but bloom for a much longer period — from late spring well into summer. Shear plants back by one-third after the first main flush to encourage a second wave of bloom. Plants self-sow freely; allow some seeds to drop for naturalised succession planting in subsequent years.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost while soil is still cool; succession sow every 3 weeks for extended bloom.
directions: Direct sow on the soil surface or barely cover; seeds germinate best at cool soil temperatures and seedlings dislike transplanting. Thin ruthlessly to 9–12 in because overcrowded plants bloom poorly and are prone to powdery mildew in humid conditions. Clarkia thrives on neglect in poor, well-drained soil — fertilizing too heavily causes lush foliage at the expense of flowers. Plants naturally set seed and die when summer heat arrives; in mild-winter climates, sow in fall for late-winter to spring bloom.

plant_note: Direct sow at or 2 weeks before last frost; cold stratification of seed for 7 days in the refrigerator improves germination.
directions: Press seed lightly onto the soil surface with barely 1/8 in of cover — light aids germination. Cleome self-sows prolifically and can become weedy; deadhead to limit reseeding if preferred. The tall, airy flower heads open from the base upward over several weeks, providing an extended season of bloom. Handle plants with care as stems bear small spines and the foliage has a musky scent.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify seeds indoors for 3–4 weeks before spring sowing.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly on the soil surface and provide 3–4 weeks of cold stratification (fall sowing outdoors achieves this naturally) to break dormancy. Columbines bloom most freely in their second and third years and are prolific self-seeders that will hybridize freely, creating a constantly shifting mix of colors. Leafminer damage creates white tunnels in the foliage but rarely threatens plant health; remove affected leaves and dispose of them. Plants are short-lived perennials (3–5 years) but the colony self-perpetuates readily through self-seeding.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface sow as seeds need light to germinate — do not cover.
directions: Press seeds onto the surface of a moist germination medium — do not cover, as light is required for germination; transplant to a humus-rich, well-drained site after last frost. Grown primarily for their spectacular mound-forming foliage in shades of burgundy, copper, lime, silver, and purple, heucheras also produce airy wands of tiny flowers beloved by hummingbirds. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to rejuvenate the woody crowns; reset divisions with the crown at soil level. Most modern hybrids tolerate more sun with adequate moisture but prefer afternoon shade in hot climates.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date; seeds tolerate light frost and germinate best in cool soil.
directions: Sow seed on the soil surface and tamp lightly; good drainage is essential since coreopsis rots in wet soil over winter. Deadhead regularly to keep the bright yellow flowers coming all summer. Shear plants back by half after the first flush to encourage compact regrowth and a second bloom. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring to prevent the center from dying out.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; cosmos thrives in poor soil and needs no fertilizing.
directions: Press seed lightly onto the soil surface and cover with 1/4 in of fine soil. Cosmos germinates best in poor, well-drained soil — avoid fertilizing, which promotes foliage over flowers. Pinch young plants at 12 in to encourage branching. Deadhead regularly or allow seed to set for self-sowing the following year.

plant_note: Plant corms in spring around last frost; in zones 6–7 apply a thick mulch in fall to protect overwintering corms.
directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in groups of 6 or more for the best visual impact; crocosmia forms clumps through underground stolons. Arching sprays of scarlet, orange, or yellow tubular flowers appear from midsummer into fall and are excellent for cutting. Where hardy, divide congested clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to refresh bloom; in zone 5 and colder, lift and store over winter like gladiolus. 'Lucifer' is among the hardiest cultivars, tolerating zone 5 with reliable mulching.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall, 6–10 weeks before the ground freezes, for late-winter to early-spring bloom.
directions: Plant corms pointed side up at a depth of 3–4 in in well-drained soil; they naturalize quickly and look best planted in large, informal drifts. Squirrels frequently dig freshly planted corms, so covering the bed with wire mesh until the ground freezes can protect plantings. Foliage is narrow and grassy; allow it to die back completely before removing. Species crocus tend to be more reliably perennial than large-flowered Dutch types.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination rates.
directions: Sow seeds indoors after cold stratification or plant divisions in spring in moist to moderately moist, organically rich soil; culver's root is an imposing native prairie plant with whorled, lance-shaped leaves and tall candelabra-like spikes of white to pale lavender flowers in mid- to late summer. The architectural form makes it a striking back-of-border plant, and the flowers attract a wide variety of native bees and butterflies. Rarely needs staking when grown in adequate light; divide every 4–5 years in spring to maintain vigor. Excellent companion for joe-pye weed, prairie blazing star, and native grasses.

plant_note: Start indoors 8 weeks early; sow seed on edge.
directions: Start cup and saucer vine indoors eight weeks before the last frost, sowing the flat seeds on edge to prevent rot. This vigorous climber uses tendrils to scale trellises and can reach twenty feet in a single season. The large bell-shaped flowers open greenish and mature to deep purple, resembling a cup on a leafy saucer. Transplant after frost into full sun and rich, moist soil, providing sturdy support.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after last frost into a dry, sunny site.
directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to a sunny, very well-drained site — catananche is native to dry Mediterranean hillsides and rots in moist soil. The papery, cornflower-blue blooms on wiry stems are excellent cut or dried flowers that hold their color well. Shear plants by half after the first flush in early summer to encourage repeat flowering into fall. Best treated as a short-lived perennial; replace plants every 2–3 years or allow to self-sow for a continuing colony.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before the ground freezes, for spring bloom.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall at a depth of 3 times the bulb's height (typically 6–8 in) in well-drained soil with the pointed end up. Daffodils naturalize readily and multiply over time; deer and rodents avoid them because the bulbs are mildly toxic. Allow foliage to yellow and die back naturally — at least 6 weeks — so the bulb can store energy for next year's bloom. Divide congested clumps every 5–7 years when flowering diminishes.

plant_note: Plant tubers after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 60°F.
directions: Plant tubers horizontally 4–6 in deep with the eye pointing up after the last frost when soil reaches 60°F; set a stake at planting time to avoid spearing the tuber later. Pinch the growing tip when plants are 12 in tall to encourage branching and more flowers. In zones below 8, dig tubers after the first killing frost, cure them for a week, then store in slightly damp peat at 40–50°F through winter. Disbudding side buds around the main bud produces exhibition-quality blooms.

plant_note: Plant bare-root divisions in spring or fall; set crowns just 1 in below soil level.
directions: Plant fans with the crown about 1 in deep, spreading roots outward over a small mound of soil in the hole. Water thoroughly after planting and keep consistently moist the first season. Divide overcrowded clumps every 4–5 years in early spring or late summer when bloom quality declines. Removing spent scapes after all buds have opened keeps the planting tidy.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in mild climates (zones 7–10) or in early spring after danger of hard frost has passed in colder zones.
directions: Soak the small, irregular corms in water for 4–6 hours before planting; set them about 2 in deep with the pointed side down (or on their side if unsure). Anemones prefer cool, well-drained soil and perform best where winters are mild and springs are long and cool. Poppy-like blooms in vivid red, blue, purple, white, and bicolors appear over a 6–8 week period in late winter to spring. In cold climates treat as an annual, planting corms in spring for early summer color.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; fresh seed germinates best and benefits from 1–2 weeks of cold stratification.
directions: Use fresh seed — viability drops quickly in storage — and cold-stratify for 1–2 weeks before sowing at 60–70°F for best germination. Transplant after the last frost into rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil and stake early, as the tall spikes are highly susceptible to wind damage. Cut flower spikes back to basal foliage after the first bloom to encourage a second, smaller flush in late summer. All parts of the plant are toxic; all plant contact should be followed by hand washing.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost; lift and store where not hardy.
directions: Plant dinnerplate dahlia tubers after the last frost, laying them horizontally about six inches deep with the eye facing up. These giant blooms can reach ten to twelve inches across and require staking at planting to support the tall stems. Grow in full sun and rich, well-drained soil, watering deeply once shoots emerge and pinching to promote branching. In cold climates lift and store the tubers in a frost-free place over winter.

plant_note: Seeds need darkness to germinate; cover completely.
directions: Cover the seed completely because Drummond phlox requires darkness to germinate. Direct sow in early spring in mild climates or start indoors six weeks before the last frost. The clustered flowers come in a wide range of colors and make excellent low bedding and cut arrangements. Deadhead regularly and shear back leggy plants in midsummer to encourage a fresh flush of bloom.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; naturalizes and self-sows.
directions: Plant drumstick allium bulbs in fall about four inches deep in well-drained soil and full sun. The egg-shaped flower heads emerge green and mature to deep burgundy on wiry stems in early summer, weaving beautifully through borders. Bees and butterflies love the blooms, and the seed heads dry well for arrangements. Keep the site on the dry side in summer and the bulbs will naturalize and self-sow over time.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost risk passes.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist germination mix and provide light at 65–75°F; germination is slow and uneven over 10–14 days. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; excellent salt and drought tolerance makes this ideal for coastal and hot dry sites. The silvery white, felted foliage is the ornamental feature — pinch off yellow flower buds when they appear to keep plants neat and maintain vigorous leaf growth. In zones 8–11 dusty miller overwinters as a short-lived perennial.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in drifts for best effect.
directions: Plant Dutch crocus corms in fall three to four inches deep in well-drained soil, setting them in generous drifts. The large goblet-shaped flowers in purple, white, and yellow open in early spring for bees emerging from winter. Grow in full sun to light shade and avoid summer irrigation while corms are dormant. Let the grassy foliage ripen fully, and the corms will multiply into showy colonies over time.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–6 in deep; in zones 5–6 apply a winter mulch for protection.
directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Dutch iris bloom in late spring to early summer and are superb cut flowers with a vase life of 7–10 days. The large falls and standards come in shades of blue, purple, yellow, white, and bicolor. After bloom, allow foliage to die back naturally; in warm, humid climates the bulbs may rot in summer — lift and store dry in a cool place if this occurs. Replant in early fall and incorporate grit into heavy clay soils to improve drainage.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; needs a dry summer baking.
directions: Plant dwarf reticulated iris bulbs in fall about four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun. The jewel-toned blue and purple flowers with contrasting markings bloom in late winter before most other bulbs. It needs a warm, dry summer dormancy to bloom well in subsequent years, so avoid irrigated beds. Plant it at the front of borders or in rock gardens where its early flowers can be admired up close.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; lobelia has extremely tiny seeds and a slow early growth rate.
directions: Surface-sow the dust-like seeds on moist fine-textured mix; do not cover, as they need light to germinate, and use a spray mist to avoid washing them away. Grow seedlings on at cool temperatures (60–65°F) once germinated; pot up in small clusters of 3–5 seedlings rather than separating individuals. Lobelia blooms best in cool weather and partial shade in hot climates, often slowing or stopping in midsummer heat — shear back by half and water regularly for rebloom as fall approaches. The vivid blue, trailing types are classic basket and container plants.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or scarify and cold-stratify seeds before spring planting.
directions: Scarify seeds and cold-stratify for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing, or direct sow in fall to let natural winter conditions break dormancy. Baptisia is a slow-starting plant — expect minimal bloom for the first 2–3 years, but once established, the clump requires virtually no care and lives for decades. The indigo-blue lupine-like flower spikes appear in late spring and are followed by attractive, inflated seedpods that rattle in the wind. Do not attempt to transplant established plants, as the deep taproot does not tolerate disturbance.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier for bloom the first season.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in the garden or start indoors; unlike true perennial rudbeckia, heliopsis often blooms robustly in its first season from an early start. Plant in full sun in average, well-drained soil; plants tolerate heat and short dry periods better than helenium. Deadhead regularly throughout summer to keep the golden-yellow, semi-double or double flowers coming continuously from July through September. Divide clumps every 3 years in spring or fall to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.

plant_note: Direct sow in place; tolerates poor dry soil well.
directions: Direct sow this western native in early spring, barely covering the small seed. Farewell to spring produces spikes of ruffled pink to lavender flowers on slender upright stems that make good cut flowers. It prefers lean, dry soil and full sun, blooming as spring transitions into summer. Avoid overwatering and fertilizing, which reduce flowering, and let plants self-sow for future displays.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring; 'Karl Foerster' is sterile and must be propagated by division.
directions: Plant in full sun to light shade in average, moisture-retentive soil; 'Karl Foerster' feather reed grass is one of the most reliable and widely adapted ornamental grasses, tolerating clay soil, humidity, and cold winters that defeat many others. Strongly upright, narrow clumps emerge early in spring with feathery bronze-pink plumes appearing in early summer that fade to wheat-gold and persist through winter. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter or early spring before new growth begins. Divide every 4–5 years in spring when the center begins to die out.

plant_note: Plant container stock after last frost; firebush is a Florida and Gulf Coast native that quickly reaches shrub size and blooms nonstop in summer heat.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil in full sun; firebush tolerates heat, drought, and poor soils once established and reblooms continuously from late spring through frost. The tubular orange-red flowers are magnets for hummingbirds and butterflies, while the small black fruits that follow attract songbirds — making it one of the most wildlife-productive native shrubs for Gulf Coast gardens. In zone 8–9 the top freezes back in winter, but the plant regrows vigorously from the base each spring; cut dead stems to the ground once new growth begins. Native firebush (var. patens) is superior to the commonly sold 'Compacta' cultivar for wildlife value and cold hardiness.

plant_note: Nick seed and direct sow after frost.
directions: Nick the seed coat and direct sow firecracker vine after the last frost in warm soil. This twining morning glory relative bears small scarlet trumpet flowers with yellow throats that draw hummingbirds. Provide a fence or trellis in full sun and average soil, and it will climb quickly through summer. Deadheading is unnecessary, but remove faded flowers if you wish to limit self-seeding.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface sow tiny seed on moist starting mix under bright light — do not cover, as seed is extremely small and needs light to germinate. Nicotiana tolerates partial shade better than most annuals and performs well in dappled light. Fragrant varieties release scent primarily in the evening, making them ideal planted near a patio or window. Deadhead spent flower clusters to encourage continuous blooming.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost with a cold stratification period; or plant divisions in spring or fall.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained shade; foamflower spreads by stolons to form a weed-suppressing ground cover with attractive, deeply lobed, often patterned foliage. Frothy spikes of tiny white to pink flowers appear in mid-spring above the foliage and last for 4–6 weeks. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to prevent overcrowding and maintain vigor. Many named cultivars with exceptional leaf markings are available and are best propagated by division to maintain cultivar characteristics.

plant_note: Sow in late summer to early fall so plants overwinter as rosettes and bloom in spring alongside tulips and daffodils.
directions: Scatter seeds on the soil surface or barely cover them; they germinate best in cool conditions and seedlings overwinter as low rosettes in mild climates. Forget-me-nots self-sow prolifically once established — let plants go to seed, then pull them after flowering to control spread. The sky-blue flowers are traditionally combined with spring bulbs in beds and create a charming filler under shrubs in partial shade. Remove spent plants when temperatures rise, as they become straggly and die back in summer heat.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; can also start indoors 4–6 weeks early.
directions: Sow seed 1/4–1/2 in deep in a warm, sunny location after the last frost. Four o'clocks form a tuberous root and tolerate drought well once established. Flowers open in late afternoon and remain open through the following morning, releasing a sweet fragrance that attracts sphinx moths. Plants self-sow prolifically and the fleshy roots can be dug and stored in frost-prone areas.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; seeds need light and should not be covered.
directions: Press seeds onto the surface of moist potting mix under bright light — do not cover, as light aids germination. Foxgloves are biennial or short-lived perennials; most produce a rosette in year 1 and the spectacular tall spikes in year 2 before setting seed and dying. Cutting the main spike after bloom often stimulates smaller side spikes for additional flowers, and self-sowing keeps the planting going indefinitely. All parts of the plant are highly toxic; use caution and wash hands thoroughly after handling.

plant_note: Plant the spider-like fleshy roots in fall, spreading them horizontally 4 in deep in a well-drained, sunny site.
directions: Spread the octopus-like fleshy roots horizontally over a mound of gritty soil, crown just below the surface, with perfect drainage — wet winter soil rots the crowns quickly. The strap-like foliage emerges in early spring and can be killed by a late frost; a light cover of straw protects emerging growth in zones 5–6. Spectacular bottle-brush spikes up to 8 ft tall erupt in late spring to early summer in shades of white, yellow, orange, and pink. Leaves die back after bloom, so interplant with later-emerging perennials to fill the gap.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall in frost-free areas (zones 9–10) or start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost in cold climates.
directions: Plant corms 2 in deep and 2–3 in apart in gritty, well-drained soil; freesias resent wet conditions especially after bloom. The fragrant, funnel-shaped flowers on arching stems need staking or a grow-through support as stems are slender. In frost-free gardens the corms are perennial and naturalize freely; in colder climates lift after foliage dies, dry thoroughly, and store in a cool dry place. Corms multiply quickly — divide every 2–3 years to maintain bloom quality.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix. Pinch out the first flower bud to encourage bushier branching and heavier bloom set. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to keep plants producing through summer. Blooms appear approximately 6–8 weeks after transplant and continue until frost.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring, 2–6 weeks after the last frost, for fall bloom.
directions: Plant in spring into fertile, well-drained soil; pinch growing tips every 2–3 weeks from planting until midsummer (around July 4) to develop compact, well-branched plants with dozens of buds. Mums are short-day plants that begin flower bud formation as nights lengthen; stop pinching by midsummer to avoid disrupting this process. Mulch heavily after the first hard frost to improve winter hardiness in colder zones. Divide annually in spring to rejuvenate the planting and maintain plant vigor.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as new growth emerges or in early fall for establishment before frost.
directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil with good air circulation to reduce powdery mildew, which is the plant's main problem. Thin stems to 5–7 per clump in spring to improve airflow and produce larger flower trusses on the remaining stems. Deadhead the central flower cluster as it fades to encourage lateral buds to open and extend the bloom season. Divide every 3 years in spring, replanting only the outermost, most vigorous sections.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in loose, well-drained germination mix; plants develop a deep taproot early so pot on before roots become crowded. Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained soil — gaura thrives on neglect and declines in fertile, wet conditions. The wand-like stems bearing butterfly-shaped white or pink flowers sway gracefully all season without deadheading. Cut plants back to 6 in after the first heavy bloom flush to encourage a tidy second flush through autumn.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; they germinate readily at 65–75°F and grow quickly into compact transplants. Plant in sharply drained, even poor soil in the hottest, sunniest location available — gazania excels in sandy or gravelly beds where other plants fail. Flowers close at night and on overcast days, but modern hybrid varieties stay open longer than older types. Drought tolerance is excellent once established; overwatering and heavy clay soils are the most common causes of failure.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds vertically, pointed end down, barely covered, as they are sensitive to orientation; maintain 70–75°F for germination. Plant so the crown sits slightly above soil level to prevent crown rot, which is the most common cause of plant failure. Water at the base rather than overhead; gerberas prefer a dry-wet-dry cycle rather than constantly moist soil. Deadhead spent flowers by cutting the stem to the base to encourage continuous bloom from spring through fall in mild climates.

plant_note: Plant large bulbs in fall at a depth of 3–4 times the bulb diameter (typically 6–8 in) in well-drained soil.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall, pointed side up, at 3–4 times their diameter in depth; good drainage is essential as bulbs rot in waterlogged soil. The bold, softball-sized purple globes atop tall stems create dramatic vertical accents in late spring to early summer borders. Foliage, which smells of onion when crushed, often begins to yellow before flowers are fully open — conceal the base with later-rising perennials. Allow seedheads to remain for architectural interest; deadhead if you wish to prevent excessive self-seeding.

plant_note: Plant corms after the last frost; make successive plantings every 2 weeks through early summer for extended bloom.
directions: Plant corms 4–6 in deep in well-drained soil with the pointed end up after the last frost; succession plant every 2 weeks through midsummer for continuous cut flowers. Tall varieties need staking; a single bamboo cane tied loosely to the stem works well. In zones below 7, dig corms after frost blackens the foliage, dry for 2–3 weeks, remove offsets (cormels), and store at 35–45°F in mesh bags through winter. Harvest cut spikes when the lowest 2–3 buds show color for longest vase life.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; plants typically bloom in their second year from seed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep; cold stratification is not required but may speed germination. Established globe thistle is extremely drought-tolerant and thrives in poor, sandy, or chalky well-drained soil — fertile, moist soil causes lax growth and flopping. The spiky steel-blue globe flowers are excellent for fresh cutting and dry perfectly for everlasting arrangements if cut just before fully open. Divide clumps in spring every 4–5 years using a sharp spade; they are slow to re-establish so divisions should be generous.

plant_note: Plant large bulbs in fall for dramatic late-spring globes.
directions: Plant Globemaster allium bulbs in fall six to eight inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Each stout stem produces a massive violet globe up to ten inches across that lasts for weeks and draws countless bees. Position the plants among perennials that hide the fading foliage, which yellows before the flowers finish. The dried seed heads make striking architectural accents in the garden or in arrangements.

plant_note: Plant tubers horizontally 4 in deep after last frost; in zones 8+ can be planted in early spring once soil is 65°F.
directions: Plant the fragile, finger-shaped tubers horizontally 4 in deep, handling carefully to avoid snapping them; provide a trellis or support as the vine climbs by leaf-tip tendrils. The striking reflexed flowers in flame-red and yellow with wavy-edged petals are produced freely from midsummer into fall. All parts of the plant are highly toxic if ingested — site away from children and pets. In zones below 8, dig tubers after foliage dies, dry them, and store in dry peat moss at 50–60°F over winter.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs 3 in deep in fall; naturalize in lawns, rock gardens, or under deciduous trees.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall, 3 in deep and 2–3 in apart, in any well-drained soil; glory-of-the-snow blooms very early, often pushing through snow in late winter to early spring. The vivid blue, star-shaped flowers with white centers naturalize freely by both offsets and self-seeding, forming large colonies over time. Plant in large drifts under deciduous trees where they receive full sun while leafless and will not be disturbed during summer dormancy. No special care is needed once established; bulbs rarely need dividing.

plant_note: Sow in place as soon as soil is workable; dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow godetia into lean, well-drained soil in early spring, barely covering the fine seed. It resents rich soil and heavy feeding, which produce foliage at the expense of the satiny cup-shaped blooms. Thin seedlings to 8-12 inches and keep evenly moist until established, then water sparingly. Cool weather intensifies the pink and lavender flower colors, so it performs best in spring and early summer before extreme heat.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; clump-forming cultivars are better-behaved than the straight species.
directions: Plant in full sun in average to poor, well-drained soil; goldenrod thrives in lean conditions and becomes overly lax and floppy in rich, moist soil. The arching plumes of golden-yellow flowers from late summer through fall are essential late-season food for monarch butterflies and dozens of native bee species. Contrary to popular belief, goldenrod does not cause hay fever — its pollen is heavy and insect-carried, not windborne. Choose named compact cultivars like 'Fireworks' or 'Little Lemon' for well-behaved garden varieties; divide every 2–3 years to control spread.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; soak seed overnight to speed germination. Gomphrena thrives in heat and humidity and is highly drought tolerant once established. The clover-like flowers hold their color and shape exceptionally well when dried — harvest stems just as the heads reach full color. Deadheading encourages continuous fresh bloom throughout the summer.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 3–4 in deep; foliage emerges in fall and overwinters, so plant early enough for roots to establish.
directions: Plant small bulbs 3–4 in deep in any well-drained soil; foliage emerges in fall and overwinters as a low ground cover, with tight clusters of cobalt-blue, urn-shaped flowers appearing in early to mid-spring. Muscari naturalizes aggressively by offsets and self-seeding and can spread widely — site where this is desirable or divide clumps every few years in fall to control spread. Excellent planted beneath tulips and daffodils to create complementary color combinations. Deer and rodents generally leave them alone.

plant_note: Soak tubers overnight before planting in fall.
directions: Soak the small knobby tubers overnight, then plant Grecian windflower in fall about two to three inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich soil.
