The daisy-like flowers in blue, pink, or white carpet the ground in early spring beneath deciduous trees and shrubs. It naturalizes freely in sun to partial shade and goes dormant by early summer. Plant in generous drifts and leave the foliage to ripen so the colony can expand year after year.

plant_note: Plant tubers shallowly in late summer under trees.
directions: Plant hardy cyclamen tubers shallowly in late summer or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees. The dainty pink or white reflexed flowers appear in autumn, followed by beautifully marbled ivy-shaped leaves that persist through winter. It thrives in dry shade where little else grows and slowly self-sows to form colonies. Keep the tubers on the dry side during their summer dormancy and mulch lightly with leaf mold.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; tolerates a wide range of soil conditions once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in a location with morning sun and afternoon shade; hardy geraniums are adaptable to poor soils and are generally low-maintenance once established. Shear plants back by one-half after the initial bloom to refresh the foliage and encourage a second flush of flowers. The attractive lobed leaves often turn red or orange in fall, extending the ornamental season. Divide every 3–5 years in spring to reinvigorate the planting.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after nicking the seed coat and soaking 24 hours; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Nick or file seed coats and soak in warm water for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination rates significantly. Plant in full sun in moist, fertile soil — hardy hibiscus is native to wetlands and performs best with consistent moisture but tolerates average garden beds with regular irrigation. This is a late riser in spring; the shoots emerge much later than other perennials, so mark plants to avoid accidental disturbance. The dinner-plate-sized flowers in white, pink, and crimson open each morning and close by evening, with new blooms produced daily from midsummer through frost.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant only after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; germination is slow (14–28 days) and benefits from bottom heat at 70–80°F consistently. Transplant into rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; heat brings out the intensely vanilla-scented, deep purple flower clusters. Pinch tips once or twice before transplanting to produce bushier plants with more flowering heads. In zones 10–11 heliotrope is a shrubby perennial; elsewhere take cuttings in late summer to overwinter prized fragrant selections indoors.

plant_note: Plant in spring or early fall in humus-rich, well-drained shade; transplanted divisions establish more reliably than seed.
directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in well-drained, humus-rich soil in dappled to full shade — morning sun is tolerated, but afternoon sun causes leaf scorch. Hellebores bloom in late winter to early spring, often through snow, and the nodding flowers face downward; tipping the pot or planting on a slope allows a better view of the flower interior. Remove old foliage in late winter before new growth emerges to improve air circulation and reduce leaf spot diseases. Hellebores self-sow freely, and seedlings from hybrid plants will vary; divide named selections in early spring to maintain specific colors.

plant_note: Direct sow in spring around the last frost date or in late summer for bloom the following year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep; hollyhocks are biennial or short-lived perennials that flower the second year from seed and self-sow freely to maintain the planting. Rust fungus is the most common disease — look for orange pustules on leaf undersides and remove affected foliage promptly, avoiding overhead watering. Plant against walls or fences and provide loose staking for the tall, top-heavy flowering spikes. Allow some seed to mature and fall to keep the colony self-renewing.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or in summer for flowers the following spring; behaves as a biennial in most climates.
directions: Sow seeds in spring or early summer; plants develop as a rosette the first year and flower the following spring — a true biennial. Purple or white cross-shaped flowers appear in mid-spring, followed by the flat, papery-silver seed pods that give the plant its common names 'honesty' and 'money plant.' To harvest pods for dried arrangements, cut when the outer husks have dried and carefully peel away both sides to reveal the lustrous translucent membrane. Plants self-sow freely and once established will perpetuate themselves year after year.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall in zones 7+ for overwintering.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in cool soil; germination is rapid in the 55–65°F range and slows dramatically in warm conditions. The blue-green waxy foliage and pendant, tubular flowers in deep violet-purple with showy blue-purple bracts are a magnet for bumblebees. Cerinthe prefers cool weather and will bolt and die in summer heat; in mild climates it self-sows prolifically and may return year after year. Harvest stems when bracts are fully colored for a distinctive and long-lasting cut flower.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as new growth emerges or in fall at least 6 weeks before frost.
directions: Set crowns at soil level in humus-rich, consistently moist soil; avoid planting too deep or crowns may rot. Hostas tolerate deep shade but produce their best foliage color with 2–4 hours of dappled morning light. Divide large clumps every 4–5 years in early spring by slicing through the crown with a sharp spade. Slugs are the primary pest; use iron phosphate bait around vulnerable new growth.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before hard frost, for intensely fragrant spring bloom.
directions: Plant bulbs at a depth of 6–8 in (about 3 times the bulb height) in well-drained soil with the pointed end up; wear gloves as the bulb sap can irritate sensitive skin. Hyacinths produce their most spectacular, dense spikes in year 1; subsequent years yield looser, more relaxed flower clusters that many gardeners find charming. Allow foliage to yellow completely before removing it so the bulb can replenish its energy. In warm climates (zones 8+), lift and chill bulbs for 6 weeks before replanting.

plant_note: Direct sow after soil warms; loves heat.
directions: Direct sow hyacinth bean after the soil has warmed, or start indoors a few weeks early in cool climates. This ornamental vine bears purple stems, spikes of pea-like lavender flowers, and glossy magenta seed pods. Provide a sturdy trellis in full sun and average soil, and it will climb rapidly in summer heat. The pods are ornamental but require thorough cooking, so it is grown chiefly for its striking display.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed into sharply drained soil.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist, gritty mix without covering, as they need light to germinate; avoid overwatering at all stages. Plant in the hottest, driest, sunniest spot in the garden — sandy, rocky, or gravelly soil is ideal, and root rot is the main cause of failure in clay or poorly drained sites. The magenta to purple daisy-like flowers cover the succulent mats nearly all summer and reopen only in bright sunlight. Hardy ice plant spreads steadily to make a weed-suppressing groundcover on slopes and rock gardens.

plant_note: In zones 7–10 sow in fall 4–6 weeks before first frost for winter and spring bloom; in colder zones direct sow in very early spring as soon as soil can be worked.
directions: Surface-sow or barely cover seeds on well-prepared, fine-textured soil; seeds need light and cool temperatures (50–60°F) to germinate — warm soil prevents germination. Plants grow as basal rosettes through winter in mild climates before sending up slender stems topped with large, tissue-paper flowers in spring. Iceland poppies are cool-season plants that stop flowering when summer heat arrives; in cold climates treat them as an early-spring annual with fall-sown or indoor-started transplants set out very early. Harvest cut flowers when buds are just beginning to crack open color and sear stem ends in boiling water to maximize vase life.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed and nights are warm.
directions: Sow tiny seed on the surface of moist starting mix without covering — light is required for germination. Impatiens are one of the most reliable shade-tolerant annuals and will self-clean most spent flowers. Pinch plants back once after transplanting to encourage compact, bushy growth. Water consistently; wilting in heat is common and plants recover overnight, but chronic drought reduces flowering.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring; slow to establish but eventually spreads vigorously.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, moisture-retentive, well-drained soil in partial shade; Japanese anemone is slow to establish in its first 1–2 years but spreads freely by underground runners once settled. Single or semi-double flowers in white, pink, or deep rose appear on tall, branched stems from late summer through fall when few other perennials are in bloom. Can become invasive in rich, moist conditions — site carefully or contain with buried barriers. Divide in early spring every 3–4 years to control spread or increase the planting.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring after frost or immediately after late-summer bloom; keep roots consistently moist after planting.
directions: Plant rhizomes 2 in deep in rich, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), moisture-retentive to wet soil; Japanese iris excel at pond margins and rain gardens. The enormous flat flowers up to 10 in across appear in early to midsummer in shades of purple, blue, white, and lavender, often with intricate veining. Avoid lime or wood ash near these plants — alkaline soil causes yellowing and poor growth. Divide every 3–4 years in late summer or early spring, replanting healthy outer fans and discarding the woody centers.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 4–6 weeks; transplant in spring.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in a moist medium in the refrigerator before sowing indoors; germination is improved but still irregular. Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; this native prairie plant tolerates occasional wet feet and dry spells once established. Cutting stems back by half in June (Chelsea chop) reduces ultimate height and prevents the need for staking without sacrificing the domed, mauve-pink flower clusters beloved by butterflies. Seedheads are ornamentally interesting into winter and provide food for goldfinches.

plant_note: Plant in fall; fragrant and good for warm climates.
directions: Plant jonquilla daffodil bulbs in fall about six inches deep in well-drained soil and full to partial sun. These fragrant narcissi carry several small golden flowers per stem and tolerate warmer climates better than most daffodils. They naturalize freely and are resistant to deer and rodents. Allow the grassy foliage to ripen fully before removal to build strong bulbs for next year.

plant_note: Plant in fall; perennializes well in warm dry climates.
directions: Plant lady tulip bulbs in fall about five inches deep in sharply drained soil and full sun. Unlike hybrid tulips, this dainty species perennializes reliably and naturalizes in warm, dry climates. The slender flowers are white or yellow with a bold red or pink exterior stripe and open flat in sun. Keep the site dry during summer dormancy to encourage the bulbs to return and multiply for years.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; most gardeners use purchased transplants due to slow, uneven germination.
directions: Sow seeds after lightly scarifying or nicking the seed coat; soak overnight to improve germination, which is slow and can take 4–6 weeks at 70–80°F. Transplant in full sun after all frost risk has passed; lantana thrives in heat, drought, and poor soil and blooms most freely under mild stress. The multicolored flower clusters change color as they age, giving plants a jewel-like effect; deadhead clusters as they fade to keep new flowers coming. Perennial in zones 9–11 where it can become invasive; grow as an annual elsewhere.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 4–8 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall in mild climates for early spring bloom. Seeds need cold stratification and prefer cool soil to germinate.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep directly in the garden as early as the ground can be worked — larkspur germinates best in cool soil below 65°F and may fail to sprout if the weather turns warm. Do not start indoors, as plants have taproots that do not transplant well. Thin to 9–12 in to promote sturdy stems. Allow some seed to fall for reliable self-sowing in subsequent years.

plant_note: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in spring as soon as the soil can be worked; they establish quickly and bloom the first season.
directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep with the flat side down in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; rich or wet soil leads to floppy growth. Liatris is unique in opening its flowers from the top of the spike downward, prolonging the bloom period for 3–4 weeks in midsummer. The purple to rose-pink spikes are outstanding cut flowers and highly attractive to butterflies and bees; stems last 10–14 days in a vase. Clumps multiply steadily; divide every 3–4 years in spring to prevent overcrowding and maintain strong bloom.

plant_note: Plant pips in fall or early spring in shade.
directions: Plant lily of the valley pips in fall or early spring, setting the rhizomes just below the soil surface in humus-rich, moist shade. The arching stems of nodding white bells are intensely fragrant and spread by rhizomes to form a dense groundcover. It thrives under trees and in woodland gardens and can become invasive, so site it where spread is welcome. All parts are toxic if eaten, so keep it away from children and pets.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost date; lisianthus requires a very long lead time and is challenging from seed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny, pelleted seeds on sterile, fine-textured mix; do not cover as seeds need light and maintain steady 70–75°F. Seedlings grow extremely slowly for the first 8–10 weeks, so patience is essential — do not overwater at this stage as damping off is common. Transplant carefully after last frost into rich, well-drained, alkaline-leaning soil in full sun. The ruffled, rose-like blooms in white, purple, pink, and bicolor make lisianthus one of the finest cut flowers; harvest stems when 2–3 buds per stem have opened.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in spring, or plant divisions in spring in full sun and lean, well-drained soil.
directions: Direct sow in full sun in lean, well-drained to dry soil; little bluestem is a native prairie grass of exceptional ornamental value that thrives in poor, sandy, or rocky soil where it develops the best fall color. Blue-green summer foliage turns brilliant coppery-orange and burgundy-red in fall, and the silver-white fluffy seedheads persist through winter, capturing hoarfrost beautifully. In rich, moist soil plants become floppy and lose their characteristic upright form. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter before new growth emerges; divide every 3–4 years if needed.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; do not start too early indoors as plants grow quickly and become rootbound.
directions: Sow seed on the soil surface and press lightly — seed needs light to germinate. Direct sowing is preferred as plants grow fast and resent transplanting. Thin seedlings to 18–24 in to allow the dramatic, rope-like crimson tassels to develop fully. The hanging panicles can be cut and hung upside down to dry when they reach full length.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in early spring; also sow in fall in zones 7–10 for spring bloom.
directions: Scatter seeds directly on prepared soil and rake in 1/8 in deep; nigella resents transplanting and germinates best in cool soil. Thin seedlings to 9–12 in to prevent overcrowding — plants are quick to grow and will self-sow freely if allowed to set the inflated seed pods. The blue or white flowers are surrounded by thread-like bracts that give the plant its misty appearance; after bloom the ornamental balloon-like pods are equally attractive and excellent for dried arrangements. Make succession sowings every 3–4 weeks until early summer for continuous bloom.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in a shaded, moist site; division is the most reliable propagation method.
directions: Plant in deep shade to partial shade in humus-rich, consistently moist soil; lungwort is one of the best shade perennials, offering both attractive silver-spotted foliage and early spring flowers. Pink buds open to blue or violet tubular flowers in early spring, often when little else is in bloom, providing nectar for early pollinators. In hot, dry summers the leaves may develop powdery mildew — shear back affected foliage after flowering and new, fresh leaves will emerge. Divide clumps every 3–4 years immediately after flowering to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Direct sow outdoors in early spring or fall; nick or soak seeds overnight to improve germination.
directions: Scarify seeds with sandpaper or soak overnight in water to soften the hard seed coat before sowing 1/4 in deep in cool, slightly acidic, well-drained soil. Lupines fix atmospheric nitrogen via root nodules and thrive in lean soils without heavy fertilization — too much nitrogen promotes foliage over flowers. They prefer cool summers; in hot climates, they often perform as cool-season annuals, blooming in late spring and fading by midsummer. Deadhead promptly after the first flowering spike fades to encourage a second flush and prevent excessive self-seeding.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring when soil has warmed; miscanthus establishes best in warm soil and can be slow in cold springs.
directions: Plant divisions in full sun in any well-drained to moderately moist soil; maiden grass forms large, dramatic clumps with arching, green or variegated foliage and showy, feathery silver plumes in late summer and fall. Cut back hard in late winter or early spring before new growth emerges — use pruners or a chainsaw on large clumps; tie the clump with twine before cutting to simplify cleanup. Divide every 3–5 years in spring when the center of the clump dies out. Some cultivars are sterile; check before planting, as seeding types can naturalize aggressively in some regions.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall at least 6 weeks before the ground freezes; disturb as little as possible once established.
directions: Plant bulbs 6 in deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil; martagon lilies are unusual in tolerating partial shade and actually prefer dappled woodland conditions. Whorled leaves and pendant, turks-cap-shaped flowers in pink, purple, or white with recurved petals appear in early summer. Martagon lilies are slow to establish and may not bloom for 2 years after planting — once settled they are very long-lived and resent being disturbed. Self-seeding is common; seedlings take 4–7 years to bloom.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost with cold stratification; or plant divisions in early spring.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist soil in partial shade; tall meadow rue creates an airy, vertical accent with blue-green, columbine-like foliage and billowing clouds of lavender-purple flowers in midsummer. The delicate flowers are held on strong, self-supporting stems and sway gracefully in a breeze. Seldom needs staking if grown in adequate light; divide every 4–5 years in early spring to keep plants vigorous. Excellent in a woodland border or at the back of a shaded perennial bed.

plant_note: Plant container stock after last frost; Mexican bush sage blooms in fall and is the showiest late-season salvia for zone 8–11 landscapes.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Mexican bush sage is drought tolerant once established and demands good drainage over fertility. It is a short-day plant that blooms from September through frost, producing velvety purple flower spikes loved by hummingbirds and bumblebees; the later into fall it blooms, the more spectacular the display. Cut the entire plant back to 6–8 in after the last hard freeze in late winter to stimulate dense new basal growth. Propagate easily by dividing the clump in spring or rooting stem cuttings in moist perlite.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date in a sunny, dry site; self-seeds profusely — site away from natural areas.
directions: Sow seeds directly in full sun in lean, very well-drained soil — rich or moist conditions produce weak, floppy plants. The hair-fine, bright green leaves form airy, billowing mounds that catch every breeze, creating a constant motion in the landscape; the foliage turns blonde and golden in summer heat. This grass self-seeds very aggressively and is considered invasive in California and some southwestern states — deadhead before seeds ripen if growing in areas with native grasslands. Cut back hard in late winter before new growth begins.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes — one of the best heat-loving annuals for attracting butterflies and hummingbirds.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; transplant carefully as Tithonia dislikes root disturbance — use biodegradable pots if starting indoors. Mexican sunflower thrives in heat, poor soil, and dry conditions, making it ideal for back-of-border plantings in Gulf Coast summers when other annuals falter. The vivid orange, 3-inch daisy-like flowers are irresistible to monarch butterflies, swallowtails, and hummingbirds from midsummer through frost. Deadhead regularly by cutting stems (not just the flower head) back to a side shoot to maintain tidy plants and prolong heavy bloom.

plant_note: Surface sow; needs light and dislikes transplanting.
directions: Direct sow mignonette on the soil surface, as it needs light to germinate and resents root disturbance. The unremarkable greenish flower spikes release an intensely sweet raspberry-like fragrance beloved in Victorian gardens. Grow in full sun to light shade in cool weather and fertile, alkaline soil. Position it near paths and seating where its perfume can be enjoyed, and make successive sowings for a long season.

plant_note: Plant tuber-like roots in fall or early spring in a cool, moist, partially shaded site; wear gloves — all parts are highly toxic.
directions: Plant with the tuber-like roots 3–4 in deep in rich, cool, moist, well-drained soil; monkshood is a stately perennial with bold, deeply lobed foliage and tall spikes of deep blue-violet helmet-shaped flowers in late summer to early fall. All parts of the plant are extremely toxic if ingested or if the sap contacts broken skin — always wear gloves when handling and wash hands thoroughly after. It is one of the best late-season blues in the perennial garden and thrives where summers are cool. Divide every 4–5 years in early spring; keep plants where they will not be disturbed by children or pets.

plant_note: Plant corms in spring; naturalizes and can spread widely.
directions: Plant montbretia corms in spring three to four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. The arching sprays of fiery orange to red tubular flowers in mid to late summer are magnets for hummingbirds. It multiplies rapidly by cormlets and can spread widely, so site it where vigorous growth is welcome. Divide congested clumps every few years and mulch for winter in the colder end of its range.

plant_note: Start indoors 2–4 weeks before last frost after nicking or soaking seed; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Nick or soak seeds in warm water for 12 hours before sowing 1/2 in deep. Moonflower is a vigorous, fast-growing vine that needs a strong trellis or fence. Unlike morning glories, its large, fragrant white blossoms open in the evening and remain open through the night and into the following morning. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers to encourage blooming.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; or nick and soak seed overnight then start indoors 2–4 weeks early.
directions: Nick the hard seed coat with a file or soak in warm water for 8–12 hours before sowing 1/2 in deep to speed germination. Morning glories need a fence, trellis, or other support and are best sown directly at the base of their support. Avoid over-fertilizing, which promotes leaves at the expense of flowers. Blooms open in the morning and close by afternoon on each individual day.

plant_note: Plant in fall; wear gloves as bulbs can irritate skin.
directions: Plant multiflora hyacinth bulbs in fall about six inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Unlike single-spike hyacinths, this type sends up several looser, fragrant flower stalks per bulb for a naturalistic effect. Wear gloves when handling, as the bulbs can irritate sensitive skin. Deadhead after bloom and let foliage ripen; flower size may diminish over the years as it perennializes.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks before last frost; nasturtiums prefer cool soil and bolt in summer heat.
directions: Soak large seeds overnight then direct sow 1/2 in deep in poor, well-drained soil. Avoid fertilizing, as rich soil produces lush leaves at the expense of flowers. Deadhead spent blooms to prolong the season, or allow them to set seed and collect for next year. Both flowers and leaves are edible with a peppery flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in fine-textured germination mix at 60–65°F; avoid higher temperatures as heat inhibits germination. Plant outdoors in cool weather in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade where summers are hot. Nemesia blooms prolifically in cool conditions with cheerful two-toned flowers in a huge range of colors; it typically falters in summer heat so shear back and move containers to a cooler spot. Plants may revive and rebloom in fall when temperatures drop again.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in a damp paper towel in the refrigerator before sowing indoors to improve germination rates. Pinch plants back by one-third in late spring to encourage bushier growth and delay bloom until fall, when their purple flowers are invaluable for pollinators. Tall varieties may need staking or can be grown through a peony cage. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in early spring to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; plants bloom in year two from seed but transplants purchased in spring bloom the first year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination. Plant in moist to average soil in full sun; established plants spread aggressively by underground rhizomes and may require annual division to prevent them from overtaking the border. The name refers to the curious behavior of individual florets staying in place when moved along the square stem. The pink to lilac spikes bloom in late summer and fall when pollinators are seeking food before winter.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring as soon as the ground can be worked; fall planting is preferred in zones 4–7.
directions: Plant bulbs 6–8 in deep (3 times the bulb diameter) in well-drained, organically rich, slightly acidic soil; apply mulch to keep roots cool. Oriental lilies prefer to have their heads in the sun and their feet in the shade — interplant with low perennials to shade the root zone. The large, intensely fragrant blooms appear in mid- to late summer; deadhead after flowering but allow foliage to remain until it yellows naturally. Avoid overhead watering to reduce the risk of botrytis; stake tall cultivars against wind damage.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seeds need cool soil and germinate best when sown directly outdoors.
directions: Surface-sow seeds in early spring on well-prepared, well-drained soil; seeds need cool temperatures and light, so press firmly without covering. Oriental poppies bloom for a brief but spectacular 2–3 weeks in late spring to early summer with crepe-paper petals in orange, red, pink, or white up to 6 in across. Plants go dormant after bloom leaving a bare spot — interplant with late-emerging annuals like baby's breath or dahlias to fill the gap. Divide clumps in late summer while dormant; established plants dislike being moved during the growing season.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall where winters are mild.
directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface in early spring as they need light and cool temperatures to germinate; do not cover seeds. Orlaya produces flat-topped umbel flowers with large, irregular outer florets that create a delicate lace effect in pure white, ideal as a cut flower filler. It prefers lean, well-drained soil — overly rich conditions produce lush foliage at the expense of flowers. Plants self-sow readily where happy, returning reliably if the ground is not disturbed; collect seed or allow them to fall for the following year.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before the ground freezes, for late-spring to early-summer bloom.
directions: Plant bulbs in fall at a depth of 3 times their diameter in sharply drained soil; the large-flowered types like 'Globemaster' can be planted 12 in deep for stability. Foliage often begins to yellow while the flowers are still in bloom — interplanting with perennials helps mask the dying leaves. Deer and rodents avoid alliums, making them a good choice for wildlife-prone gardens. Spherical seedheads are ornamentally interesting and can be dried for arrangements.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil has warmed thoroughly.
directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost at 70–75°F; transplant after frost danger passes into full sun and fertile, well-drained soil — warm soil is essential for strong establishment. Grown as an annual for its striking deep burgundy-purple foliage and the large, cylindrical flower/grain heads that arch gracefully under their own weight in late summer. An excellent anchor plant in tropical-style or mixed annual borders, and the dried seed heads are useful in arrangements. Highly attractive to birds in fall — leave some plants intact for wildlife value.

plant_note: Sow in place in early spring for summer color.
directions: Direct sow annual painted daisy in early spring where plants are to bloom. The colorful banded daisies with contrasting central rings make superb long-lasting cut flowers. Grow in full sun and average, well-drained soil, staking taller plants in windy sites. Deadhead frequently to prolong flowering, and the plants often self-sow lightly in favorable conditions.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds in the dark (cover lightly and keep the tray in darkness) at 60–70°F; darkness improves germination for salpiglossis. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; the petunia-like flowers with intricate veining in yellow, red, purple, maroon, and bicolor thrive in cool weather. Provide light support with twiggy stakes since plants can flop in wind; pinch tips once for bushier plants. Plants decline in summer heat — treat them as a spring and fall flower in warm climates or plant where afternoon shade moderates temperatures.

plant_note: Start indoors 12–14 weeks before last frost for spring planting, or start in late summer for fall transplanting. Plants tolerate hard frost once established.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in a moist starting mix; cover the tray with dark plastic as darkness aids germination. Transplant outdoors several weeks before the last frost, as cool weather brings out the richest colors and most abundant bloom. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to prevent seed set, which stops flowering. Plants decline as summer heat arrives; remove and replace with heat-tolerant annuals.

plant_note: Plant in fall in a sheltered spot; blooms are heavy.
directions: Plant parrot tulip bulbs in autumn six to eight inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and a spot sheltered from wind. The flamboyant fringed and twisted petals are streaked and feathered in bold colors, making dramatic cut flowers. Because the large blooms can flop, choose a protected location and stake if necessary. Let foliage die back naturally, and in mild-winter areas chill bulbs before planting.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall; sap and foliage can irritate skin — handle with gloves and avoid contact with eyes.
directions: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a sunny, gravelly, or rocky, very well-drained site; pasque flower germinates best from fresh seed with natural cold stratification and is slow to establish but extremely long-lived once settled. Silky, nodding purple, lavender, or white flowers emerge in early spring from the fuzzy, hairy foliage and are followed by fluffy, ornamental seedheads similar to clematis. Perfectly drained, lean, alkaline or neutral soil is essential — clay or continuously wet conditions are fatal. Resents transplanting; sow in situ or plant container-grown specimens when small.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; seeds benefit from 4 weeks of cold stratification.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks in damp medium in the refrigerator before sowing indoors for best germination; transplant after the last frost into well-drained to dry soil. Penstemon thrives in poor, gravelly soil and is one of the most important native nectar plants for hummingbirds and native bees. The tubular white to pale pink flowers appear in late spring to early summer; deadhead to a basal rosette of leaves after bloom. Excellent drainage is critical — wet winter conditions are the leading cause of plant loss.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist fine-textured mix and provide light at 70–80°F; germination occurs within 7–14 days. Pentas is one of the best butterfly and hummingbird plants for hot, humid gardens, blooming continuously from transplant to frost without deadheading. Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil and fertilize monthly to sustain the heavy flower production. Shear plants back by one-third in midsummer if they become leggy; they recover quickly in heat and resume flowering within 2–3 weeks.

plant_note: Plant bare-root divisions in fall 4–6 weeks before the ground freezes; eyes no deeper than 1–2 in below soil surface.
directions: Plant roots with the pink eyes no more than 1–2 in below the soil surface — planting too deeply is the most common reason peonies fail to bloom. Peonies require 6 weeks of temperatures below 40°F to set flower buds, so they need cold winters and are not suited to zones 9+. Expect minimal bloom the first 1–2 years as the plant establishes; mature plants can live 50+ years with little care. Cut foliage to the ground in fall to remove potential disease inoculum.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained soil; established plants are drought-tolerant.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil — perennial salvia is exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant once established and will rot in soggy conditions. Shear plants back by one-third to one-half after the first flush of deep violet-blue spikes fades to trigger a strong second (and sometimes third) bloom. The aromatic foliage deters deer and rabbits, making it a reliable choice for wildlife-challenged gardens. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to keep them vigorous.

plant_note: Plant tuberous roots in spring; superb cut flower.
directions: Plant Peruvian lily tuberous roots in spring about six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil, handling the brittle roots carefully. The orchid-like flowers in warm oranges, pinks, and reds are among the longest-lasting of all cut flowers. Grow in full sun to light shade with consistent moisture, and pull rather than cut spent stems to stimulate rebloom. In colder zones mulch heavily for winter or lift the roots for storage.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow tiny seed on the surface of moist starting mix — do not cover, as petunias need light to germinate. Pinch transplants by one-third when planting to encourage branching. Deadhead or shear back leggy plants by half in midsummer to renew vigor and flush of bloom. Feed every two weeks with a balanced liquid fertilizer for best performance.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in a well-drained mix; germination is reliable at 60–70°F and seedlings grow quickly. Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly alkaline soil — scabiosa performs best in cool to moderate temperatures and fades in intense summer heat. Cut flowers frequently to encourage continuous bloom from early summer until fall; flowers are excellent for cutting with long, wiry stems and last 7–10 days in a vase. In zones 7–10 try sowing in fall for earlier spring bloom before heat sets in.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed into a sunny, alkaline-friendly site.
directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost, sowing 1/4 in deep in a light germination mix; transplant outdoors after frost into well-drained, slightly alkaline or neutral soil. The domed, pincushion flowers in rich maroon, lavender, white, pink, and burgundy are magnets for butterflies and superb for cutting. Deadhead consistently to prevent self-seeding and extend the long bloom period from early summer through frost. Avoid rich, wet soils — scabiosa thrives in moderately lean conditions and is drought-tolerant once established.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in spring after soil warms; lift where not hardy.
directions: Plant pineapple lily bulbs in spring about five inches deep in rich, well-drained soil once the ground has warmed. Each stout stem produces a dense spike of starry flowers topped by a tuft of leafy bracts resembling a pineapple. Grow in full sun with regular summer moisture, and it also performs beautifully in large containers. In cold climates lift the bulbs before frost or grow them in pots moved indoors for winter.

plant_note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring; avoid fall planting in zones 6–7 as plants need a full season to establish before winter.
directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained, lean to average soil; muhly grass is tolerant of sandy soil, heat, humidity, and drought once established and is an outstanding choice for hot, dry southern landscapes. The main attraction is the spectacular cloud of airy, rose-pink flower plumes in September–October that transform the planting into something otherworldly when backlit by low autumn sun. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter before new growth begins; avoid heavy organic mulching which can cause crown rot in humid climates. Divide every 4–5 years in spring to rejuvenate large clumps.

plant_note: Sow directly; a top pollinator and self-sowing native.
directions: Direct sow plains coreopsis on the soil surface, as light aids germination. This North American native bears masses of yellow daisies banded in mahogany-red that attract bees and butterflies. It tolerates poor, dry soils and full sun, making it excellent for meadows and low-maintenance borders. Allow some seed to ripen for dependable self-sowing, or deadhead to extend the bloom period.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost after surface-sowing and cold-stratifying at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist, sterile mix and cold-stratify at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks to break dormancy, then move to 65°F for germination. Grow on at cool temperatures (55–60°F) since heat causes etiolation; transplant into partial shade in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in early spring. Primroses bloom best in cool, moist conditions — they are outstanding spring plants but typically go dormant in summer heat. Divide crowded clumps after bloom in spring and replant divisions in refreshed, compost-amended soil.

plant_note: Plant tubers after frost; excellent long-lasting cut flower.
directions: Plant pompon dahlia tubers after the last frost about four to six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil. The small, perfectly spherical, tightly quilled flowers are outstanding long-lasting cut flowers. Grow in full sun, stake the stems, and pinch young plants to encourage more blooms. Deadhead regularly and, in cold zones, dig and store the tubers over winter after the first frost blackens the foliage.

plant_note: Direct sow on the soil surface after last frost when soil is warm; seed needs warmth and light to germinate.
directions: Scatter seed on the surface of sandy or gritty soil and press lightly — do not cover seed, as light is needed for germination. Portulaca is one of the most drought- and heat-tolerant annuals available, thriving in poor, dry soil where other flowers fail. Blooms open in full sun and close at night or in cloudy weather; modern varieties stay open longer. Self-sows readily in warm climates.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; very slow to establish from seed — divisions or plugs reach ornamental size much faster.
directions: Sow seeds in full sun in lean, well-drained, sandy or rocky soil after last frost; prairie dropseed is a slow-growing native that takes 2–3 years from seed to develop into an ornamental clump. The extremely fine-textured, emerald-green foliage forms a perfect arching fountain shape, and open, airy flower panicles in late summer emit a distinctive fragrance of warm popcorn or cilantro. Fall color is a beautiful tawny orange-gold and the foliage persists attractively through winter. Once established it is extremely drought- and heat-tolerant and virtually maintenance-free.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; or start indoors in late winter with 8–10 weeks of cold moist stratification.
directions: Sow seeds in fall and allow winter cold to naturally stratify them, or cold-stratify indoors for 8–10 weeks before spring sowing; plant in lean, well-drained to dry soil in full sun. The nodding, burgundy-pink flowers in mid-spring are followed by the spectacular feathery, smoke-like seed plumes that give the plant its name and provide ornamental interest for weeks. Prairie smoke is a rugged, drought-tolerant native wildflower that thrives in rocky or sandy soils where more demanding plants struggle. It spreads slowly into a weed-suppressing groundcover in open, sunny areas.

plant_note: Start indoors 10 weeks early; slow to establish.
directions: Start purple bell vine indoors ten weeks before the last frost, surface sowing the fine seed and providing warmth. This delicate climber produces dangling flowers with rosy-pink parasol-shaped calyces and dark tubular petals. Grow on a light trellis or in hanging baskets in full sun to partial shade with rich, moist soil. It is grown as a tender annual in most climates and blooms until frost.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; cold stratification for 4–8 weeks improves germination rates significantly. Transplant after last frost into well-drained soil — established plants tolerate drought well. Flowers typically appear in year 2 from seed; deadhead spent blooms to extend the season or leave seedheads for birds. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Plant container-grown transplants after last frost; this cultivar does not come true from seed and is propagated by division.
directions: Plant after last frost in full sun and very well-drained soil; purple fountain grass is grown as an annual north of zone 9 for its arching burgundy-red foliage and showy, rose-tinged plumes from midsummer through frost. It thrives in heat and drought once established but requires consistent moisture the first few weeks after transplanting. In frost-free climates it is a perennial that can become invasive — avoid planting near natural areas and remove flower heads before seeds ripen. Bring container-grown specimens indoors before frost to overwinter as a houseplant in colder zones.

plant_note: In mild-winter climates (zones 8–10) plant corms in fall for late-winter/spring bloom; in cold climates start indoors and transplant after last frost.
directions: Soak corms in cool water for 4 hours before planting, then set them claw-side down about 2 in deep in well-drained, fertile soil. Ranunculus thrives in cool temperatures (45–65°F) and will decline when heat arrives, so timing is critical. In zones 9–10 plant October through January for blooms from February through May; provide afternoon shade in the warmest areas. After foliage yellows, allow corms to dry in situ or lift, cure, and store in a cool dry location for replanting.

plant_note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall for natural cold stratification; transplants poorly due to deep taproot.
directions: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a sunny prairie or meadow garden in lean, well-drained soil; rattlesnake master develops a deep taproot and resents being moved after establishment. The dramatic, yucca-like basal rosette of spiny blue-green leaves is topped by tall branched stems bearing small, spiny, globe-shaped white flower heads in midsummer that are highly attractive to native bees. It is extremely drought-tolerant and actually performs better in poor, sandy or rocky soil than in rich garden beds. The sculptural seedheads provide winter interest and are prized for dried arrangements.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed in spring.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep and maintain even moisture at 65–75°F until germination. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil — good drainage is critical, especially in winter, as plants rot in waterlogged conditions. The torch-like flower spikes transition from red or orange in bud to yellow as the florets open, creating a two-toned effect that attracts hummingbirds throughout summer and fall. In zones 5–6 tie the leaves together and mulch deeply around the crown before the first hard freeze to protect the growing point.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; plants may bloom the first fall from an early sowing or establish and bloom their second year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained soil; germination is fast and reliable. Plant in full sun in poor to average, very well-drained soil — rose campion thrives under dry, sunny conditions that would stress many perennials. The intensely magenta-crimson flowers glow against the woolly, silver-white felted stems and leaves, which are ornamental throughout the growing season. Individual plants are short-lived (2–3 years) but self-sow abundantly so colonies persist indefinitely; thin or transplant self-sown seedlings to prevent overcrowding.

plant_note: Plant in spring after last frost in lean, well-drained soil; established plants are extremely drought-tolerant.
directions: Plant in poor to average, sharply drained soil in full sun — Russian sage declines quickly in fertile or moist soil and is highly susceptible to root rot in clay. Cut plants back hard in early spring (to about 6 in) just as new basal growth emerges; do not cut back in fall as the woody framework helps protect the crown. The airy silver stems and lavender-blue flowers create a haze of color from midsummer through fall and are beloved by pollinators. Deer avoid this plant due to its strongly aromatic foliage.

plant_note: Direct sow in place; short-lived, so succession sow.
directions: Broadcast scarlet flax seed thinly on prepared soil in early spring and rake in lightly. The brilliant red saucer-shaped flowers dance atop wiry stems and are a favorite in wildflower mixes. Because individual plants are short-lived, make successive sowings for continuous color through the season. It thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil with minimal supplemental water.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; tap-rooted and resents transplanting — sow where plants will remain.
directions: Sow seeds directly in well-drained, even poor, sandy or gravelly soil; sea holly develops a deep taproot and strongly dislikes being transplanted after establishment. The metallic steel-blue stems, bracts, and thimble-shaped flower heads appear in midsummer and hold their color and structure well into fall. Drought-tolerant once established, sea holly declines in rich, moist soil — lean, infertile conditions produce the best blue coloration. Cut stems for fresh or dried arrangements before flowers fully open to preserve the vivid blue color.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; division is easily done by digging and separating rooted stems.
directions: Plant in any well-drained soil — even poor, gravelly soil suits sedum perfectly; overly rich or moist soil causes floppy, open-centered plants. Tall sedums may flop in midseason; pinch once in late spring or grow through a support ring to keep them upright. Leave the russet seedheads standing through winter as they are attractive and provide structure in the dormant garden. Divide every 3–4 years in spring by separating the fleshy crown sections.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist potting mix and keep at 60–70°F for germination; transplant after the last frost into well-drained soil. Shasta daisies often bloom lightly the first year from seed and fully the second year — named cultivars from division or plugs bloom more reliably in year 1. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage repeat blooming from midsummer into fall. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring, discarding the woody center and replanting vigorous outer sections.

plant_note: Surface sow on cool soil; do not cover the fine seed.
directions: Scatter the tiny Shirley poppy seed on the surface of cool, moist soil in very early spring, or sow in fall in mild regions. Do not cover the seed, which needs light and cold to germinate well. The delicate crepe-paper flowers in pink, red, and white are classic cottage and meadow plants. Poppies resent transplanting, so always direct sow, and let some pods ripen for self-seeding.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring as soon as plants can be purchased; also self-seeds moderately in moist, shaded sites.
directions: Plant in partial to full shade in cool, humus-rich, moist but well-drained soil; brunnera is one of the best foliage plants for shade, particularly the silver-variegated cultivars like 'Jack Frost.' Delicate sprays of forget-me-not blue flowers appear in early to mid-spring above the large, heart-shaped leaves. In hot or dry conditions the leaves may scorch — consistent moisture and afternoon shade are essential in warmer climates. Divide clumps every 4–5 years in early spring; variegated cultivars must be divided rather than grown from seed to maintain their markings.

plant_note: Plant divisions in early spring as growth begins, or in late summer after flowering; set rhizomes just below soil surface.
directions: Plant rhizomes just 1–2 in below the soil surface in moisture-retentive, slightly acidic soil; unlike bearded iris, Siberian iris prefers consistently moist conditions and tolerates wet spots. Graceful, narrow foliage forms attractive clumps all season, topped by elegant blue, purple, or white flowers in early summer. Siberian iris are very long-lived and rarely need dividing — only do so when the center of the clump dies out or blooming diminishes, typically every 5–8 years. Division is best done immediately after flowering or in early fall; replant promptly and water well.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs in fall; naturalizes in lawns.
directions: Plant Siberian squill bulbs in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil in sun to partial shade. The nodding, intensely blue star-shaped flowers appear very early in spring and provide crucial forage for early bees. It naturalizes freely in lawns and under deciduous trees, forming sheets of blue over the years. Plant in generous drifts and leave the foliage to ripen so the colony can expand.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in early fall, 4 in deep, in moist but well-drained soil; ideal for naturalizing in damp meadows.
directions: Plant bulbs in early fall, 4 in deep and 3–4 in apart, in organically rich, moisture-retentive but not waterlogged soil; perfect for damp meadow or pond-edge plantings. The nodding, checkerboard-patterned bells in deep purple, white, or pink appear in mid-spring and are among the most intricately patterned flowers in the spring garden. Naturalizes freely in damp grassland — avoid mowing until foliage has died back completely in early summer. Once established the bulbs multiply steadily; divide congested clumps in early fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Press seed onto the surface of moist starting mix and do not cover — seed needs light to germinate. Pinch seedlings once to promote multiple spikes. Transplant to the garden a few weeks before the last frost, as snapdragons thrive in cool conditions and can tolerate a light freeze. After the primary spike blooms, cut it off to encourage a second flush from side branches.

plant_note: Plant corms in fall; naturalizes and resists squirrels.
directions: Plant snow crocus corms in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil and full to partial sun. This early species crocus naturalizes rapidly in lawns and borders and is more squirrel-resistant than large Dutch crocus. The slender lavender flowers are among the first blooms of late winter, providing vital early forage for bees. Leave the foliage undisturbed until it withers to allow the corms to multiply.

plant_note: Plant small bulbs in fall, 3 in deep; alternatively plant 'in the green' (just after bloom) for better establishment.
directions: Plant bulbs 3 in deep in moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees or in a shaded border; snowdrops are one of the first bulbs to bloom, often in January or February. They establish most successfully when transplanted 'in the green' — lifted and divided just after blooming while foliage is still active. Clumps multiply steadily and benefit from division every 3–4 years right after flowering to prevent overcrowding. Allow foliage to die back naturally; avoid mowing naturalised areas until leaves have fully yellowed.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; naturalizes readily in shade.
directions: Plant Spanish bluebell bulbs in fall about four inches deep in moist, well-drained woodland soil in partial shade. The upright stems carry many bell-shaped blue, pink, or white flowers in mid to late spring. It naturalizes vigorously beneath trees and shrubs and can spread aggressively, so site it thoughtfully. Allow the foliage to yellow and die back naturally to feed the bulbs for next season.

plant_note: Soak and nick seed; start indoors 6 weeks early.
directions: Nick and soak the hard seeds overnight, then start Spanish flag indoors six weeks before the last frost. The vigorous vine produces one-sided sprays of tubular flowers that shade from scarlet through orange to creamy yellow. Provide a strong trellis in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil, transplanting after all frost danger passes. Hummingbirds and butterflies flock to the blooms, which continue until first frost.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; cold stratification for 4 weeks improves germination of seed.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks before sowing; established divisions establish more reliably than seed-grown plants and can be done in spring or fall. Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, average to rich soil; plants tolerate wet conditions and make excellent rain garden plants. The three-petaled flowers in purple, blue, pink, or white open only for a single morning but are produced in rapid succession over many weeks. Shear the entire plant to 8 in after the main bloom flush to stimulate fresh foliage and a second round of flowers in fall.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; foliage smells of onion when crushed.
directions: Plant spring starflower bulbs in fall about three inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. The fragrant, star-shaped, pale blue flowers appear over grassy foliage that smells faintly of onion when bruised. It naturalizes readily in mild climates, forming spreading clumps in rock gardens and borders. Keep the site dry during summer dormancy and divide crowded clumps every few years.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; huge seed heads dry beautifully.
directions: Plant star of Persia bulbs in fall about six inches deep in sharply drained soil and full sun. Each stem bears an enormous globe up to ten inches across of metallic star-shaped violet florets in late spring. The dried seed heads persist for weeks and are prized for arrangements. Site it in a hot, dry spot and let the bulbs bake during summer dormancy to ensure reliable return.

plant_note: Direct sow; drought tolerant and heat loving.
directions: Direct sow star of the veldt in warm, well-drained soil after frost danger passes. This South African annual bears glowing orange and apricot daisies that open in bright sun and close on cloudy days. It thrives in heat and lean soil with minimal water, making it excellent for dry sunny beds and containers. Deadhead spent blooms and avoid overwatering, which can cause rot.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate mild frost.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix; seeds come in a papery casing and germination is slow. Statice prefers cool conditions early in the season. Harvest flower stems for drying when 75% of the florets on each branch have opened. The papery calyces — which hold their color dried — are the ornamental part; the true petals are tiny and brief.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as young plants tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix — light aids germination, so cover with only the thinnest layer of vermiculite if at all. Stock thrives in cool weather (55–65°F nights) and the fragrance is intensified by cool temperatures. Transplant to the garden in early spring; plants begin to decline when daytime temperatures consistently exceed 80°F. Cut flowers when one-third of the buds on the spike are open for the longest vase life.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 4–6 weeks; transplant after last frost.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist medium at 35–40°F for 4–6 weeks before sowing indoors; germination is slow but improved by stratification. Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy or loamy soil; Stokes' aster is native to the American Southeast and tolerates heat and humidity well but dislikes wet winter conditions. The large, cornflower-like blooms in blue, lavender, or white appear from early summer to fall and are excellent for cutting. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor; plants spread slowly and are well-behaved in the border.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix and press lightly — light aids germination, so barely cover seed. Strawflowers tolerate drought and poor soils remarkably well. For drying, harvest stems when the central button of each head is still closed, before the outer bracts fully open; they will continue to open as they dry. Deadhead garden plants regularly to maintain continuous bloom.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; tolerates wet soils.
directions: Plant summer snowflake bulbs in fall about four inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in sun to partial shade. Despite its name it blooms in mid to late spring, bearing nodding white bells tipped with green on arching stems. Unusually among bulbs it tolerates heavy, wet soils and naturalizes along ponds and in damp borders. Leave the strappy foliage to ripen fully, and the clumps will slowly increase over the years.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost when soil is warm; succession sow every 2–3 weeks for extended bloom.
directions: Direct sow seed 1 in deep after the last frost date, thinning to final spacing once seedlings reach 6 in tall. Sunflowers prefer poor-to-average soil and resent transplanting due to a taproot. Single-stemmed types bloom once; branching varieties produce multiple heads if cut regularly. Harvest seed heads when the back of the head turns yellow-brown and seeds feel firm.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 weeks early; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Start Swan River daisy indoors about six weeks before the last frost for earliest bloom. The dainty, fragrant daisies in blue, mauve, and white cover mounding plants ideal for edging, containers, and hanging baskets. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, avoiding overly rich conditions that soften growth. Shear lightly after the first flush and feed lightly to keep the plant blooming through summer.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; alyssum tolerates light frost and prefers cool weather.
directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface and press gently — light aids germination; just barely cover if needed to prevent the seed from blowing away. Sweet alyssum may slow or stop blooming in peak summer heat; shear plants back by half and they will rebound strongly when temperatures cool in early fall. The honey-scented flowers attract beneficial insects throughout the season.

plant_note: Direct sow outdoors 4–8 weeks before last frost; seeds prefer cool soil (55–65°F) and young plants tolerate frost.
directions: Nick or soak seeds overnight before sowing 1 in deep in cool, enriched soil at the base of a trellis. Sweet peas perform best during cool weather and decline as summer heat arrives — sow as early as possible. Cut flowers frequently, as even a few fading blooms left on the plant will trigger seed set and halt blooming. Provide sturdy support from the start, as vines climb to 4–6 ft.

plant_note: Start indoors 6 weeks early for cut flowers.
directions: Start sweet scabious indoors about six weeks before the last frost, or direct sow once soil warms. The domed pincushion flowers in deep maroon, pink, and white are long-lasting cut flowers and pollinator magnets. Grow in full sun and fertile, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil, staking tall stems if needed. Harvest or deadhead frequently to keep the plants producing new blooms all summer.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; does not transplant well due to taproot.
directions: Sow seeds directly in a sunny, well-drained location in early spring; thin to 9–12 in apart as seedlings emerge. The fragrant, fringed, powder-puff flowers in shades of pink, purple, yellow, and white are excellent for cutting, with a pleasant, musky sweet scent. Plants prefer cool weather and will begin to decline as summer heat intensifies — make a second sowing in late summer for fall bloom in warm climates. Successive sowings every 3–4 weeks in spring extend the flowering season through early summer.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant can go out 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants are frost-tolerant.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; germination is fast and reliable at 65–70°F. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; sweet William is technically a biennial that flowers in its second year, but modern annual varieties bloom the first year from an early indoor start. The domed, fragrant flower clusters in vivid shades of red, pink, white, and bicolor appear in late spring and early summer. Deadhead spent clusters promptly to extend the blooming period; plants self-sow readily to maintain a colony from year to year.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in spring; warm soil (above 60°F) promotes best germination.
directions: Sow seeds in full sun after last frost in average to poor, well-drained soil; switchgrass is extremely adaptable and tolerates clay, sand, wet or dry conditions, and even brief flooding — a true workhorse native grass. The airy, branching panicles of tiny seeds in late summer create a misty, luminous effect, and many cultivars develop spectacular red, burgundy, or orange fall color. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter; divide every 3–4 years in spring when the clump center dies out. Named cultivars like 'Shenandoah' and 'Prairie Fire' offer predictably strong red fall color.

plant_note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 1–2 weeks; often blooms from seed the first year.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist towels for 1–2 weeks at 35–40°F, then sow indoors at 65–75°F; germination remains erratic over 14–28 days. Plant in full sun in poor to average, well-drained soil; this South American native is drought-tolerant and naturalizes readily once established. The wiry, branching stems carry clusters of small purple flowers from midsummer through frost at an unusual height that allows it to weave transparently through other plants. Grown as an annual north of zone 7, it self-sows prolifically even in cold climates and seedlings replace killed parent plants.

plant_note: Direct sow after frost; thrives in heat.
directions: Direct sow tassel flower after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil. The small brush-like scarlet and orange flowers rise on wiry stems above tidy rosettes, adding fine texture to arrangements. It thrives in heat and full sun and blooms freely all summer with little care. Deadhead to prolong flowering and allow occasional self-sowing for future plants.

plant_note: Plant in fall; includes paperwhites for forcing.
directions: Plant tazetta daffodil bulbs in fall six inches deep in well-drained soil, or force paperwhite types indoors in pebbles and water. These clustered, intensely fragrant narcissi are well suited to mild-winter gardens and bloom early. Give them full sun and let the foliage die back naturally outdoors. Forced bulbs are generally discarded after flowering, while garden bulbs naturalize in suitable zones.

plant_note: Sow in fall or early spring; a reliable wildflower for meadows.
directions: Scatter tidy tips seed on cool, moist soil in fall or early spring and rake in lightly. This California native produces cheerful yellow daisies tipped in white that attract native bees and butterflies. It thrives on lean soil with minimal water once established, making it ideal for wildflower meadows and drought-tolerant plantings. Allow some flowers to set seed for reliable self-sowing in future seasons.

plant_note: Plant bulbs after all frost danger passes in spring, once soil has warmed above 60°F.
directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; each exotic three-petaled flower lasts only one day but new buds open in succession over weeks. The vivid blooms come in yellow, orange, red, pink, and white, typically with a heavily spotted or blotched center cup. Plant in groups of a dozen or more for a showy display from midsummer into fall. In zones colder than 8, dig bulbs after first frost, dry thoroughly, and store in paper bags in a cool, frost-free location.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall; propagates from stem bulbils.
directions: Plant tiger lily bulbs in fall six to eight inches deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. The recurved orange flowers heavily spotted with black nod in mid to late summer atop tall sturdy stems. This vigorous heirloom produces small dark bulbils in the leaf axils that can be planted to grow new bulbs. It is a known carrier of viruses, so plant it well away from other prized lilies.

plant_note: Plant divisions in early spring as growth begins; they establish quickly and bloom in their first season from divisions.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained shade; toad lily is an elegant late-season bloomer that produces exotic-looking, orchid-like flowers in white, purple, or lilac with dark spots from late summer through fall. The arching stems with clasping leaves create a graceful, architectural presence throughout the season. Consistent moisture is essential — plants decline in dry soil; mulch heavily to maintain soil moisture and temperature. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to manage the slowly spreading clumps.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall 6-8 inches deep before ground freezes.
directions: Plant Triumph tulip bulbs in fall about six to eight inches deep and four to six inches apart in well-drained soil. This mid-season class offers sturdy stems and classic egg-shaped blooms in a vast color range, excellent for beds and cutting. Provide full sun and allow the foliage to yellow naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb. In warm climates pre-chill bulbs for ten to fourteen weeks before planting, or treat them as annuals.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 8–11 it acts as a short-lived perennial and blooms from spring through hard frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in warm starting mix and transplant after frost danger has passed; tropical milkweed establishes quickly and blooms in its first season unlike most native milkweeds. It is an important monarch host plant and nectar source throughout the Gulf Coast growing season, but in zone 9–11 where it does not freeze back, cut plants to 6 in in fall and winter to disrupt the overwinter buildup of Ophryocystis elektroscirrha (OE), a protozoan parasite that accumulates on non-dormant milkweed. Deadhead spent flower clusters to encourage continuous blooming, or allow seedpods to dry and open for easy self-sowing. The red-and-yellow bicolor flowers distinguish it from native milkweeds at a glance.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring; never let dry out.
directions: Plant trumpet lily bulbs in fall or early spring about eight inches deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil. The tall stems carry many large, powerfully fragrant trumpet-shaped flowers in midsummer and often need staking. Give them full sun with cool, shaded roots and never allow the bulbs to dry out completely. Leave stems standing until they yellow so the bulbs can store energy for the following year.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall when soil temperatures drop below 55°F, about 4–8 weeks before hard frost.
directions: Plant bulbs pointed end up at a depth of 3 times their height, typically 6–8 in deep, in well-drained soil — standing water will cause rot. In warmer zones (7–8), pre-chill bulbs for 6–8 weeks in the refrigerator before planting to simulate the cold period they need. Most hybrid tulips perform best in year 1; replanting fresh bulbs annually is common practice in mild climates. Allow leaves to die back naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb.

plant_note: Plant container stock after last frost; an extremely tough Texas native that thrives in heat, part shade, and dry soils where few flowering shrubs will grow.
directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil in partial to full sun; Turk's cap is one of the most shade-tolerant flowering natives for the Gulf South and performs well under the canopy of live oaks. The distinctive red flowers never fully open, forming a twisted turban shape, and are a top hummingbird nectar source from summer through fall. Small red fruits that follow are edible and mildly sweet — birds devour them quickly, and new plants sprout readily from seeds they drop. Prune back to 12–18 in in late winter; in zone 7–8 the top dies back but the roots are reliably hardy to about 10°F.

plant_note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in well-drained soil; excellent drought tolerance once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; speedwell is notably drought-tolerant and performs poorly in soggy or heavy clay conditions. The upright spikes of blue, pink, or white flowers bloom from late spring into summer and are excellent for cutting. Deadhead by removing spent spikes back to a lateral bud or leaf to extend the flowering period and prevent excessive self-seeding. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor and an attractive mounding habit.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant several weeks before last frost as plants tolerate mild freezes.
directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in a cool, moist starting mix; darkness during germination is beneficial. Violas are more petite and heat-tolerant than pansies, often self-sowing prolifically in the garden. Deadhead to extend flowering, or allow seed to set for naturalized self-seeding the following year. They shine in early spring containers and border edges.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a shaded, moist site; or plant divisions/transplants in early spring.
directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, shaded to partly shaded woodland soil; Virginia bluebells are a spring ephemeral that goes dormant by early summer, disappearing completely underground. The clusters of pink buds open to sky-blue, nodding tubular flowers in early to mid-spring and are among the most beautiful of native wildflowers. Mark the location of clumps so they are not accidentally disturbed; interplant with hostas, ferns, or other perennials that will fill the gap when bluebells go dormant. Divide crowded clumps or transplant in early fall when dormant.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring for late spring color.
directions: Direct sow viscaria thinly in early spring where plants are to bloom. The dainty rose, blue, and white five-petaled flowers cover slender stems and suit cottage gardens and cutting beds. It appreciates full sun and well-drained soil and blooms quickly from seed. Thin seedlings for good air movement and deadhead to prolong the flowering period.

plant_note: Plant corms after last frost in spring for summer bloom; in mild-winter climates (zones 9–10) plant in fall.
directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in fertile, well-drained soil; watsonia closely resembles gladiolus and thrives in similar conditions. The tall, sword-like foliage produces elegant spikes of tubular flowers in shades of pink, red, coral, white, and bicolor throughout summer. In frost-free climates corms multiply into large colonies and can become invasive — divide every few years to manage spread. In colder zones lift corms after frost kills the foliage, cure, and store in a cool, dry, frost-free location.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost due to slow growth; transplant after frost danger has fully passed.
directions: Sow dust-fine seed on the surface of moist, sterile starting mix under bright light — do not cover, as seed needs light to germinate. Start early as wax begonias are slow growers before transplant size. These self-cleaning plants rarely need deadheading, blooming continuously from spring through frost. Bronze-leaved cultivars tolerate more direct sun than green-leaved types.

plant_note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify indoors and spring-sow after last frost.
directions: Sow in lean to average, well-drained soil in full sun; wild quinine is a sturdy native prairie plant that tolerates heat, drought, and clay once established. The flat-topped clusters of small white button flowers appear in midsummer and persist for many weeks, drawing in a wide diversity of native bees, wasps, and beetles. The button-like, persistent seed heads provide architectural interest into fall and winter. A reliable, long-lived plant that requires virtually no maintenance once established in a sunny meadow or native plant garden.

plant_note: Plant tubers in fall as soon as available — they dry out quickly; soak in water for 24 hours before planting.
directions: Soak the small, hard tubers in water for 24 hours before planting to rehydrate them; plant 2 in deep under deciduous trees or shrubs as soon as received in late summer or early fall. Bright yellow cup-shaped flowers surrounded by a green ruff appear in late winter, often alongside snowdrops, before any leaves emerge on overhead trees. Winter aconite self-seeds freely and slowly forms large, cheerful carpets if left undisturbed. Best planted where they can stay permanently; they strongly dislike being moved.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist mix without covering; they need light to germinate at 70–75°F. Torenia is one of the best flowering annuals for shaded and part-shaded spots in hot, humid climates where impatiens are lost to downy mildew. The bicolored, snapdragon-like flowers in purple, pink, white, or yellow bloom profusely without deadheading throughout summer. Keep soil consistently moist, as plants wilt quickly when dry but recover readily; fertilize monthly with a balanced liquid feed.

plant_note: Plant rhizomes in fall in humus-rich shade.
directions: Plant wood anemone rhizomes in fall just below the surface in cool, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade. The starry white spring flowers carpet the ground before deciduous trees leaf out, then the plant goes dormant by summer. It spreads slowly by creeping rhizomes to form a lovely ephemeral groundcover. Keep the soil moist during growth and mark the location, as the foliage disappears entirely in summer.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost; prefers to be direct sown as transplants disturb its taproot.
directions: Direct sow after last frost in full sun with lean, well-drained soil; xeranthemum is one of the easiest everlastings to grow and tolerates poor, dry conditions well. The papery, daisy-like flowers in shades of lavender, pink, purple, and white are naturally self-preserving and air-dry on the stem. Harvest stems for drying when flowers are about two-thirds open; hang upside down in a warm, airy location for 2–3 weeks.
