Cones take three years to mature from pollination to seed release, so a productive tree has three overlapping cone crops at any given time. Harvest brown, closed cones by hand or after they fall naturally in late autumn, then sun-dry or gently heat them until the scales open and release the papery-shelled nuts inside. The tree thrives in sandy, well-drained, alkaline to acidic soil and is extremely drought-tolerant once the taproot is established.

plant_note: Plant after all frost danger has passed; sugar apple requires a long, hot, humid growing season and is limited to frost-free zones.
directions: Sugar apple is a semi-deciduous small tree that drops leaves during the dry season — a natural dormancy that can be encouraged in cultivation by reducing water in late autumn to trigger uniform bloom flush in spring. Like other Annona species, the flowers are bisexual but dichogamous; hand-pollinate using a soft brush to transfer pollen from male-stage flowers (petals separating but pollen not yet shed) to female-stage flowers (stigma sticky and receptive). Fruit is composed of loosely packed segments that separate when ripe; harvest when skin segments begin to separate and the fruit yields to light pressure. Trees are fast-growing and benefit from annual pruning after fruit set to maintain canopy density and manageable size.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost; established plants recover from brief dips to 28°F but young plants need protection.
directions: Surinam cherry is a densely foliaged shrub or small tree commonly used as a formal or informal hedge in frost-free zones; it tolerates shearing well without sacrificing fruiting wood because it blooms on new growth. The fruits change color through green, orange, and red to very dark red (nearly black) — they are most flavorful when fully dark red, as earlier stages carry a pronounced resinous flavor. Self-fertile and prolific, it bears twice a year in suitable climates. It has naturalized invasively in Hawaii and parts of Florida; check local regulations before planting.

plant_note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring before buds swell.
directions: Sweet cherries require 700–1100 chill hours and are among the least self-fertile of the stone fruits — always plant at least two compatible varieties, choosing cultivars from the same incompatibility group (or self-fertile types such as Stella and Lapins). They prefer well-drained, slightly alkaline soil and dislike wet feet, which promotes root rot and crown rot. Protect ripening fruit with bird netting. Harvest when fruit is deeply colored, fully sweet, and the stem parts cleanly from the spur.

plant_note: Plant in spring in frost-free areas or grow in a container to overwinter indoors.
directions: Sweet lime yields mild, low-acid fruit prized in South Asia and the Mediterranean for fresh juice. Grow in full sun on well-drained soil, protecting it from any frost, or keep it in a large pot to shelter indoors in cold climates. Feed with a citrus fertilizer through the growing season and water deeply but infrequently. Harvest when the rind turns pale yellow-green and the fruit yields slightly to pressure.

plant_note: Plant in spring after all frost danger has passed; tamarind tolerates coastal conditions and is surprisingly drought-tolerant once established.
directions: Tamarind is a massive, long-lived tropical tree that can take 6–8 years from seed or 3–5 years from grafted stock to begin bearing the distinctive hard-shelled brown pods. It is self-fertile and thrives in hot, dry conditions; it is one of the most drought-tolerant tropical fruit trees. The interior pulp surrounding the seeds is intensely sour when young and sweet-sour when fully ripe; pods are harvested when the shell is brittle and the pulp is sticky, fibrous, and deep brown. Trees require no significant pruning and are largely pest-free in the continental U.S.; remove only dead or crossing wood.

plant_note: Plant in spring in frost-free citrus regions.
directions: The tangelo is a juicy, loose-skinned cross between a mandarin and a grapefruit or pomelo, often bearing a distinctive neck at the stem. Plant in full sun on well-drained soil in a frost-protected location, and note that some cultivars set better crops with a pollinizer nearby. Water deeply and feed with citrus fertilizer during the growing season. Harvest when the fruit is fully colored and heavy with juice, typically from midwinter onward.

plant_note: Plant in spring in frost-free areas.
directions: A tangor is a mandarin-sweet orange hybrid, combining easy peeling with rich, full orange flavor. Grow in full sun on well-drained soil and protect it from frost, which readily damages leaves and fruit. Apply a balanced citrus feed in spring and summer and mulch to conserve soil moisture. Harvest when fully colored and sweet, and prune lightly after cropping to remove crossing or weak wood.

plant_note: Plant in spring; exceptionally cold-hardy for a citrus relative.
directions: The hardy trifoliate orange is a deciduous citrus relative bearing small, aromatic, seedy fruit used for marmalade and flavoring. Remarkably cold-tolerant, it survives well below freezing and is widely used as a dwarfing rootstock for other citrus. Plant in full sun on well-drained soil; its dense, fiercely thorny growth makes an effective barrier hedge. Harvest the downy yellow fruit in autumn, using the tart juice and rind rather than eating it fresh.

plant_note: Plant in spring after frost risk passes; trees are damaged below 28°F but recover from brief cold if the trunk is protected.
directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; wax jambu tolerates brief flooding better than most tropical fruits and grows well near water features. Trees are self-fertile and fruit heavily along the branches — a single mature tree produces hundreds of bell-shaped, crisp, mildly sweet fruits per season. Fertilize three times per year with a balanced fertilizer; maintain a regular irrigation schedule during flowering and fruiting to prevent premature fruit drop. Harvest when fruits reach their full color (pink, red, or white depending on variety) and the waxy skin is firm and glossy; the spongy flesh has a very short shelf life and is best eaten within a day or two of picking.

plant_note: Plant in spring; extremely adaptable to poor soils.
directions: White mulberry is a fast-growing, exceptionally hardy tree bearing sweet, mild fruit and historically grown to feed silkworms. It tolerates drought, heat, and poor soils, thriving in full sun in a very wide range of conditions. Plant a fruiting cultivar rather than a fruitless ornamental, and site away from walks since the ripe fruit drops freely. Prune in winter to control size, and expect abundant crops within a few years.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost danger; established trees tolerate brief dips to 22°F and suit zone 9 in sheltered microclimates.
directions: White sapote is a fast-growing, drought-tolerant evergreen tree that bears creamy, custard-flavored fruit often compared to a blend of peach and vanilla. Grafted cultivars (Vernon, Suebelle, Cuccio) fruit in 3–5 years and produce more reliably than seedlings, which can take a decade or more and show highly variable fruit quality. Trees are self-fertile, though two cultivars nearby increase set. Harvest when fruit is light green to yellowish, yields slightly to palm pressure, and separates easily from the stem; it ripens to soft within 3–5 days at room temperature and has very short shelf life.

plant_note: Plant in spring in well-drained, even poor soils; wood apple is extremely drought-tolerant once established.
directions: Wood apple is a deciduous tree from South and Southeast Asia related to bael; it tolerates poor, rocky, alkaline soils and drought conditions that defeat most fruit trees, making it valuable for arid-zone agroforestry. The spherical, gray-shelled fruits have a hard woody shell that must be cracked open with a hammer; the dark, pungent, sticky flesh inside is strongly acidic and astringent and is usually mixed with sugar, coconut, and spices or blended into drinks rather than eaten plain. It is self-fertile and pollinated by insects; a single tree crops reliably after the first 5–7 years. Harvest when fallen fruit has fully ripened on the ground, or remove from the tree when the gray shell turns slightly yellowish and sounds hollow when struck.

plant_note: Plant grafted trees in spring; yuzu is among the hardiest citrus and can tolerate temperatures to 14–18°F when established, making it viable in zone 8 and possibly sheltered zone 7b.
directions: Yuzu is prized almost entirely for its intensely aromatic rind and highly acidic juice rather than its thin, seedy flesh; the fruit is rarely eaten out of hand but is indispensable in Japanese cuisine as a seasoning, in yuzu kosho paste, and in bath products. It is self-fertile and grafted trees begin to fruit in 3–4 years, though seedlings are notoriously slow — often taking 10 or more years. The tree has sharp thorns and is more cold-hardy than any lemon or standard lime. Harvest from late autumn through early winter when the fruit turns from green to yellow; green fruit has the most intense aroma for zesting.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings after last frost; this sterile hybrid does not set viable seed and must be propagated vegetatively.
directions: Take 4–6 in stem cuttings, remove lower leaves, and root in moist perlite or water; transplant once a vigorous root system develops. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun after all frost danger has passed; in zones below 10, treat as a long-season annual or overwinter rooted cuttings indoors under grow lights. Because this sterile hybrid never sets seed, it flowers continuously throughout the season — a great benefit for pollinators — without the bolting problem common to culinary basil. Harvest stem tips freely; the camphor-scented, dark-veined foliage is used in cooked dishes and infused oils.

plant_note: Cold-stratify seed for 4–6 weeks, then start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Cold-stratify seed in moist peat in the refrigerator for 4–6 weeks before surface-sowing onto warm starting mix; germination is slow and erratic without this treatment. Plant in well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; agrimony is native to dry meadows and roadsides and tolerates drought and lean soils well. Harvest the entire above-ground plant just as the lower flowers on the spike begin to open in early summer, cutting stems to leave at least 2 in of growth for regrowth. Dry gathered material in bundles for use in traditional astringent teas for sore throats, mild digestive complaints, and as a wound-cleansing wash.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost once soil has warmed; ajwain needs a long, warm season to set seed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in the garden in well-drained, fertile soil once soil temperature reaches 65°F; thin to 9–12 in. Ajwain closely resembles caraway and parsley in appearance but the tiny fruits (commonly called seeds) carry an intensely sharp thymol flavor — far stronger than thyme — used in Indian flatbreads, lentil dishes, and digestive preparations. Young leaves are also edible and make a flavorful addition to salads and chutneys. Harvest seed umbels when they turn tan and dry, cutting into bags to catch the seeds, and cure in a warm, airy location before storing in airtight jars.

plant_note: Sow stratified seed in autumn in a woodland bed; seeds require 18–24 months of warm-then-cold stratification before germinating.
directions: Purchase properly stratified seed and sow 1–2 in deep in a shaded, forested bed with well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil; do not expect germination until the following spring at the earliest. Ginseng requires 60–75% canopy shade and the deep, crumbly forest soil of its native Appalachian habitat — it will not thrive in open garden conditions. Plants must grow for a minimum of 5–7 years before roots reach marketable size, making patience the primary gardening virtue here. Harvest roots in autumn by carefully digging to preserve the branched root shape; roots are dried slowly at low heat and used in adaptogenic teas, tinctures, and traditional Chinese medicine preparations.

plant_note: Seed needs cold stratification.
directions: Sow seed in fall so winter cold provides the stratification needed to break dormancy. This woodland perennial forms a large clump valued for its aromatic roots used in traditional respiratory remedies. Plant in humus-rich, moist soil in dappled shade at the edge of a woodland garden. Be patient, as roots may take several years to reach harvestable size. Mulch generously each autumn and divide mature clumps to propagate.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed in autumn just before first frost; seed loses viability quickly and requires cold stratification through winter to germinate reliably in spring.
directions: Sow fresh seed immediately after harvest or purchase in autumn, pressing 1/4 in deep into moist soil; stored or old seed germinates poorly. Angelica is a short-lived perennial or biennial that requires deep, rich, moisture-retentive soil and partial shade — its ideal habitat is moist woodland edge. Removing the flower head in the second year prolongs the plant's life, while allowing it to set seed means the plant dies after seeding but self-sown seedlings will follow. Harvest young leaf stems and petioles in spring for candying; collect seeds when ripe for use in gin and liqueur flavoring.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost; anise dislikes transplanting and needs a long, warm season.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in their final location in rich, well-drained soil; anise develops a taproot that transplants poorly. Thin to 6–12 in apart once seedlings are established, and stake plants in exposed sites as they can be top-heavy when in flower. Anise needs a long, frost-free season to set seed—at least 120 days—so start as early as possible in cool climates. Harvest umbels when seeds begin to turn gray-green, cutting whole heads into paper bags to catch seeds as they ripen.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds prefer light for germination.
directions: Press tiny seeds onto the surface of moist starting mix without covering; keep warm at 70°F until sprouts appear. Plants are drought-tolerant once established and perform well in average to poor soil with good drainage. Harvest leaves throughout the growing season for anise-scented teas and culinary use; the edible flowers attract bees and butterflies prolifically. Allow some spikes to go to seed in autumn for natural self-sowing or collect seed for the following year.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings around the last frost date in spring; apple mint spreads vigorously by underground runners.
directions: Plant divisions in moist, moderately fertile soil and install a root barrier or grow in a sunken container to contain the vigorous spreading rhizomes. Apple mint is more heat- and drought-tolerant than peppermint and copes better with partial shade; the large, soft, woolly leaves have a mild, fruity mint fragrance with an apple note. Cut plants back by half after the first flush of growth to encourage dense, leafy regrowth and delay flowering. Use fresh leaves in fruit salads, jellies, and summer drinks; the soft texture makes it excellent for mint sauce and a gentler choice for culinary use than the more pungent peppermint.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger passes into a sunny, warm location.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix and keep consistently warm at 70–80°F until germination; transplant after last frost into sandy or loamy, well-drained soil. Ashwagandha thrives in hot, dry conditions similar to its native Indian range — avoid overwatering and boggy soil. The fleshy taproot is the primary medicinal part and is harvested after the plant's first autumn, when the small lantern-like berries begin to turn red. Wash, slice, and dry the roots for use in adaptogenic powders and decoctions; yields improve substantially if plants are grown through a full growing season.

plant_note: Propagate by division; spreads fast.
directions: Start banana mint from divisions or cuttings, as it will not reproduce true from seed. Its soft fuzzy leaves carry a sweet fruity aroma prized for desserts and beverages. Grow in consistently moist, fertile soil in dappled sun, keeping roots contained to stop rampant spreading. Water generously in hot weather and cut plants back hard mid-season to renew tender foliage. Lift and divide crowded clumps every couple of years.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant only after nights stay reliably above 50°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in a warm, moist seed-starting mix and keep soil above 65°F for reliable sprouting. Pinch out flower buds as soon as they appear to encourage bushy leaf production and delay bolting. Water at the base to avoid fungal spots, and feed lightly with a balanced fertilizer mid-season. Harvest by snipping stems just above a leaf node once plants have at least six true leaves.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or established transplants after last frost in spring.
directions: Take semi-ripe stem cuttings in summer, dip in rooting hormone, and insert into gritty propagating mix; expect rooting in 6–9 weeks. Plant in well-drained soil in a sheltered, sunny site; in zones 7 and colder, grow in containers that can be moved indoors before hard frost. Clip the canopy to shape annually and remove any reverted plain shoots from variegated forms. Harvest individual leaves as needed once the plant is established; leaves are most pungent when dried for 1–2 weeks.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or nursery transplants after last frost; this non-blooming cultivar is propagated vegetatively.
directions: Root 3–4 in semi-ripe cuttings in moist perlite in late summer; Berggarten sage rarely flowers and is almost exclusively vegetatively propagated. Plant in well-drained, lean soil in full sun; the exceptionally large, rounded, gray-green leaves make it one of the most productive and ornamental culinary sages. Because it does not bolt to flower, the plant concentrates energy into leaf production and remains more compact and tidy than the species. Harvest large leaves throughout the season, cutting back the plant by one-third in spring to remove any winter-killed wood and encourage fresh basal growth.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; cold stratification for 4 weeks improves germination.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks in the refrigerator before sowing 1/4 in deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade. Betony (Betonica officinalis, synonym Stachys betonica) has a long history in European herbal medicine as a nervine tonic and digestive herb; the leaves are dried for tea. Grow in partial shade with moisture-retentive soil; plants are long-lived once established and require little attention beyond dividing crowded clumps every 3–4 years. Harvest leaves and flowering tops in early summer when the rosy-pink flower spikes are just opening, then dry thoroughly in a well-ventilated space.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; as a cool-season annual, it can also be sown in late summer for an autumn harvest.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in well-drained, poor to moderately fertile soil in full sun; blessed thistle thrives in lean conditions and declines in rich, moist soil. As a cool-season annual it bolts in summer heat, so early spring or late summer sowing provides the best leaf harvest. Harvest the young leaves and leafy stems before the spiny flower heads fully open, wearing gloves to protect against the prickles. Dried aerial parts are used in traditional bitter digestive tinctures, teas for stimulating appetite, and historical preparations reputed to support lactation.

plant_note: Cool-season perennial green.
directions: Direct-sow in early spring in cool, moist soil, covering seed lightly and thinning to a hand's width. The striking red-veined leaves add tangy lemon flavor and ornamental color to salads. Grow in partial shade with steady moisture to keep leaves tender and slow bolting. Remove flower stalks to prolong leaf harvest and prevent aggressive self-seeding. Cut leaves young for the best texture and divide crowns every few years.

plant_note: Slow, double-dormant seed.
directions: Sow fresh seed in fall in a shaded woodland bed, expecting slow, erratic germination over one or two seasons. This native perennial has a long history in traditional medicine, though the berries are toxic if eaten. Provide rich, moist, humusy soil in full to partial shade beneath deciduous trees. Keep the site mulched with leaf litter and never let it dry out during summer. Harvest the roots only from well-established, multi-year clumps.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring; seeds benefit from cold stratification and light exposure, so surface-sow and leave outdoors over winter for spring germination.
directions: Surface-sow seeds and cold-stratify by sowing in fall or placing moistened seeds in the refrigerator for 30 days before spring sowing; do not cover, as seeds need light. Blue vervain is a North American native wetland herb used in traditional and folk herbalism as a nervine and bitter tonic; the aerial parts are harvested when flowering begins. Plant in moist, fertile soil in full sun to light shade; it thrives along stream edges and in rain gardens. Harvest aerial portions — leaves, stems, and flower spikes — in midsummer when the lower flowers on the spike have just opened, and dry for tincture or tea.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; cold-moist stratification for 4–6 weeks significantly improves germination of this native species.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in the refrigerator before surface-sowing onto warm starting mix; germination is erratic without this treatment. Blue vervain is a native North American wetland plant that thrives in consistently moist to wet soil along streams, pond margins, or rain gardens. Harvest aerial parts — leafy stems and the distinctive candelabra-like flower spikes — when in bloom through summer, tincing in alcohol for use as a traditional nervine for tension and anxiety. Plants self-seed freely in moist conditions and will naturalize into attractive wildlife-supporting stands over time.

plant_note: Surface-sow; needs light and cold to germinate.
directions: Surface-sow the fine seed in fall onto moist ground, pressing it in without covering so light and cold can trigger sprouting. Boneset is a native wetland perennial long used in folk medicine for fevers and colds. Plant it in consistently damp to wet soil in full sun to light shade, ideal for rain gardens. The white flat-topped flower clusters draw many pollinators in late summer. Harvest the leaves and flowering tops as blooms open and dry them thoroughly.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost date; borage dislikes root disturbance and self-seeds freely.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 in deep directly in their permanent position; borage has a taproot that is easily damaged and transplants poorly. Grow in full sun in any well-drained soil—even poor, dry soil suits it. Once established it self-seeds readily, so remove unwanted seedlings while they are small. The star-shaped blue flowers are edible with a cool cucumber flavor; harvest them when newly opened for garnishing salads and drinks.

plant_note: Direct sow around the last frost date; fennel dislikes transplanting.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in their final position; bronze fennel resents root disturbance and transplants poorly. Grow in full sun with well-drained, fertile soil and deadhead spent flower heads to limit self-seeding, which can become excessive. Plant away from dill, coriander, and most vegetables as fennel is allelopathic to many garden neighbors. Harvest the feathery bronze fronds as needed; flavor is best before flowering.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; buckler-leaf sorrel is one of the first perennial herbs to produce harvestable leaves in spring.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained soil with moderate fertility; thin to 12 in apart once seedlings reach 2 in. Buckler-leaf sorrel has small, shield-shaped silvery-green leaves with a clean, bright lemon-acid flavor that is preferred over common sorrel in French cuisine for sorrel soup and sauces. Remove flowering stalks as they appear to redirect energy into leaf production; divide clumps every 2–3 years to maintain vigor. Harvest the small leaves regularly while they are fresh and tender, as flavor intensifies in older foliage to the point of bitterness.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or divide established plants in spring; transplant after frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface-sow seeds on well-drained seed-starting mix and lightly press in; germination is steady within two to three weeks in warm conditions. Plant in lean, well-drained soil in full sun; calamint thrives in rocky or gravelly sites and is highly drought-tolerant once established. The small, aromatic leaves have a flavor that blends pennyroyal-mint with oregano, making it a traditional Italian seasoning for mushrooms and bean dishes. Cut back by half after the first flush of lavender flowers in midsummer to encourage a second bloom and fresh, tender growth for harvesting.

plant_note: Resents transplanting; sow in place.
directions: Direct-sow in early spring onto lean, sandy soil, as the taproot dislikes disturbance and transplanting. The whole plant is used in traditional herbalism as a mild sedative and sleep aid. Give it full sun and minimal water, since rich, moist ground produces weak, floppy growth. Thin seedlings to prevent crowding and let some flowers set seed for reliable self-sowing. Harvest the aerial parts when in bloom and dry them for tea or tincture.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; caraway is biennial and flowers in its second year.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in deep, loose, well-drained soil; caraway has a fleshy taproot and dislikes waterlogged conditions. As a biennial, it produces a rosette of ferny leaves in the first year, then flowers and sets seed in the second before dying. Thin to 6–9 in once seedlings are established and keep the bed weed-free. Harvest seed heads when they turn brown, cutting into bags to catch the aromatic seeds before they shatter.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date; crushing or nicking seed coat before sowing improves germination rates.
directions: Direct sow by pressing seeds lightly into moist soil; crushing the seed coat slightly before sowing can speed germination. Plants grown from seed attract cats less strongly than those that have been bruised, so direct-sown plants may establish more easily in gardens with resident cats. Cut back plants by half after the first flowering to encourage a second flush and prevent excessive seeding. Harvest aerial parts just before flowering for drying into cat toys and sachets; the nepetalactone content is highest at this stage, and the herb has mild calming properties for human tea use as well.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; light frost will not harm established seedlings.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds by pressing them onto moist soil without covering; they require light to germinate. Thin seedlings to 6–9 in apart in well-drained, moderately poor soil—rich soil encourages lush stems that flop easily. German chamomile self-seeds reliably and will naturalize in a sunny spot with minimal care. Harvest flower heads just as the white petals begin to reflex downward, which indicates peak essential oil content for tea.

plant_note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost in a shaded spot; again in late summer for a fall harvest.
directions: Scatter seeds on the soil surface and press them in lightly, or cover with 1/8 in of fine soil; chervil prefers cool, partly shaded conditions and bolts quickly in heat. Direct sow rather than transplanting, as the taproot is sensitive to disturbance. Keep the soil consistently moist during germination and throughout the cool season. Harvest delicate, fern-like leaves when plants are 6–8 in tall, before any flower stalks emerge, to capture the mild anise flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; for root harvest, sow by early summer so roots mature by autumn.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in deep, well-drained soil; chicory develops a long taproot that makes it drought-tolerant once established but resists transplanting. Young leaves provide a pleasantly bitter addition to salads and are most palatable before the plant flowers; blanching the developing crown under an upturned pot for 2–3 weeks produces creamy, milder forced leaves in late winter. The thick taproot is roasted and ground as a coffee substitute or additive, harvested in the plant's second autumn. Bright blue flowers open and close with the sun daily, attracting bees through summer.

plant_note: Grown for edible flower stems.
directions: Start seed indoors 8 weeks before last frost, sowing in clusters and keeping soil around 65F. This garlic chive selection is grown especially for its tender edible flower stalks used in stir-fries. Transplant clumps to full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and keep evenly watered. Harvest the round flower buds and stems just before they open for the mildest flavor. Divide the perennial clumps every few years and cut back foliage after frost.

plant_note: Direct sow or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; chives tolerate light frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in groups of 4–6 and thin clumps to 6–9 in apart once established; thinnings are edible. Water regularly and divide crowded clumps every 2–3 years in spring or fall to maintain vigor. Remove spent flower heads to prevent excessive self-seeding, or leave a few for their edible, mildly onion-flavored blooms. Harvest by snipping leaves to about 1 in above the soil; plants regrow quickly.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings around the last frost date; this cultivar is a sterile peppermint hybrid and must be propagated vegetatively to maintain its chocolate-mint character.
directions: Plant divisions in rich, consistently moist soil and confine the roots with a buried barrier or container, as this mint spreads aggressively like all peppermint cultivars. Provide consistent moisture and partial shade in hot climates to prevent the dark-stemmed foliage from scorching. Harvest stems just before flowers open for the most pronounced menthol-and-chocolate aroma; the leaves are prized for their distinctive scent in desserts, hot chocolate, and after-dinner teas. Cut plants back to 2–3 in above ground after each main harvest to stimulate a flush of fragrant new growth.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; succession-sow every 3 weeks through spring and again in early fall.
directions: Lightly crush the husks before sowing, then press seeds 1/4 in into the soil and keep evenly moist until germination. Thin seedlings to 6 in apart so plants don't compete for moisture, which slows bolting. Avoid transplanting; cilantro's taproot resents disturbance. Begin harvesting outer leaves once plants reach 4–6 in, and harvest seed heads (coriander) when they turn tan and dry on the stem.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant only after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in fertile, moist seed-starting mix held above 65°F, and transplant outdoors once night temperatures stay above 50°F. Cinnamon basil has reddish stems and pink-purple flowers; it contains methyl cinnamate, which gives the foliage a distinct warm spice note alongside the usual basil flavor. Pinch out flower stalks as they appear to maintain leaf production, and harvest by cutting stems back to a set of lower leaves. Use fresh or dried in fruit dishes, desserts, teas, and anywhere a subtle cinnamon note complements the basil flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; as a biennial, plants flower and produce oil-rich bracts in their second summer.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained, alkaline or neutral soil in full sun; germination is quick and reliable. In the first year the plant forms a large rosette of bold, wrinkled leaves; in the second year it sends up a dramatic 4–5 ft flower spike laden with showy pink-purple and white bracts. Harvest the flowering tops just as the flowers begin to open — the essential oil sclareol is concentrated in the sticky glands of the bracts and calyces. Dry the cut stems in bundles or steam-distill for essential oil used in perfumery, aromatherapy, and as a fixative in soaps and cosmetics.

plant_note: Warmth-loving; frost kills it.
directions: Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, surface-pressing it and keeping flats near 78F. This African and Asian shrubby basil carries a warm clove-like scent used medicinally and in cooking. Transplant to warm, fertile soil in full sun after frost, giving each plant ample room to bush out. Pinch flowering tips to prolong leaf production and improve branching. In cool climates grow it as a container plant that can overwinter indoors.

plant_note: Plant root cuttings or crown divisions in early spring; even small root fragments will regenerate into new plants so choose the planting site carefully.
directions: Propagate from 2–4 in root cuttings planted horizontally 2–3 in deep; plants grow vigorously and are nearly impossible to eradicate once established, so site them permanently. Mulch heavily around plants and cut back to the ground two or three times per growing season to harvest the biomass-rich leaves as a liquid fertilizer or compost activator. Leaves can also be processed into poultice form for topical soothing use on bruises and minor sprains. Note that internal use is not recommended due to pyrrolizidine alkaloid content.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring or summer; biennial plants form a rosette the first year and flower the second year.
directions: Scatter tiny seeds on bare, disturbed soil and do not cover — they need light to germinate; thin to 18–24 in apart once seedlings are established. As a biennial, mullein forms a large, velvety rosette of leaves in the first year before sending up a tall flower spike in its second season. First-year leaves are harvested and dried for use in respiratory-support teas and smoking blends; flowers are gathered as they open along the stalk, often infused into olive oil for ear-drop preparations. Allow the towering flower spike to set seed for reliable self-sowing in subsequent years.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed as young plants are more cold-sensitive than mature ones.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; germination is reliable at 65–70°F. Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil and prune plants hard in spring to remove winter-killed tips and promote bushy growth from the base. Harvest leafy stems before flowering for culinary and medicinal use; the volatile oils are most concentrated on sunny mornings before flowering. Dried sage leaves and bundles are used in cooking, traditional salubrious teas, and as smudge sticks in ceremonial contexts.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow or barely cover seed as light aids germination.
directions: Surface-sow seeds or cover to 1/8 in in warm, well-drained starting mix; seedlings are small and slow to establish so start early indoors. Plant in sharply drained, lean soil in full sun — like other Mediterranean herbs, thyme deteriorates in fertile, moist conditions. Trim plants lightly after each harvest to prevent woodiness; replace plants every 3–4 years when the center becomes unproductive. Harvest leafy stem tips before or during flowering for the highest thymol content, using fresh or dried in culinary preparations and traditional antiseptic and cough-relief remedies.

plant_note: Divide or transplant rooted mats around the last frost date; Corsican mint is the smallest mint and prefers cool, moist conditions.
directions: Plant small divisions in consistently moist, humus-rich soil in a partially shaded spot; Corsican mint forms a moss-like carpet of tiny peppermint-scented leaves just 1–3 in tall and is ideal between stepping stones or at the edge of a cool, moist path. Unlike most mints, it dislikes hot, dry conditions and should never be allowed to dry out completely. The plant releases a powerful peppermint fragrance when lightly stepped on, making it a sensory ground cover. In zones 6–7 it may die back in hard winters but typically returns from the root; mulch lightly in late autumn to protect the shallow root system.

plant_note: Divide established root clumps in early spring around the last frost date; seed germination is slow and unreliable so division is the standard propagation method.
directions: Divide the clumping rhizome in early spring, replanting sections 2–3 in deep in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; costmary tolerates partial shade but becomes straggly and produces fewer of the large, minty-balsamic leaves. The broad, silver-green leaves were historically placed in Bibles and hymnals as bookmarks because the pleasant fragrance helped keep the reader awake during long services — hence the common name. Harvest large, mature leaves at any point during the growing season for use as a herb in ales and meat dishes, for sachets in linen, or as a flavoring in salads and teas. Cut plants back hard after flowering to rejuvenate the foliage and prevent excessive spread.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or divide established plants in spring; transplant after frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on well-drained seed-starting mix and press in without covering; germination is slow and irregular over 2–4 weeks. Plant in dry, gritty soil in full sun; creeping thyme thrives in rock gardens, between stepping stones, and on sunny slopes where it forms a fragrant, flowering mat. Avoid heavy clay and excessive moisture, which rot the shallow roots quickly. Harvest sprigs as needed for cooking — the flavor is milder than upright thyme but usable; the primary garden value is as a low, aromatic, bee-friendly ground cover.

plant_note: Root cuttings in water or moist mix and transplant after frost; in zone 9–11 plants overwinter but may die back to roots in zone 9 hard freezes.
directions: Propagate from stem cuttings 4–6 in long; cuttings root in 1–2 weeks in moist perlite or a glass of water and are far more reliable than seed. Plant in any well-drained soil in full sun to bright partial shade; Cuban oregano tolerates heat, humidity, and dry spells far better than Mediterranean oregano. The thick, succulent leaves have a pungent, oregano-like aroma intensified by thyme — use fresh in Caribbean, Indian, and Southeast Asian cooking. Remove flower spikes to keep the plant in vigorous vegetative growth; take a few cuttings before the first frost if growing as an annual.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost in a warm, partially shaded site; culantro prefers high heat and humidity.
directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface and press in lightly; germination can be slow and erratic, so sow generously and keep soil consistently moist and warm. Culantro thrives in partial shade and high humidity, performing well in spots too hot and bright for cilantro; its strap-like, serrated leaves have a much more intense cilantro flavor and hold up better in cooking. Thin seedlings to 9–12 in apart and keep soil moderately moist; the plant bolts to flower in dry or cool conditions. Harvest individual outer leaves at any time; one or two leaves substitute for a much larger amount of cilantro in sofrito, chutneys, and Caribbean stews.

plant_note: Surface-sow; needs cold and light.
directions: Surface-sow the dust-fine seed in fall, leaving it uncovered so cold and light can trigger germination. This tall native perennial was used by Indigenous peoples and early settlers as a liver remedy. Grow it in moist, fertile soil in full sun to light shade where the white flower spires shine in summer. Keep the roots evenly moist, as dry soil causes lower leaves to scorch. Harvest roots from established plants in autumn and dry for at least a year before use.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost and transplant carefully after soil warms; cumin needs a long hot season.
directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost in deep cells to minimize taproot disturbance, sowing 1/4 in deep and keeping soil at 70°F or above. Transplant with great care, disturbing roots as little as possible, into sandy or well-drained soil in full sun. Cumin thrives in hot, dry conditions and performs poorly in cool, wet summers; mulch to retain ground warmth. Harvest umbels when seeds turn brown, drying the cut heads in a warm, airy spot before threshing out the pungent seeds.

plant_note: Plant container-grown stock after last frost; in zone 9 protect with frost cloth during freezes — the woody stems can survive brief dips to 28°F once established.
directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; curry leaf shrubs grow slowly at first but accelerate once established in a warm zone 9–11 site. Fresh leaves are essential to South Indian cooking and release their aroma only when heated in oil — dried leaves are a poor substitute, so growing your own is worthwhile. Harvest by snipping young branch tips regularly, which encourages dense new growth; remove flower clusters as they appear if you prefer to maximize leaf production. Mulch heavily through zone 9 winters and wrap the trunk if temperatures below 28°F are expected for more than a night or two.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings after last frost; easier to propagate from cuttings than from slow and variable seed.
directions: Take 3–4 in softwood cuttings in late spring, strip lower leaves, and insert into sharp-draining propagating mix; roots develop in 3–4 weeks. The silver-needle foliage has a striking curry-like aroma but this herb is primarily used for its fragrance rather than as a true culinary spice — a leaf or two can be added to braises or soups but should be removed before serving. Plant in sharply drained, lean soil in full sun; it is native to Mediterranean coastal cliffs and thrives under hot, dry conditions. Trim lightly after the yellow button flowers fade to keep the plant tidy; in zone 7 mulch over winter or take cuttings as insurance against hard freezes.

plant_note: Grown for leaves, not stalks.
directions: Surface-sow indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost, keeping the fine seed moist and lightly covered. This leafy celery is grown for its strongly flavored foliage rather than thick stalks. Transplant to rich, consistently moist soil in full sun to partial shade and never let it dry out. Harvest outer stems and leaves as needed, and the plant will keep producing all season. In mild winters it survives to provide an early spring cut, and it stores well dried.

plant_note: Root cuttings in late spring and transplant after all frost danger has passed; damiana is a subtropical shrub that needs consistent warmth to establish.
directions: Take 3–4 in stem cuttings in spring or summer and root in gritty, barely moist medium; damiana is easier and faster from cuttings than from the slow-germinating seed. Plant in sharply drained, sandy or rocky, poor soil in full sun — damiana is native to the dry shrublands of Mexico and the Caribbean and rots in moist, fertile conditions. In zones below 9, grow in containers and overwinter in a frost-free, bright location, watering sparingly. Harvest leafy stems and the small yellow flowers throughout the growing season, drying them for use in traditional Mexican tonic teas and liqueur preparations.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable; also sow in late summer for autumn and early spring harvest.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in deep, loose, well-drained soil; cultivated broad-leafed varieties produce larger, more tender leaves than typical lawn weeds. Blanch developing rosettes under a cover for 1–2 weeks in spring to produce milder, less bitter leaves for salads. All plant parts are useful: young leaves are eaten raw or cooked, flowers are made into wine or fritters, and the taproot is roasted as a coffee substitute or used fresh in bitter-tonic preparations. Harvest roots in autumn of the second year when they are largest and most concentrated in the liver-supportive compound taraxacin.

plant_note: Purple foliage; ornamental and edible.
directions: Sow indoors 4-6 weeks ahead of the last frost and keep flats warm and bright to preserve rich color. This deep-purple basil adds dramatic color to vinegars, salads, and garnishes. Transplant after frost into full sun, where strong light intensifies the burgundy pigment. Pinch regularly to build a dense plant and prevent early flowering. Harvest young leaves for the most vivid color and infuse them into oils or vinegars.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; succession-sow every 2–3 weeks for a continuous leaf harvest.
directions: Broadcast seeds thinly over a weed-free bed and cover with 1/8 in of soil, firming gently; dill needs light to germinate well. Avoid transplanting—the long taproot is easily damaged. Stake tall plants if the site is windy, and keep irrigation steady to delay the rush to flower. Snip feathery fronds once the plant has at least five leaf sets; for seed, allow umbels to go fully brown before cutting.

plant_note: Set out only after nights stay above 50F.
directions: Start seed indoors 8 weeks before last frost in warm, moist flats and keep soil at 75-85F for reliable sprouting. Transplant into rich, well-drained soil once nights stay above 50F, spacing clumps well apart to allow fanning growth. Water consistently through the season and feed monthly with a nitrogen-rich fertilizer for lush stalks. In zones colder than 9, dig and pot up a division to overwinter indoors in a bright window. Harvest by twisting off outer stalks at the base once they reach pencil thickness.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; transplant after frost danger passes.
directions: Sow seed on the surface or just 1/8 in deep; cold stratification for 4–8 weeks significantly improves germination rates, especially for fresh seed. Plant in well-drained soil and avoid overwatering once established, as echinacea tolerates drought well. Roots are the primary medicinal part and are harvested in fall of the third or fourth year; flowers and leaves can be harvested earlier for teas and tinctures. Allow some seed heads to remain for self-seeding and to feed overwintering birds.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; a brief cold stratification of 2–3 weeks can improve germination.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep indoors after a brief cold stratification period; plants grow into impressive clumps by their second year, eventually reaching 6–8 feet tall. Plant in moist, deep, fertile soil as this large plant develops an extensive root system that benefits from ample space. Harvest the thick, inulin-rich roots in autumn of the second or third year, washing and slicing them before drying for respiratory-supportive preparations. The yellow daisy-like flowers appear in midsummer of the second year and are attractive to pollinators.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has fully passed into well-drained, alkaline soil.
directions: Surface-sow seeds and keep barely moist; germination is slow and uneven, so starting from cuttings or divisions is often more reliable. Plant in fast-draining, lean soil — rich or wet conditions promote rot and weak growth. Prune plants back by about one-third after the first flush of blooms to keep growth compact and encourage a second flowering. Harvest flower spikes just as the buds begin to open, bundling and hanging upside down in a warm, airy space to dry for sachets, culinary use, and aromatherapy.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost once soil has warmed; epazote self-seeds prolifically once established.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds by pressing them gently into soil without covering, as they require light for germination; thin to 12–18 in. Epazote is remarkably drought-tolerant once established and thrives in lean, well-drained soil; rich, moist conditions encourage excessive growth. Deadhead regularly to control self-seeding, which can be invasive in warm climates. Harvest young stem tips throughout the season; the pungent, resinous flavor mellows somewhat with cooking.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; can also sow in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Soak seeds overnight to speed germination, then sow 1/2 in deep in rows and thin to 6 in apart; fenugreek prefers cool conditions and will bolt quickly in summer heat. Grow in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Days to maturity listed is for young leaf harvest; allow 3–5 months for seed pods to develop and dry if growing for seed spice. Harvest leaves at any time for use as a slightly bitter herb, and collect seed pods once they turn tan and feel firm.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; press seeds onto the surface of the mix as they need light to germinate.
directions: Seeds are tiny and require light for germination — press them gently onto moist starting mix without covering. Once established, plants are quite drought-tolerant and spread readily by self-seeding. Shear plants back by half after flowering to maintain a tidy mound and encourage fresh foliage. The leaves are the medicinal part; harvest fresh leaves for tea or tincture — wear gloves as some people experience skin irritation from the foliage.

plant_note: Cold-sensitive; wait for warm soil.
directions: Sow indoors 4-6 weeks before the last frost, barely covering the seed and keeping flats around 75F. This tiny-leaved Italian basil naturally forms a dense round bush without much pinching. Transplant after all danger of frost into warm, fertile soil in full sun. Water evenly and harvest by clipping whole stems just above a leaf pair to keep growth bushy. Remove flower buds promptly to extend the productive leafy season.

plant_note: Vigorous spreader; contain in beds or pots.
directions: Plant rooted divisions in moist to boggy soil in spring, ideally in a sunken container to curb its aggressive rhizomes. This Southeast Asian herb carries a distinctive tangy, fishy-citrus aroma used raw in Vietnamese and Chinese cooking. It thrives in partial shade with constant moisture and even tolerates shallow standing water. Cut back runaway runners to keep it from overtaking a bed. Harvest young leaves and tender shoots through the growing season, and mulch crowns in colder zones.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; sorrel is one of the first herbs to emerge in spring.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; thin to 12 in apart once seedlings are 2 in tall. Remove flower stalks as soon as they appear to prevent the plant from diverting energy away from leaf production. Divide established clumps every 2–3 years to maintain vigor and refresh the planting. Harvest the shield-shaped leaves regularly while they are young and tender; the bright, lemony oxalic flavor becomes more pronounced in larger, older leaves.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings around last frost date; French tarragon does not come true from seed.
directions: Always source French tarragon as a division or cutting—seeds sold as tarragon are typically the flavorless Russian type. Plant in lean, well-drained soil; overly rich soil encourages lush but bland foliage. Divide plants every 2–3 years in spring to maintain flavor and vigor, discarding old woody centers. Begin harvesting once shoots reach 6 in; the anise-like fragrance intensifies just before the plant's inconspicuous flowers open.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions after last frost once soil has thoroughly warmed to at least 70°F; galangal needs extended warm conditions.
directions: Plant rhizome sections with at least one growing bud 2–4 in deep in rich, moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil in partial shade; galangal needs consistent moisture and high humidity to thrive. Unlike ginger, galangal has a distinctly peppery, piney, medicinal flavor with less sweetness, and is a foundational ingredient in Thai, Indonesian, and Malay cuisines. In zones below 9, grow in large containers that can be moved indoors before frost; the rhizomes are frost-sensitive but the plant can be stored dormant above 50°F in winter. Harvest rhizomes after 10–12 months when the foliage begins to yellow in fall, digging with care to take only what is needed while leaving part of the clump to regrow.

plant_note: Fast; succession-sow every two weeks.
directions: Direct-sow thickly in cool weather, barely covering the seed, which sprouts within days. This peppery quick crop is harvested young as a spicy microgreen or baby salad green. Sow in succession every couple of weeks and keep the soil evenly moist to slow bolting. Provide partial shade in warm spells to extend the harvest window. Snip seedlings with scissors just above the soil once they reach a few inches tall.

plant_note: Sow directly 4–6 weeks before last frost or transplant established divisions in spring.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep and keep soil moist; germination is similar to common chives but the flat, grass-like leaves have a distinct mild garlic flavor. Grow in well-drained, fertile soil and divide clumps every 2–3 years to prevent overcrowding and maintain productivity. Deadhead flowers promptly, as garlic chives self-seed prolifically and can become weedy. Harvest flat leaves at any height; older leaves become slightly tough but remain useful cooked.

plant_note: Classic pesto basil.
directions: Start seed indoors 4-6 weeks before last frost, covering lightly and keeping soil near 75F. This large-leaved Italian classic is the standard for pesto and Mediterranean cooking. Transplant into warm, rich soil in full sun only after all frost danger passes. Pinch out the growing tips at six leaves to force a bushy, high-yielding plant. Harvest whole stems above a leaf node and remove flower buds promptly to keep leaves tender and sweet.

plant_note: Plant rhizome divisions in early autumn in a shaded woodland bed; seeds require prolonged double dormancy and are best left to experienced growers.
directions: Plant small rhizome pieces 1–2 in deep in autumn in deep, shaded woodland soil rich in leaf mold; rhizome division is far more reliable than growing from seed. Goldenseal requires 70–80% shade and the moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic conditions of mature eastern North American forests — raised beds under deciduous trees work well. This is a slow-growing, at-risk native species; growing your own reduces pressure on wild populations, but expect 4–6 years before roots are substantial enough to harvest. Harvest small portions of the golden rhizome in autumn of the fourth or fifth year, leaving the majority of the root system intact for continued growth.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring; seeds may need cold stratification for best germination.
directions: Cold-stratify seeds in the refrigerator for 2–4 weeks before sowing to improve germination rates; sow 1/4 in deep in moisture-retentive, fertile soil. This old European cottage garden vegetable-herb was once widely grown for its young shoots (used like asparagus), leaves (used like spinach), and flower buds (used like broccoli). Grow in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade; plants are long-lived perennials once established and require little care. Harvest young shoots in spring, leaves throughout the season, and flower buds before they open; do not harvest heavily in the first year to allow the plant to establish.

plant_note: Transplant rooted runners after all frost danger has passed into a warm, moist location; propagation from stolons is far more reliable than seed.
directions: Propagate from stolons (creeping stems with nodes), which root readily in moist potting mix or at the edges of ponds; plant in rich, consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade, as gotu kola thrives in the margins of water bodies in its native tropical Asian habitat. In zones below 9, grow in containers that can be overwintered on a warm, bright windowsill with consistently moist soil. Harvest the small, fan-shaped leaves regularly once the plant is established; the fresh leaves are eaten in salads in Sri Lankan cuisine and used in Ayurvedic and traditional Chinese medicine as a cognitive tonic and wound-healing herb. Harvest leaf material by snipping stems without uprooting the plant to allow continued production.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after nights are reliably above 50°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm, moist seed-starting mix and thin seedlings to avoid crowding once they have two true leaves. Greek basil forms a naturally compact, globe-shaped mound with tiny, intensely aromatic leaves — no pinching required to maintain shape, though removing flower buds prolongs leaf harvest. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; the petite habit makes it an excellent edging plant or container herb. Harvest by snipping small stem tips; the high surface-area-to-volume ratio of the small leaves delivers concentrated flavor.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes into very well-drained, rocky or sandy soil.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained, alkaline starting mix and keep warm at 68–72°F until germination; use gritty, mineral-rich mix to mimic its rocky Mediterranean mountain habitat. Plant out in the poorest, most well-drained spot in the garden — excess moisture and rich soil are the primary causes of failure. The silver-felted stems and leaves are harvested in full bloom in summer when the yellow flowers are visible; cut entire stems and hang in bundles to dry in a warm, airy space. Brew the whole dried stems as an herbal tea by simmering 5–10 minutes; the resulting infusion has a mild, honey-and-herb flavor and is traditionally consumed throughout Greece for immune support and as an everyday tonic.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface-sow seeds by pressing them into barely moist, well-drained seed-starting mix without covering, as they need light to germinate. Greek oregano (also called Italian or true oregano) is the pungent, white-flowered subspecies prized in Mediterranean cooking; it has far stronger flavor than common Origanum vulgare. Plant in lean, gritty, alkaline-leaning soil in full sun — excessive fertility dilutes the essential oils that give it its distinctive bite. Cut plants back by one-third after flowering each year to keep them compact; harvest just as buds begin to open for peak aroma.

plant_note: Plant divisions or rooted suckers after last frost; in zones below 8 grow in large containers and overwinter indoors.
directions: Divide established clumps in spring by separating rooted suckers from the base of the mother plant and replanting in rich, moist soil. Hoja santa grows rapidly in warm, humid conditions and prefers partial shade with consistent moisture; the enormous heart-shaped leaves can reach 12 in wide and emit a distinctive anise–root beer aroma from their essential oils. In Mexican cuisine the leaves are used to wrap tamales, season mole verde, and envelop fish for grilling. In zones 8–9, plants die to the ground after frost but reliably regrow from the roots; mulch heavily to protect the root zone through winter.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; do not transplant until nights are reliably above 55°F as cold soil stunts growth.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix at 75–85°F; germination is swift when soil is adequately warm. Transplant after night temperatures stay above 55°F and pinch growing tips immediately to promote bushy growth. Regular pinching and removal of flower spikes prolongs the leaf harvest and delays bitterness; harvest leaves in the morning after dew has dried for the most aromatic result. Fresh or dried leaves are used in adaptogenic teas, Ayurvedic preparations, and as a sacred plant in Hindu tradition.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow as seeds are small and benefit from light for germination.
directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix 6–8 weeks before transplanting; horehound germinates readily in warm soil but can be slow and erratic. Plant in dry, well-drained, even rocky or sandy soil where most plants would struggle — fertile, moist conditions cause leggy, weak growth. Harvest stems and leaves before flowering for the most potent bitterness, bundling for drying or infusing fresh into candy and cough syrups. Shear plants back in late summer to keep growth compact and prevent excessive self-seeding.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost once soil is warm; can also be started indoors 4 weeks early for a longer season.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained soil after last frost; thin to 18–24 in apart as huacatay grows into a tall, upright plant. This Andean herb — essential in Peruvian cuisine for dishes like ocopa sauce and anticuchos marinades — has a complex flavor combining mint, tarragon, and citrus. Plants are day-length sensitive and flower in late summer to fall; harvest leaves and stem tips throughout the vegetative season before flowering commences. Grow in full sun with moderate fertility; the plant is drought-tolerant once established and spreads by self-seeding in warm climates.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow or cover lightly with 1/8 in of fine mix.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep indoors in warm starting mix; germination is fast and reliable at 65–70°F. Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil — hyssop is drought-tolerant once established and thrives in conditions similar to lavender. Prune plants back by about one-third after flowering to keep them from becoming woody and to promote a compact, bushy habit. Harvest leafy stems and flowers in the morning just as the blossoms begin to open, drying them for use in expectorant teas, culinary herb blends, and potpourri.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed as young plants are cold-sensitive.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm, moist starting mix and provide a trellis or support on which the vigorous twining vine can climb; jiaogulan grows 10–20 ft if given the right conditions. Plant in moderately fertile, well-drained soil with some afternoon shade in hot climates; consistent moisture promotes the most lush and harvestable leaf growth. In zones 7–8 the plant dies to the ground in winter but regrows from the root; in zone 9 and above it may be evergreen. Harvest the five-lobed leaflets throughout the growing season for fresh or dried tea; known as southern ginseng, the leaves contain saponins similar to those in Panax ginseng and are used in adaptogenic preparations.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; kalonji prefers cool spring conditions and dislikes heat.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly where they are to grow; kalonji has fine, thread-like foliage similar to its ornamental cousin love-in-a-mist and dislikes transplanting. Grow in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; the plant bolts quickly in summer heat, so time sowing for a cool-season harvest. Harvest the swollen seed pods when they begin to turn tan and paper-like, cutting whole stems into bags before the pods split and release the seeds. The intensely aromatic black seeds are used in South Asian cooking on naan breads and in spice blends, and have a long history of medicinal use; they are not to be confused with black cumin (Bunium persicum).

plant_note: Surface-sow; light aids germination.
directions: Surface-sow indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, pressing seed onto the mix without covering it. This East Asian herb has minty-anise leaves used in Korean cooking and herbal teas. Transplant to full sun in well-drained soil, where purple flower spikes draw abundant bees. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and allow a few seed heads to ripen for self-sowing. Harvest young leaves through summer and cut plants back in fall.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold stratification; or sow fresh seed outdoors in autumn for natural stratification and spring germination.
directions: Cold-stratify seed in the refrigerator for 3–4 weeks before surface-sowing; the tiny seeds need cool temperatures and moderate moisture to germinate. Plant in humus-rich, well-drained but moisture-retentive soil; lady's mantle tolerates full sun in cooler climates but benefits from afternoon shade in hot regions. The pleated, velvety leaves catch dewdrops and rainwater, creating sparkling spheres of water — a notable ornamental feature. Harvest leaves and the chartreuse flower sprays in early summer for use in traditional women's tonics; the foliage and flowers also add texture to fresh-cut and dried flower arrangements.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after the last frost date.
directions: Surface-sow seeds and press lightly into moist mix without covering; they need light and warmth to sprout. Plant in well-drained soil in partial shade, particularly in hot climates, to maintain fresh-looking, non-scorched foliage. Cut plants back by half after the first flush of growth and again after any flowering to encourage compact, leafy regrowth. Harvest leaves before the plant flowers for the strongest lemon fragrance, picking in the morning after dew has dried.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant only after nights are reliably above 50°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm seed-starting mix at 70°F or above; germination is rapid once soil temperature is adequate. Transplant into rich, well-drained garden soil in full sun and pinch the growing tip early to encourage a bushy, multi-stemmed plant. Lemon basil is compact compared to sweet basil and has a distinct citrus fragrance from the presence of citral; it pairs well with fish, poultry, and Southeast Asian cuisines. Harvest by snipping stems just above a leaf pair once plants are well established, and pinch flower buds promptly to extend the harvest season.

plant_note: Direct sow or start indoors 4 weeks before last frost; lemon catnip self-seeds freely once established.
directions: Sow seeds on the surface of well-drained starting mix and press in lightly; fresh seeds germinate readily in 1–2 weeks. Lemon catnip has all the nepetalactone chemistry of common catnip (making it equally irresistible to cats) but its foliage carries a pleasant lemon-mint scent suitable for tea and aromatherapy. Grow in full sun in any well-drained soil; cut plants back by half after the first flush of flowers to encourage a second bloom and fresh aromatic foliage. Harvest stems before flowers fully open for the best flavor in herbal teas; dry quickly in a warm, airy spot and store in airtight containers.

plant_note: Root stem cuttings in late spring; named cultivars do not come true from seed, so vegetative propagation is necessary to preserve the lemon character.
directions: Take 3–4 in tip cuttings in late spring or early summer, remove lower leaves, and insert into gritty, free-draining mix; roots develop in 2–3 weeks. Plant in full sun in sharply drained, lean, slightly alkaline soil, as lemon thyme rots in wet or rich conditions just like common thyme. Shear lightly after flowering each season to prevent woodiness and maintain a tidy mound of fragrant foliage. Harvest leafy sprigs at any time; the lemon-thyme combination flavor is prized in fish dishes, salad dressings, and desserts, and is excellent fresh or dried.

plant_note: Transplant rooted cuttings outdoors after all frost danger has passed; bring containers indoors before the first autumn frost.
directions: Propagate from softwood cuttings taken in summer rather than from seed, which has poor viability; root cuttings in moist perlite under plastic until rooted. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil; in zones below 8 grow in containers that can be overwintered indoors in a cool, bright spot. Prune hard in spring when new growth emerges from what may look like dead wood — lemon verbena is often a slow riser after dormancy. Harvest leaves any time for intensely lemon-scented tea, dessert flavoring, and potpourri; fragrance peaks just before the plant begins to flower.

plant_note: Transplant divisions or rooted stalks after last frost when soil is reliably warm.
directions: Propagate by placing fresh grocery-store stalks (with roots intact) in water until roots develop, then transplant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun. Lemongrass needs heat and consistent moisture; water deeply and regularly during the growing season. In zones below 10, grow in large containers and bring indoors before first frost, cutting back to 6 in tall. Harvest outer stalks by cutting or twisting at the base when they reach pencil thickness; use the pale, tender base for cooking.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after scarifying or soaking seed overnight; transplant carefully after frost danger passes to avoid disturbing roots.
directions: Scarify seed coat lightly with sandpaper or soak overnight before sowing 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; germination improves significantly with this treatment. Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun — licorice develops an extensive, deep root system and resents being moved. Roots are ready to harvest in the third or fourth autumn when they are at least pencil-thick, digging carefully to extract as much of the lateral root run as possible. The dried root is used medicinally as a demulcent and anti-inflammatory for digestive and respiratory conditions; note that excessive consumption raises blood pressure.

plant_note: Chartreuse foliage; light-dependent seed.
directions: Surface-sow indoors 8 weeks before last frost, leaving seed uncovered since light aids germination. This golden-leaved anise hyssop offers licorice-scented foliage for teas and blue flower spikes for pollinators. Transplant to full sun in well-drained soil, spacing to allow good airflow. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering into fall and prevent excessive self-seeding. Harvest leaves and flowers fresh or dried and cut plants back after frost.

plant_note: Direct-sow into warm soil after frost.
directions: Direct-sow after the soil warms past 65F, scattering seed thinly and thinning to a hand's width apart. The bright citrus scent makes this basil superb in Southeast Asian dishes and cold drinks. Give it full sun and steady moisture in rich, free-draining soil for the best oil content. Pinch growing tips early to build a bushy plant and delay bolting. Harvest leaves in the morning when the fragrant oils are most concentrated.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring, 2–4 weeks before last frost; fresh seed germinates best.
directions: Sow fresh seed 1/4 in deep in moist, fertile soil in early spring; stored seed loses viability quickly. Give lovage a permanent spot as it develops a large taproot that dislikes disturbance, and provide deep, moisture-retentive, fertile soil. Remove flower stalks as they appear to keep the plant producing leafy growth for longer. Harvest young outer leaves and stems throughout the season; the intense celery-like flavor means a little goes a long way.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds without covering, pressing them gently into moist, well-drained seed mix and placing in a warm, bright spot. Transplant into well-drained, slightly alkaline garden soil after nights are reliably above 50°F. Pinch out growing tips regularly to keep plants bushy and delay flowering. Harvest stems just as the knotted flower buds form for the sweetest, most aromatic leaves.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; scarifying the seed coat lightly with sandpaper or soaking overnight in warm water speeds germination.
directions: Scarify or soak seed overnight before sowing 1/4 in deep; marshmallow appreciates consistent moisture, particularly during its establishment period. Plant in deep, moderately moist soil as the tap root grows substantial in mature plants. Harvest leaves and flowers in summer for use in soothing teas and poultices; the fleshy root is the most mucilaginous part and is best dug in the plant's second or third autumn. The dried root can be sliced, powdered, or made into a cold-water infusion to extract its slippery mucilage for throat-coat and digestive preparations.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after 4–6 weeks of cold stratification; transplant to a moist site after frost danger passes.
directions: Cold-stratify seed in moist medium in the refrigerator for 4–6 weeks before surface-sowing; plant out in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade or full sun where soil never dries out, mimicking its native pond-margin habitat. Meadowsweet bears frothy, sweetly almond-scented flower plumes in midsummer that were historically used to flavor mead and wines. Harvest flowers and upper leaves just as the buds begin to open, drying quickly at low heat to preserve the salicylate compounds that give the herb its traditional analgesic and anti-inflammatory reputation. The plant is the original source from which salicylic acid — the basis of aspirin — was first isolated.

plant_note: Plant rooted cuttings or nursery transplants after last frost; in zones below 8 overwinter indoors or take cuttings each fall.
directions: Root 4–6 in softwood cuttings in moist perlite; this shrubby herb from the Mexican highlands is unrelated to Mediterranean oregano but delivers a stronger, more citrusy-oregano flavor essential in chili, mole, and refried beans. Plant in sharply drained, lean soil in full sun; Mexican oregano is extremely drought-tolerant once established and actually develops more aromatic foliage under moderate stress. Prune plants lightly after each harvest to encourage fresh, leafy growth and prevent excessive woodiness. Harvest leaves and small branch tips at any time, drying them at low heat for the most authentic dried herb for Tex-Mex and Mexican cooking.

plant_note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 8–11 it overwinters and becomes a dependable perennial where French tarragon languishes in summer heat.
directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in warm starting mix or propagate by stem cuttings, which root readily in moist perlite. Mexican tarragon thrives in the same hot, humid summers that kill French tarragon and delivers a very similar anise-like flavor; harvest foliage throughout the season by pinching stem tips. It blooms in fall with small golden-yellow flowers that attract butterflies — you can allow blooming or deadhead to maintain leaf production. In zone 9–11 the plant is perennial; cut back hard in early spring to stimulate fresh growth.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; seeds germinate readily in cool soil and plants can handle light frost.
directions: Direct sow 1/4 in deep in early spring; milk thistle germinates readily in cool, moist conditions and self-seeds vigorously once established. Plants become quite large and spiny — space generously and wear gloves when tending them. Monitor flower heads closely as seeds ripen quickly and can be scattered by wind; harvest heads when the fluffy pappus begins to appear but before seeds fully disperse. Dry seed heads on a tray, then thresh to separate the hard achenes, which are ground or extracted for their silymarin content in liver-supportive preparations.

plant_note: Direct sow in early spring as the seed benefits from natural cold stratification or a brief refrigerator stratification period of 2–4 weeks.
directions: Direct sow in early spring or late autumn and allow natural freeze-thaw cycles to stratify the seed over winter for strong spring germination. Motherwort thrives in partial shade and moderately fertile, well-drained soil, often naturalizing along hedgerows and woodland margins. Harvest the upper third of flowering stems just as the lower flowers begin to open, using aerial parts fresh or tinctured in alcohol. Wear gloves when harvesting as the prickly calyces are sharp; avoid use during pregnancy due to its uterine-stimulating properties.

plant_note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant nursery divisions around the last frost date; cold-stratified seed can also be sown indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost.
directions: Divide clumps by prying them apart with two forks and replant in moderately moist, average soil in full sun to light shade; mountain mint naturalizes readily and spreads by rhizomes, though less aggressively than true mints. The leaves and silvery-white flower clusters are intensely menthol-scented — far more potent than peppermint — and are excellent as a native tea herb. Harvest leafy stems and the clusters of tiny white flowers in midsummer just as blooming begins for the most aromatic result. The prolific flowers are exceptional for native bees and other pollinators, making this a valuable dual-purpose plant.

plant_note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant root divisions around the last frost date; mugwort also self-seeds freely if flower stalks are allowed to mature.
directions: Plant root divisions in any well-drained soil; mugwort is extremely vigorous and can become invasive via rhizomes, so consider installing a root barrier or growing in a contained bed. The deeply lobed, silver-backed leaves are strongly aromatic with a bitter, sage-like scent used in traditional European and East Asian medicine. Harvest leafy stems just before flowering in midsummer for use in moxibustion, digestive bitters, dream pillows, and traditional holiday flavoring of fatty meats. Cut plants back hard in late summer to control spread and prevent self-seeding.

plant_note: Citrus-scented; propagate vegetatively.
directions: Propagate from cuttings to preserve the distinct orange-balsam fragrance of this thyme. The narrow grey-green leaves lend a citrusy note to poultry, fish, and marinades. Set plants in lean, fast-draining soil in full sun and keep water to a minimum. Shear lightly after bloom to maintain a tidy mound and prevent the base from going woody. Harvest sprigs throughout the season and protect crowns with gravel mulch over winter.
