directions: Sow 1/4 inch deep in warm mix at 80°F and pot up once seedlings have two true leaves; transplant to the garden after last frost. Fresno peppers resemble jalapeños but are slightly thinner-walled, fruitier in flavor, and ripen from green to bright red, with heat increasing as they mature (2,500–10,000 Scoville units). Harvest green fruits at 70 days for a jalapeño substitute, or ripen fully to red for a sweeter, hotter pepper that is excellent in hot sauces and salsas. Consistent deep watering (rather than frequent shallow watering) produces the best quality fruit and reduces stress-induced heat spikes.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; start a fall crop by direct sowing 8–10 weeks before first frost.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil amended with compost, and thin to 8–12 inches. Harvest the central flowering shoot when buds are tight and stems are still tender, cutting 6–8 inches down the stalk before any flowers open; leaving a few leaves on the stem encourages multiple side shoots. Keep soil evenly moist and feed with a balanced fertilizer at planting and again at mid-season. Hot weather causes rapid bolting and tough stems, so timing the crop for cool weather is essential.

plant_note: Direct sow around last frost date; established plants return each spring and produce for many years.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in well-drained soil and thin to 12–18 in; sorrel is a perennial that emerges early in spring and can be one of the first greens harvested each year. Remove flower stalks as they appear to keep the plant in vegetative production and prevent self-seeding. Harvest young, tender leaves 3–4 in long for the mildest lemony flavor; large, older leaves become coarser. Divide clumps every 3–4 years to rejuvenate the plant and control its spread.

plant_note: Plant cloves 4–6 weeks before the ground freezes in autumn so roots establish before winter; spring planting produces smaller bulbs.
directions: Break bulbs into individual cloves and plant each 2 in deep, pointed tip up, in well-amended soil enriched with compost; hardneck varieties are better for cold climates while softneck types store longer. Mulch the bed with 4–6 in of straw after the ground freezes to insulate and prevent heaving. Snap off hardneck scapes in early summer to redirect energy to the bulb. Harvest when the lower 4–5 leaves have dried to brown but 5–6 green leaves remain, then cure in a shaded, breezy spot for 3–4 weeks.

plant_note: Start indoors 12–14 weeks before last frost to allow enough growing time; transplant only after nights are reliably above 60°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep and maintain a constant soil temperature of 85–90°F; germination is slow and irregular, so start more seeds than needed and be patient. Transplant to a sheltered, heat-retaining bed after all frost danger has passed, and protect young plants from cool nights with row cover. Fruits turn from green to red (or yellow in some strains) at full maturity; wear gloves and avoid touching the face when harvesting, as the heat exceeds one million Scoville units. The extremely long growing season makes this pepper best suited to zones 9–12 or greenhouse culture in cooler regions.

plant_note: Start rhizome pieces indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; move outside only after all danger of frost has passed and temperatures are consistently warm.
directions: Break fresh ginger rhizomes into 1–2 in pieces each with a visible growth bud and pre-sprout them in a warm, moist environment for 2–3 weeks before planting 2 in deep in rich, moisture-retentive soil. Ginger thrives in dappled shade and resents direct afternoon sun in hot climates. Keep soil consistently moist and avoid letting it dry out. Harvest young ginger in as little as 4–5 months for milder flavor, or leave in the ground until foliage yellows for full-size, more pungent roots.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; expose seedlings to cool temperatures (50°F) for 2 weeks before transplanting to vernalize and trigger bud set.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep indoors and expose young plants to 2 weeks of 50°F nights to vernalize them for first-year bud production. Transplant into deeply amended, well-drained soil in a permanent location, as plants are long-lived when winters are mild enough. Harvest the central bud first, while bracts are tight and the stem snaps cleanly, then side buds as they develop. In zones 7–9, cut plants back to 12 in after harvest and mulch heavily for overwintering; divide clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.

plant_note: Germination can be erratic; sow a bit thicker to compensate.
directions: Golden beets offer a mild, sweet flavor without the earthy note of red types and do not bleed when cut. Because germination is often spotty, presoak seed for a few hours and sow slightly denser than usual. Thin ruthlessly to 3 inches so roots can size up without crowding. Keep beds weed-free and evenly watered to avoid tough, cracked roots. Harvest young for salads or at full size for roasting.

plant_note: Needs warm nights above 60 F to set fruit reliably.
directions: This Italian type bears glossy fruit striped in purple and white that fades to solid purple as it matures. Start seed indoors eight weeks before the last frost and transplant only after nights stay reliably above 60 F. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and stake plants that become top-heavy with fruit. Harvest while the skin is still glossy for the mildest, least seedy flesh. Consistent picking encourages the plant to keep flowering and fruiting.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; grain amaranth thrives in hot weather and tolerates drought and poor soil better than most grains.
directions: Surface-sow or barely cover seed 1/8 in deep, then thin to 12–18 in when seedlings are 2 in tall; amaranth seed is tiny and germinates readily in warm soil but competing weeds must be controlled in the first 3 weeks before the plant takes over. The tall, dramatic seed heads ripen from the bottom up — harvest when the top of the panicle begins to dry and seed crumbles easily when pinched. Cut heads into a bag or over a tarp, dry for 1–2 weeks, then thresh and winnow; a single plant can yield 1/4–1/2 lb of seed. Grain amaranth seed is gluten-free and high in lysine, making it nutritionally complementary to corn or wheat.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is reliably above 60°F; grain sorghum needs a long, warm season but withstands drought far better than corn.
directions: Drill seed 1/2 in deep in rows 24–30 in apart, thinning to 6–8 in within the row once plants are established; grain sorghum stalls visibly during drought but resumes growth when moisture returns, unlike corn which suffers permanent yield loss. The compact seed heads (panicles) are ready to harvest when seeds dent and the plant begins to dry down naturally, roughly 30 days after bloom. Cut heads by hand into bags or combine at low cylinder speed to avoid shattering; dry grain to 13% moisture or less before storage. Whole sorghum grain is gluten-free and can be popped like popcorn or ground into flour.

plant_note: Direct sow from 4 weeks before to the time of the last frost; succession sow every 3 weeks throughout the growing season.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in bands 3–4 in wide rather than single rows to maximize bed productivity. Thin seedlings to 1–2 in apart; the thinnings are already usable as tiny scallions. Water steadily to keep growth rapid and tender; drought causes tough, hot-tasting stems. Pull bunching types when stems reach pencil width, or cut leaves an inch above the base and allow them to regrow for a second harvest.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds shallowly, just 1/8 inch deep, in warm starting mix 6–8 weeks before the last frost. Transplant to the garden once nighttime temperatures stay above 50°F and provide moderate, consistent moisture. Fruits drop to the ground when ripe, so check beneath the plants frequently once husks begin to dry and turn tan. The papery husk protects fallen fruit and allows it to keep for several weeks at room temperature.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed into warm, well-drained soil.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 80°F and maintain consistent warmth until transplanting time. Plants grow tall and may benefit from staking; set into fertile, well-drained soil after last frost and side-dress with a balanced fertilizer when flowering begins. Long, narrow pods ripen from green to dark glossy red and must be fully dried for authentic guajillo chile flavor — harvest red ripe pods and dry in a food dehydrator or warm oven. The dried pods develop a distinctive tangy, berry-like flavor essential in Mexican mole and enchilada sauces.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost due to the long growing season; transplant 2 weeks after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep and maintain soil temperature at 85–90°F to achieve reliable germination, as Capsicum chinense species are notably slow to sprout. Transplant into the warmest, most sheltered spot in the garden once conditions are fully settled. Consistent feeding with a potassium-rich fertilizer during fruiting will maximize yields. Harvest when fruits reach their characteristic orange or red color and feel firm; handle with gloves due to extreme capsaicin levels.

plant_note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in spring; the long growing season requires an early start and the slow germination period means pre-soaking seeds is beneficial.
directions: Soak seeds in warm water for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination; sow 1/4 inch deep in deeply loosened, stone-free soil in rows, as seeds germinate slowly and unevenly over 2–4 weeks. Thin seedlings to 6–8 inches apart once established; the flat-leaf parsley-like foliage is edible throughout the season as an herb. The ivory-white, parsnip-like root develops slowly all season and tastes like a concentrated, slightly sweet parsley-parsnip hybrid — exceptional in Central European soups and stews. Harvest in fall after light frost improves sweetness; roots left in the ground over winter in milder climates will re-sprout in spring but roots lose quality as the plant prepares to bolt.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for spring crop; sow again in late summer for fall harvest.
directions: Sow seed at 1/8 in depth and thin to 8–12 in once seedlings are 2 in tall; butterhead types form loose, tender heads with buttery inner leaves. Keep soil evenly moist and mulch to maintain cool root temperatures, as heat quickly triggers bolting. Harvest heads by cutting at the base when they feel moderately firm but before the center begins to elongate, which signals imminent bolting. Outer leaves can be removed as the head develops to stretch the harvest period over several weeks.

plant_note: Sow at or just after last frost; hemp grows very quickly and tolerates light frost at seedling stage but is damaged by hard freezes.
directions: Drill seed 3/4–1 in deep at 20–30 lb/acre for grain production (denser stands reduce branching and improve yield); hemp requires a federal or state license to grow legally in the United States. Plants are dioecious — female plants produce the seed-bearing flowers; for grain production allow plants to go naturally to seed and harvest when seeds are firm and no longer milky. Thresh and dry seed to below 10% moisture for storage; hulled hemp seed (hearts) is nutritionally rich in protein and balanced omega-3/6 fatty acids. Do not confuse grain hemp with high-THC varieties; certified grain cultivars contain less than 0.3% THC.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil is warm; in short-season climates, start indoors 3 weeks early.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, well-drained soil enriched with compost, in hills of 4–5 seeds thinned to 2. Honeydew requires a longer, hotter season than cantaloupe; black plastic mulch is helpful in northern climates to boost soil warmth. Unlike cantaloupes, honeydews do not slip from the vine when ripe, so judge by skin color changing to creamy white, a waxy feel, and a sweet aroma from the blossom end. Cut fruits from the vine with a knife rather than pulling.

plant_note: Plant root cuttings 2–4 weeks before last frost; any root left in the soil will regenerate, so choose the site carefully as it becomes permanent.
directions: Plant 6–8 in root cuttings (thongs) at a 45-degree angle, 2–3 in deep, in deeply tilled, fertile soil; vertical planting produces rougher roots while angled planting encourages smoother, straight taproots. Horseradish is extremely vigorous and should be contained or given a dedicated bed where spreading is acceptable. Harvest main roots in autumn after the first hard frost triggers maximum heat and oil development. Save pencil-thick side roots to replant as next year's cuttings.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil has warmed to 65°F; Hubbard requires a long, warm season so time carefully against fall frost dates.
directions: Plant 3–4 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in deeply worked, fertile soil, thinning to two vines after establishment. Hubbard squash produces very large, warty fruits with a hard blue-gray or orange shell and very sweet, dry, fine-grained flesh ideal for pie and soup. Allow vines to spread freely over 8–12 feet of ground, or train onto a very strong trellis and support individual fruits in slings. Fruits are ripe when the rind resists a fingernail, the skin has turned its characteristic color, and the stem is fully dry; cure for two weeks before storing.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 1–2 weeks after frost danger passes once nights are consistently above 50°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm mix maintained at 78–82°F; germination is fastest and most even with bottom heat from a heat mat. Hungarian wax peppers produce thick-walled, banana-shaped fruits that ripen from pale yellow through orange to red; they range from mildly hot (1,000 Scoville) when yellow to moderately hot when fully red. Harvest yellow fruits at 65 days for pickling (the classic use), or allow to ripen through the color stages for fresh eating and sauces. Plants are heavy producers and benefit from a midseason application of balanced fertilizer to sustain yield.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in a warm starting mix and maintain soil temperature above 75°F for best germination. Transplant to the garden once nights are reliably warm and the soil is fully settled. For hotter fruit, allow stress by reducing water slightly as fruits mature. Harvest at 3–4 inches long while still firm and dark green, or leave on the plant until red for a milder, slightly sweeter heat.

plant_note: Earlier and more productive than large globe types in cool summers.
directions: Long, slender, thin-skinned fruit with tender flesh that cooks quickly and rarely needs peeling. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant into black plastic mulch to boost soil temperature in cooler regions. Feed with a balanced fertilizer and keep evenly watered to prevent bitter, seedy fruit. Cut fruit with pruners while young and glossy, typically at 6 to 8 inches long. These plants often outyield globe eggplants where summers are short or mild.

plant_note: Plant tubers 2–4 weeks before the last frost; Jerusalem artichokes are extremely cold-hardy and will regrow reliably each spring.
directions: Plant small, golf-ball-sized tubers 4 in deep in any well-drained soil; sunchokes tolerate poor, dry conditions and will crowd out weeds once established. Contain plantings with a buried barrier or dedicate a permanent bed, as any tuber left behind will regrow. Harvest tubers after the first hard frost sweetens them by converting inulin to sugars, digging with a fork and leaving the smallest tubers to perpetuate the planting. Store freshly dug tubers in the refrigerator as they do not keep well at room temperature.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks after last frost into warm soil; jicama needs a long frost-free season of at least 5 months and thrives in zone 9–11 Gulf Coast conditions.
directions: Sow scarified seeds 1 in deep in loose, deep, well-drained soil 2–4 weeks after the last frost once soil is thoroughly warm. Jicama is a vigorous vine requiring a sturdy trellis; pinch flower buds as they appear throughout the season — leaving flowers reduces tuber size dramatically since the plant diverts energy to seed set. Begin harvesting small tubers at about 5 months; larger tubers become more fibrous if left past 9 months. Only the roots are edible — all above-ground parts, including seeds and pods, contain rotenone and are toxic.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; jicama requires a long, frost-free season of at least 5 months to form substantial roots.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in warm, well-drained soil after soaking them overnight to speed germination. Remove all flower buds as they appear throughout the season; allowing flowering diverts energy from root development and shortens the season significantly. Vines can climb and should be trellised or allowed to sprawl; note that all aerial parts of the plant (seeds, pods, leaves) are toxic — only the root is edible. Harvest roots after the first cool weather of autumn when skins turn tan and cork-like.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks after frost danger has passed once soil is warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 75–80°F and transplant after last frost into fertile, well-drained soil. This Italian heirloom from Basilicata bears long, thin-walled, crinkled fruits that dangle in clusters and turn from green to brilliant red at maturity. The fruits are nearly heat-free and intensely sweet, making them outstanding for frying in olive oil, roasting, or drying. Harvest when fully red for peak sweetness, or dry on a string in a warm, airy location — the thin walls dehydrate rapidly and concentrate flavor.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches 65°F; count back from first fall frost to confirm enough growing days.
directions: Sow 2–3 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in deeply amended, compost-rich soil and thin to the strongest vine after the first true leaves appear. Kabocha is a Japanese winter squash of the Cucurbita maxima species; its flattened, dark-green fruits have dense, dry, sweet orange flesh superior to many other squash types. Harvest when the skin is very hard, the stem corky and dry, and a small section of rind scratched with a fingernail shows no damage. Cure at room temperature for 10–14 days before storing; properly cured kabocha keeps four to six months in a cool, dry room.

plant_note: Indian heirloom; heat-loving and quick to ripen.
directions: A striking Indian heirloom melon striped in green, orange, and cream with sweet, pale flesh. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil once nights stay warm. Train vines with room to sprawl and mulch to keep fruit clean and warm. The dramatic rind color develops as the fruit ripens, and mature melons detach easily from the stem. Cut back on water near harvest to concentrate the sugars in the flesh.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; sow again in late summer for a fall and overwintering crop.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep and thin to 12–18 in; kale tolerates crowding better than most brassicas but produces larger leaves with proper spacing. Side-dress with compost or a nitrogen fertilizer midseason to support continued leaf production. Harvest outer leaves first, leaving the growing center intact to keep the plant producing. Flavor becomes noticeably sweeter after the first hard frost, and plants often survive winter in zones 7 and warmer.

plant_note: Sow after last frost in warm soil; kenaf grows tall rapidly and is grown for bast fiber, animal fodder, and paper pulp.
directions: Direct-sow seed 1/2–1 in deep at 25–30 lb/acre for fiber production (narrower spacing promotes taller, less branched stems); kenaf is a warm-season annual related to okra that can reach 14 feet in a single season. For fiber, harvest at early flowering before stems become too woody — cut at ground level and ret the stalks in water for 10–20 days to separate the long bast fibers from the woody core. The core material (hurds) is useful as animal bedding or biomass fuel, while the bast fibers make durable rope, sacking, and paper. Young leaves and shoots are edible and nutritious — commonly cooked as a green in West African cuisine.

plant_note: Pickling type; succession sow for a steady supply.
directions: Kirby is the classic short, bumpy, thin-skinned pickling cucumber with crisp, dense flesh. Direct sow after the soil warms, either in hills or along a low trellis for cleaner fruit. Provide abundant, even water since pickling cucumbers are especially prone to bitterness and hollow centers under drought. Pick daily at 3 to 5 inches for pickling, as the vines produce heavily over a short window. Succession sowing every two to three weeks extends the harvest into late summer.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last spring frost, or sow in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Direct sow seed 1/4 in deep in cool, fertile soil and thin plants to 5–8 in to give the swollen stem room to develop. Keep soil consistently moist, as drought stress causes the bulb to become woody and strongly flavored. Harvest when the swollen stem is 2–3 in across for best tenderness; larger bulbs become fibrous. Pull the whole plant and trim the leaves before storing in the refrigerator.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring, or 6–8 weeks before first fall frost; succession sow every 2–3 weeks.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep, thinning to 4 inches for baby greens or 8 inches for full-sized plants. Komatsuna is one of the most versatile Asian brassicas — milder than mustard, more heat-tolerant than spinach, and remarkably cold-hardy. Begin harvesting at 30 days for tender salad leaves, or allow plants to reach full size at 8–14 inches for stir-frying and braising. Water consistently to keep leaves mild and succulent.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; succession sow every 2 weeks through spring, then again in fall.
directions: Scatter or row-sow seed on the surface or barely cover with 1/8 in of soil, as light aids germination. Thin to 6–10 in and use thinnings as baby greens in salads to avoid waste. Harvest outer leaves continuously once plants are 4–6 in tall, or cut the entire plant an inch above the soil line for a cut-and-come-again harvest. Bolting in warm weather makes leaves bitter; provide shade cloth in late spring to extend the season.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before the last frost; transplant seedlings into deep trenches after the last frost date.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep indoors in flats and thin to 2 in; transplant pencil-thick seedlings into 6-in-deep trenches, dropping each plant into a dibber hole and filling with water rather than soil to encourage a long white shank. Gradually fill trenches and hill up soil around stems as they grow to blanch the shanks. Leeks tolerate hard frost and can be left in the ground well into winter for a late harvest. Dig as needed once shanks reach 1 in or more in diameter.

plant_note: Sow in warm soil; seedlings resent cold, wet conditions.
directions: This heirloom bears round, yellow, lemon-sized fruit with sweet, mild, non-bitter flesh. Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil and grow on a trellis to keep the round fruit clean and easy to spot. Keep evenly watered to avoid bitterness and misshapen fruit during dry spells. Harvest while the skin is still pale yellow and before it turns deep gold and seedy. Frequent picking keeps the prolific vines producing until frost.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; lentils prefer cool growing conditions and deteriorate in summer heat.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in rows 18 in apart in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers that delay podding. The slender, branching plants benefit from brushy twigs as supports to keep pods off the ground and improve air circulation. Harvest the entire plant when the majority of lower pods have turned tan but before they shatter. Thresh by beating dried plants on a tarp and winnow lightly to clean seed before storage.

plant_note: Sow 2–3 weeks after last frost; lima beans demand warm soil and will rot in cold, wet ground.
directions: Sow seeds 1 to 1.5 in deep in loose, warm soil with the eye of the bean facing downward. Lima beans are sensitive to drought during flowering; irregular moisture can cause blossom drop and reduced pod set. For fresh shell use, harvest pods when they are bright green and plump but before they begin to fade or yellow. Allow pods to dry completely on the vine for dry bean storage.

plant_note: Heat-loving Spanish heirloom; mulch to retain warmth and moisture.
directions: A Spanish heirloom with teardrop fruit striped purple and white and a notably sweet, mild flavor. Give it a long warm season, starting seed indoors early and transplanting into the warmest bed available. Fruit skin is thin and bruises easily, so handle carefully and harvest with shears. Pick when fruit is 5 to 7 inches and still shiny, before seeds harden. Steady watering prevents blossom-end problems and keeps flesh creamy.

plant_note: Plant rhizome sections in still or slow-moving water 1–2 feet deep after last frost when water temperature stays above 65°F.
directions: Plant intact rhizome sections (with at least one growing tip) horizontally in the mud bottom of a pond, tub, or container submerged under 12–18 inches of still water; never allow the growing tip to dry out. Full sun is essential — lotus will produce only foliage and few flowers in shade. Harvest rhizomes in fall once foliage dies back by reaching into the mud and carefully pulling out the chain of large, starchy sections; the distinctive lacy, hole-patterned cross-section is a signature feature. Rhizomes must be cooked before eating; they are eaten stir-fried, pickled, or braised throughout East and Southeast Asia and have a crunchy texture even after cooking.

plant_note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed. Luffa requires a long, hot season.
directions: Nick or soak seeds for 24 hours before sowing 1/2 inch deep indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost to give this long-season crop a head start. Provide a very sturdy trellis, as vines can reach 15 feet and carry heavy fruit. For eating, harvest young fruits at 4–6 inches before the fibrous skeleton hardens. For sponges, allow fruits to fully mature and dry on the vine until the skin browns, then soak in water, peel, and rinse out the seeds.

plant_note: Sow 4–6 weeks before first frost for fall and winter harvest; also sow in very early spring as soon as soil can be worked.
directions: Broadcast or row-sow seed at 1/4 in depth in cool soil and keep moist; mache is extraordinarily cold-tolerant and grows slowly through winter in mild climates. Thin to 4–6 in for well-formed rosettes, though closer spacing produces acceptable baby greens. Harvest entire rosettes by cutting at the soil line, or pinch individual rosettes for salad use. Mache does not tolerate heat and will quickly bolt and disappear as spring temperatures rise; enjoy it as a winter green.

plant_note: Start indoors 4 weeks before last frost; transplant once nights stay above 55°F — the heat-season answer to spinach for zone 8–11 gardens.
directions: Soak seeds overnight before sowing to soften the hard seed coat; germination is slow but improves significantly with scarification and warm soil above 70°F. Provide a trellis or fence — the twining vines reach 6–10 feet and produce heavily when they climb. Harvest young leaves and stem tips frequently; the leaves are mucilaginous (similar to okra) and excellent in stir-fries, soups, and anywhere cooked greens are used. The red-stemmed variety (Basella rubra) is slightly more ornamental and equally productive — both tolerate heat and humidity that quickly kills regular spinach.

plant_note: Sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; it prefers cool, moist conditions and will self-sow prolifically if allowed to set seed.
directions: Broadcast seeds over moist soil and press lightly into contact; no covering is needed as seeds germinate best with light. Miner's lettuce is best sown in partial shade in mild climates, where it will often naturalize and self-sow. The distinctive round, perfoliate leaves (stem appearing to grow through the leaf) are mild and succulent — excellent raw in salads. Harvest whole plants or outer leaves before warm weather triggers bolting, which happens quickly once temperatures exceed 65°F.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; make a second sowing 6–8 weeks before first fall frost for autumn harvest.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in rows or broadcast thinly, thinning to 6–9 inches for full heads or leaving dense for cut-and-come-again baby leaves. Mizuna is extremely fast-growing and bolt-tolerant compared to most brassicas, making it one of the most reliable salad greens for both spring and fall. Begin harvesting outer leaves at 21 days or cut the whole plant an inch above the soil line and allow it to regrow. Consistent moisture promotes tender, mild-flavored leaves; drought causes bitterness.

plant_note: Heat-loving green; thrives in the hottest part of summer.
directions: Also called Egyptian spinach or jute mallow, molokhia is a heat-loving green that thrives when other leafy crops bolt. Direct sow after the soil is thoroughly warm, since seedlings need heat and stall in cool weather. Pinch the growing tips to encourage bushy branching and a continuous supply of young leaves. Harvest the tender upper leaves and stems, which turn silky and thicken soups and stews when cooked. Keep it well watered through summer, and pick regularly to delay flowering.

plant_note: Sow in cool soil; germination fails above 75 F.
directions: This large French heirloom spinach produces enormous smooth leaves and is known for fast, heavy yields. Sow seed half an inch deep in cool soil as early as it can be worked, since heat prevents germination and triggers bolting. Thin to 6 inches and keep beds evenly moist and rich in nitrogen for lush leaves. Harvest whole plants or pick outer leaves for a cut-and-come-again supply. Sow again in late summer for a fall crop that sweetens with frost.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; mung bean needs a long, warm, relatively dry summer to set seed.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in rows, thinning to 3–6 inches; inoculate with cowpea rhizobium inoculant before planting to ensure nitrogen fixation. Pods can be harvested young (50–60 days) for fresh shelling or snap use, or left on the plant to dry for dried beans or sprouting seeds. Pick pods when they turn black and rattle to collect dry beans; harvest in the morning before pods shatter. Mung beans are also a popular choice for home sprouting — the dry seeds germinate in a jar within 2–4 days.

plant_note: Very winter-hardy; can stand in the ground for winter harvest.
directions: This hardy Scottish heirloom leek withstands hard freezes and can be harvested through winter in many climates. Start seed indoors early, then transplant seedlings into deep dibble holes so the shanks blanch white. Hill soil around the stems or drop plants into holes to lengthen the tender white portion. Keep well watered and side-dress with nitrogen midseason for thick shanks. Dig leeks as needed all winter, lifting the last before spring regrowth begins.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; succession sow every 2–3 weeks and again in early fall.
directions: Broadcast or row-sow seed at 1/4 in depth and thin to 6–12 in; mustard germinates rapidly even in cool soil. Begin harvesting outer leaves when plants are 6–8 in tall for mild flavor, or allow larger leaves to develop for a stronger pungency. Hot weather causes rapid bolting and sharply increases bitterness, so time sowings to mature in cool conditions. Baby leaves can be cut at 3–4 weeks as a spicy salad green.

plant_note: For fall crop, direct sow 8–10 weeks before first frost; for spring, sow as soon as soil can be worked and harvest before summer heat triggers bolting.
directions: Direct sow 1/4 in deep in fertile, well-drained soil and thin to 12–18 in once seedlings are 3–4 in tall. Water regularly, as dry spells encourage premature bolting in warm weather. Fall planting generally produces better-quality heads with fewer pest problems. Harvest when heads feel firm and compact, cutting at the base before temperatures drop below 20°F.

plant_note: Direct sow after last frost when soil has warmed; soak seeds overnight to speed germination.
directions: Nick or soak seed for 24 hours before sowing 1/2 in deep after soil has warmed to at least 60°F; germination is slow and erratic without pre-soaking. Plants spread vigorously and fill space, so allow ample room of 2–3 ft per plant. Harvest by pinching stem tips of 3–4 in including the attached young leaves; regular harvesting keeps growth compact and productive through the entire warm season. Unlike true spinach, New Zealand spinach thrives in heat and is not bothered by long days, making it a valuable summer substitute.

plant_note: Plant tubers just before to just after last frost — oca needs a long, cool season and tuber formation is triggered by shortening day length in autumn.
directions: Plant small tubers 3 inches deep in loose, well-drained soil in early spring; oca is an Andean crop from the clover family and grows best in cool, moist conditions similar to potato, but requires a very long growing season. The plants produce clover-like foliage and only initiate tuber formation in response to the short days of autumn, so harvesting cannot occur until after the first frosts kill the foliage — typically November. Harvest tubers by digging carefully after frost kills the tops; tubers range from white to yellow, orange, red, and purple depending on the variety. Fresh-dug tubers are acidic and starchy but sweeten after a week of sun exposure that converts starch to sugars; eat roasted, boiled, or raw once sweetened.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches at least 65°F; okra thrives in heat.
directions: Soak seeds in water for 12–24 hours before sowing 1 inch deep to soften the hard seed coat and speed germination. Plant in the hottest, sunniest spot available, as okra is a true heat-lover and grows slowly in cool conditions. Side-dress with a nitrogen-rich fertilizer when plants reach 12 inches tall to promote vigorous growth. Harvest pods every 2–3 days when they are 3–4 inches long and still tender; pods left to mature become tough and woody and reduce plant productivity.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; seeds need cold stratification or cool soil to germinate well and the crop runs to seed in summer heat.
directions: Press seeds firmly into soil no deeper than 1/4 inch; germination is improved by pre-soaking seeds for a few hours before sowing. Orach grows quickly into an upright plant with red, gold, or green leaves that can substitute for spinach in any cooked preparation. Pinch the central growing tip when the plant is 12 inches tall to encourage bushy side growth and delay bolting. Harvest young leaves frequently; once flower stalks emerge, leaves become less palatable.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 1–2 weeks after frost danger passes.
directions: Start seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm mix at 78–82°F; transplant into fertile, well-drained soil with consistent moisture. Padrón peppers are the Spanish tapas classic — small, thin-walled, and mostly mild, but a percentage of fruits become significantly hot (especially those left to grow larger or harvested later in the season). Pick pods when they are 1.5–2 inches long and still green for the mildest flavor; fried in olive oil and salted, they are traditionally served as pimientos de Padrón. Consistent watering during fruit set prevents irregular heat and blossom drop.

plant_note: Round roots tolerate heavy or shallow soils where long types fail.
directions: This round French heirloom carrot forms golf-ball-sized roots that thrive in clay or rocky ground where long carrots struggle. Sow seed thinly and shallowly, keeping the surface damp until the slow seedlings emerge. Thin to 2 inches to allow the little globes to plump up evenly. Because roots are short, they mature quickly and are excellent for containers. Harvest young at about 1 to 1.5 inches across for the sweetest, most tender roots.

plant_note: Sow fresh seed 2–4 weeks before the last frost; parsnip seed has a very short viability window so always use seed from the current season.
directions: Sow 3 seeds per station 1/2 in deep in very deeply dug, stone-free soil and thin to the strongest seedling; long taproots will fork in compacted ground. Parsnip germination is notoriously slow and erratic — sowing radish seed in the same row acts as a quick-germinating row marker. Leave roots in the ground through autumn frosts, which convert starches to sugars for a sweeter harvest. Wear gloves when handling foliage in bright sun to avoid phototoxic sap burns.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature is at least 65°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, fertile soil in hills of 2–3 seeds, thinning to the strongest plant. Consistent moisture at the root zone keeps the bush compact and productive throughout the season. Pattypan's distinctive flying-saucer shape makes sizing easy. Harvest when fruits are 2–3 inches across for the most tender flesh; over-mature fruits become tough and seedy.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks after last frost; peanuts need a long, frost-free growing season of at least 120 days.
directions: Shell peanuts and plant seeds 2 in deep in loose, sandy, well-drained soil; heavy clay prevents proper peg penetration and pod development. After flowering, keep the area around plants free of weeds and loosen surface soil lightly so fertilization pegs can easily enter the ground. Harvest when foliage begins to yellow and the inner shell veins turn dark, by lifting the entire plant with a fork. Cure plants upside-down in a warm, airy location for 2–3 weeks before removing pods.

plant_note: Sow thickly and do not thin; crowding keeps bulbs small.
directions: Tiny pearl onions are grown deliberately crowded so each bulb stays small and round for pickling and boiling. Direct sow densely in a wide band and skip thinning, since competition limits bulb size. Keep the bed evenly moist and weed-free during the short growing period. Harvest when tops yellow and fall, then cure the little bulbs before storing or pickling. Blanching the cured bulbs in hot water makes peeling far easier.

plant_note: Direct sow at last frost date or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier; seeds require light to germinate so press onto the soil surface without covering.
directions: Press seeds onto warm, moist soil surface without covering, as light aids germination; cold stratification in the refrigerator for one week before sowing improves germination rates. Perilla is a heat-loving herb-vegetable used extensively in Japanese and Korean cuisines — red types as a colorant and flavoring, green types as a wrap and garnish. Harvest individual large leaves from the base of the plant upward, or cut the whole plant back by half to promote bushy regrowth. Pinch flower spikes as they emerge to extend the leaf harvest and prevent aggressive self-seeding.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–6 weeks after last frost; in zone 9–11 pigeon pea grows as a woody short-lived perennial and may produce for 3–5 years before replanting.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in well-drained, low-fertility soil; pigeon pea is a deep-rooted drought tolerant shrub that fixes nitrogen and does not require fertilization. It grows into a woody perennial in frost-free zones and produces pods over an extended season rather than all at once. Harvest green pods when plump for fresh shelling or allow to dry on the plant for dried dal. Prune back to 2–3 ft after the main harvest season to stimulate a second flush of growth and pods.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and transplant to fertile, well-drained garden soil once the risk of frost has fully passed. The heart-shaped, thick-walled fruits ripen from green to deep red and are sweeter and more aromatic than most bell peppers, with a rich, almost spicy fragrance that is distinct to the type. Harvest at full red for fresh use, or roast and peel for preserving in oil — pimento is the classic stuffing for green olives. High yields from compact plants make this a productive choice for containers or small beds.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks after frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and provide bottom heat around 80°F to speed germination. Transplant to fertile, well-drained soil once nights are consistently above 55°F. Water deeply but infrequently to encourage deep rooting and reduce disease pressure. Harvest dark green fruits at 4–5 inches long for fresh use, or allow to turn red and dry them for ancho chiles.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; erect a trellis or pole structure before sowing to avoid disturbing roots later.
directions: Plant seeds 1 in deep at the base of a sturdy trellis or teepee of poles set 6–8 ft tall, spacing 3 seeds per pole. Vines twine naturally but may need early guidance toward supports. Harvest pods before seeds bulge prominently for the tenderest texture. Pole beans produce over a longer season than bush types, so keep picking to prevent pod maturation from stopping new flower set.

plant_note: Direct sow at or up to 2 weeks after last frost once soil reaches at least 60°F. Isolate from sweet corn by at least 250 feet or time plantings to avoid simultaneous silking.
directions: Sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in blocks of at least 4 rows for proper wind pollination, isolating from sweet corn to prevent cross-pollination that ruins eating quality. Side-dress with nitrogen at knee-height and again when tassels appear. Leave ears on the stalk past eating stage until husks are completely brown and papery; pull husks back and hang ears in a dry, ventilated spot for 4–6 weeks until kernels release from the cob easily. Kernels are ready to pop when moisture content drops to around 14%.

plant_note: Plant seed pieces 2–4 weeks before last frost when soil reaches 45°F; in warm climates a second planting is made in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Plant certified seed potatoes cut to egg-sized pieces (each with 1–2 eyes) 4 in deep in trenches spaced 30 in apart. Hill soil up around the stems as they grow, keeping tubers covered to prevent greening from light exposure. Water consistently and watch for late blight in humid weather — infected foliage should be removed promptly to protect tubers. Harvest new potatoes when plants flower, or cure full-season types for a week at room temperature before long-term storage.

plant_note: Plant bulbs in fall 4–6 weeks before hard freeze in zones 5–9; in zones 3–4, plant as early as possible in spring.
directions: Plant individual bulbs 1 inch deep in well-drained, fertile soil — large bulbs planted in fall will multiply into a cluster of 8–12 medium onions, while small bulbs tend to produce fewer but larger bulbs. Potato onions are a multiplier onion type that propagates entirely by bulb division, forming a cluster of bulbs in the same way shallots do; no seed is needed or typically produced. Allow tops to fall over and begin to yellow before harvesting the entire cluster; cure in a warm, airy spot for 2–3 weeks. Save the smallest bulbs from the cluster for replanting, perpetuating the variety indefinitely without seed purchases.

plant_note: Sow after last frost; proso millet is the shortest-season grain available to home gardeners, maturing in as little as 60 days.
directions: Drill or broadcast seed 1/2 in deep at roughly 20–25 lb per acre in home-scale rows and thin to 4–6 in; proso millet germinates quickly in warm soil and produces an open panicle of seed in just 60 days, making it practical even in short-season gardens above zone 4. Harvest entire stalks when the lower seeds are hard and the panicle begins to droop; hang bundles upside-down over a tarp for 2 weeks to finish drying. Thresh by beating bundles against the inside of a barrel and winnow outdoors; the hull is loose and comes free with light rubbing in warm water. Proso millet is gluten-free and has the lowest water requirement of any grain crop.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost; time planting to mature before the first fall frost.
directions: Sow 3–4 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in deeply worked, compost-rich soil once soil temperature is at least 65°F, then thin to the 2 strongest seedlings. Large-vined types need 6–10 feet of space per plant; choose bush types for smaller gardens. Place a piece of cardboard or straw under developing fruits to prevent rot. Harvest when the shell is fully orange, the rind is hard throughout, and the stem has begun to dry and cork.

plant_note: Keep seedbed constantly moist during slow germination.
directions: These anthocyanin-rich carrots range from purple skin with orange cores to solid purple throughout. Sow the fine seed a quarter inch deep in deep, loose, stone-free soil and never let the surface dry during germination. Thin seedlings to 2 to 3 inches so roots develop without forking or crowding. Purple pigment intensifies in cool weather, so fall crops often show the deepest color. Harvest when shoulders reach three-quarters of an inch and store in cool, humid conditions.

plant_note: Consistent cool weather prevents buttoning; avoid heat stress.
directions: The vivid purple curds come from anthocyanins and do not require blanching like white types. Start seed indoors five to six weeks before transplanting and set plants out in rich, moisture-retentive soil. Keep growth steady and unchecked, since any stress from heat, drought, or transplant shock can cause premature small heads. Harvest while the head is still tight and dome-shaped, before the curds begin to separate. The color softens somewhat when cooked but stays vibrant if steamed briefly.

plant_note: Sow 2 weeks after last frost into warm soil; a quintessential Gulf Coast staple that thrives in heat and humidity that discourage northern legumes.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep directly into warm, well-drained soil — purple hull peas rot in cold, wet ground, so wait until soil consistently exceeds 65°F. Inoculate seed with cowpea rhizobium for maximum nitrogen fixation and avoid excess fertilizer, which promotes foliage over pods. Harvest pods when the purple color intensifies and the peas feel plump inside, shelling immediately or refrigerating; over-mature pods turn tan and the peas starch up quickly. Succession-plant every 3 weeks through summer for a continuous harvest; a second planting in late July produces a heavy fall crop in the Gulf South.

plant_note: Overwintering type; sow midsummer for spring harvest in mild zones.
directions: This hardy overwintering broccoli produces many small purple spears in early spring rather than a single central head. Start seed indoors in mid to late summer and transplant in early fall so plants establish before cold sets in. Provide steady moisture and rich soil, and mulch heavily to protect crowns through winter in zones 6 and 7. Harvest spears when buds are tight and cut regularly to encourage continued side-shoot production. Exposure to winter chill is what triggers the abundant spring flush.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; germination is erratic in cool soil below 65°F.
directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds or press them 1/8 inch into warm, well-drained soil; do not overwater as seeds rot easily in cool, wet conditions. Golden or green cultivated purslane is much larger and less bitter than its weedy relative, with thick, succulent stems and paddle-shaped leaves. Harvest stem tips 5–6 inches long when plants are lush and before flower buds form; the plant regrows readily after cutting. Purslane is one of the few plant sources of omega-3 fatty acids and is excellent raw in salads or lightly sautéed.

plant_note: Sow around the last frost date in spring; quinoa bolts in midsummer heat, so timing for a cool finish is critical in most climates.
directions: Broadcast or sow seed 1/4 in deep in firm, well-drained soil and thin to 12–18 in when seedlings are 3–4 in tall; quinoa is highly drought-tolerant once established and prefers cool, dry weather through seed fill. The colorful seed heads (red, orange, or cream) are ready to harvest when the seed falls easily from the panicle when rubbed — typically after the first light fall frost has dried the plant. Cut the seed heads into paper bags, dry for 1–2 weeks in a warm airy space, then thresh by rubbing heads between hands and winnow away the chaff outdoors in a breeze. Rinse seed thoroughly before eating to remove bitter saponins from the seed coat.

plant_note: For tight, colorful heads, sow in midsummer so radicchio heads up during cool fall weather, 10–14 weeks before first frost.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep and thin to 10–14 in; radicchio produces loose, green summer foliage before forming its characteristic red and white tight head as days shorten and temperatures fall. Cut back summer foliage to about 1 in when plants look tired and cool temperatures arrive; new heads will form from the crown within 3–4 weeks. Harvest compact heads by cutting at the base once they feel firm and the color is deep burgundy. Hard frost may damage outer leaves but the inner head often survives.

plant_note: Sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; succession sow every 10 days for continuous harvest, stopping when summer heat sets in.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 in deep in cool, well-drained soil at 10-day intervals for a steady supply. Radishes bolt quickly in heat or long days, so spring and fall are the prime windows. Thin to 2 in as soon as seedlings are up to prevent overcrowding, which causes lanky tops and small or misshapen roots. Pull spring types at 3/4 to 1 in diameter before they become hot and pithy.

plant_note: Transplant divisions or plant fresh seed in fall under deciduous hardwood canopy; ramps are woodland ephemerals that emerge in early spring and die back by early summer.
directions: Plant bulb divisions 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich woodland soil under deciduous trees that provide deep summer shade; ramps are native North American woodland plants and require the ephemeral light window of early spring before tree leaf-out. Seed germinates slowly and erratically, often taking 18 months for full emergence after a double dormancy period requiring warm then cold stratification; vegetative division of established clumps is the most reliable propagation method. Harvest sparingly — take no more than one leaf per plant or one bulb in three, leaving the colony to regenerate — as over-harvesting wild populations is a significant ecological concern. The entire plant (broad flat leaves, small white bulb, and stems) carries a strong, complex garlic-onion flavor and is celebrated as a spring delicacy across Appalachia.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil warms to 65°F; in short-season areas, start indoors 3 weeks before transplanting.
directions: Sow 2–3 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in compost-enriched soil and thin to one or two vines per hill once established. Red Kuri is a teardrop-shaped Cucurbita maxima with vivid orange-red skin and nutty, chestnut-flavored, dry flesh that is among the finest of all winter squash. Harvest when skin is deep orange-red and hard; the stems will be beginning to dry and the skin will resist a thumbnail. Cure for 10–14 days at room temperature before storing; fruits keep well for three to four months under cool, dry conditions.

plant_note: Direct sow at or just after last frost once soil is consistently warm; the heat-season replacement for spinach in tropical and subtropical gardens.
directions: Scatter seeds thinly on the soil surface — they need light to germinate — then press gently and keep moist; thin seedlings to 6 inches apart, using thinnings as baby greens. Leaves are brilliantly colored in shades of red, purple, and green and are mildly flavored, used in stir-fries, soups, and as a cooked green throughout South and Southeast Asia. Harvest outer leaves or cut entire young plants at 6–8 inches tall to encourage regrowth; plants that are allowed to flower and set seed will readily self-sow for the following year. This species thrives in the same hot, humid conditions that cause true spinach to bolt immediately.

plant_note: Direct sow 3–6 weeks before last frost for spring; sow again 6–8 weeks before first fall frost for the best-flavored autumn harvest.
directions: Scatter seeds thinly and cover with 1/4 inch of soil in a cool, moist seedbed; germination is rapid in cool conditions. Red mizuna has the same deeply-cut, feathery leaf form as green mizuna but with vivid burgundy-red stems and leaf coloring that intensifies in cool weather, making it as ornamental as it is edible. Harvest outer leaves continuously once plants reach 4 inches, or cut the entire plant 1 inch above the base to allow regrowth; flavor is peppery and nutty, similar to green mizuna but with slightly more bite. Fall sowings consistently outperform spring plantings in flavor and color intensity as frost sweetens the leaves.

plant_note: Sow only after soil has warmed thoroughly; cold soil rots seed.
directions: Provide a sturdy 7-8 ft trellis before sowing, as vines climb aggressively and produce deep burgundy pods up to 18 inches long. Direct sow two weeks after last frost when soil reaches at least 70 F, planting seeds 1 inch deep. Harvest pods young and tender at pencil thickness for best flavor and to keep plants productive. Pick every two to three days during peak season to prevent pods from becoming fibrous. Feed lightly with a balanced fertilizer since heavy nitrogen reduces pod set.

plant_note: Tender enough for baby-leaf salad harvest as well as mature use.
directions: This oleracea-type kale has flat, frilly gray-green leaves with purple stems and is notably tender and sweet. Direct sow half an inch deep and thin to 12 inches, or harvest thinnings as baby greens. It tolerates both heat and cold better than curly kale, making it a good four-season crop. Snap off outer leaves as needed and the plant will keep pushing new growth from the center. A light frost improves its mild, sweet flavor.

plant_note: Long-day Italian type; best where summers have long daylight.
directions: This elongated torpedo-shaped Italian onion has a mild, sweet flavor ideal for fresh eating and grilling. Start indoors and transplant early, giving each bulb room to elongate in loose, fertile soil. Maintain even moisture through bulbing to develop full size, then taper off as tops lodge. Because it is a fresh-use onion, it stores only a few weeks compared with storage types. Harvest when most tops have fallen and cure briefly before use.

plant_note: Plant divisions as soon as soil is workable in early spring; each division should have at least one large bud.
directions: Plant root divisions 3–4 in deep in deeply dug, compost-enriched soil, setting buds just at the soil surface. Do not harvest any stalks in the first year, and take only a few in year two so the crown can establish a deep root system. From year three onward, harvest stalks when they are 12–18 in long and 3/4 in thick by pulling and twisting at the base; remove any flower stalks that appear immediately to prevent weakening the crown. Note that only the stalks are edible—the leaves contain high concentrations of oxalic acid and are toxic.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 75°F; requires sustained heat to produce well.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in warm soil after last frost and provide a trellis or fence immediately, as vines climb vigorously by tendrils. Ridge gourd differs from smooth luffa in having ten prominent sharp ridges running the length of the fruit and slightly more tender flesh when young. Harvest fruits at 6–9 inches long while skin is still soft and the ridges are firm and green — the young flesh is mild, slightly sweet, and commonly used in Indian dal, stir-fries, and curries. Like smooth luffa, fruits left to fully mature develop a tough fiber skeleton and can serve as a scrubbing sponge.

plant_note: Start indoors 12–14 weeks before last frost; unlike most peppers, rocoto prefers cool nights and actually performs poorly in intense summer heat.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep at a cooler 65–70°F — unlike other peppers, Capsicum pubescens actually germinates better in cooler soil and is damaged by excessive heat. This Andean species is unique among cultivated peppers for its purple flowers and distinctive black seeds; it tolerates light frost better than any other pepper species. Transplant to a position with afternoon shade in hot climates, as rocoto stops setting fruit when temperatures exceed 90°F — coastal or mountain gardens suit it best. Fruits resemble small apples (hence 'manzano') and ripen to yellow, orange, or red with thick, sweet flesh and intense, burning heat.

plant_note: Direct sow or transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost; for fall, sow 8–10 weeks before first frost.
directions: Sow seed at 1/8 in depth in cool soil and thin to 8–12 in; romaine forms an upright, elongated head that needs slightly more space than loose-leaf types. Keep soil evenly moist to prevent tip burn, which appears as brown leaf edges due to calcium deficiency from moisture stress. Harvest by cutting the whole head at soil level when it is 8–10 in tall and the inner leaves feel firm. Outer leaves can be removed individually as the head develops to extend the harvest period.

plant_note: Start indoors and transplant so heads mature in cool fall weather; aim to transplant about 16 weeks before first frost.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep indoors 6–8 weeks before transplant date; romanesco demands consistent temperatures between 60–65°F during head formation or it fails to produce proper fractal spirals. Transplant into deeply amended soil and water evenly throughout the season, as any moisture stress affects head quality. Harvest the single central head when the spiral turrets are sharp and well-defined but before any yellowing begins. Unlike broccoli, romanesco rarely produces usable side shoots after the main head is cut.

plant_note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; roselle needs a long growing season and should be transplanted out only after all danger of frost is past.
directions: Start seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm mix; germination is fastest at 80–85°F. Transplant after last frost into fertile, well-drained soil and water deeply but infrequently once established. The calyces (fleshy sepals surrounding the seedpods) are the harvested vegetable, ready when they are plump, bright red, and still tender — about a week after the flower drops. Harvest calyces promptly every few days as they become fibrous if left on the plant too long; they are used fresh for teas, jams, and sauces.

plant_note: Sow around or just after last frost; runner beans prefer cooler summers than common beans and will drop flowers in intense heat.
directions: Sow seeds 2 in deep at the base of a robust trellis or obelisk at least 6 ft tall; runner beans are vigorous climbers and will overwhelm light supports. The scarlet flowers attract hummingbirds and are themselves edible. Pick pods frequently at 6–8 in before they become stringy; pods left to mature inhibit further flowering. In frost-free climates, tuberous roots can be overwintered and will regrow the following spring.

plant_note: Sow 12–14 weeks before the expected first autumn frost so roots mature in cool weather.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 in deep in fertile, well-drained soil; rutabagas need more time and space than turnips to produce their large storage roots. Thin to 6–8 in once seedlings are established and keep soil evenly moist to prevent splitting. Flavor is significantly improved by frost, so aim to leave roots in the ground until after the first hard freeze. Harvest before the ground freezes solid and store in a cool, humid root cellar for several months.

plant_note: Sow early in spring; safflower has a deep taproot that scavenges subsoil moisture, making it the most drought-resistant oilseed for dryland gardens.
directions: Plant seed 1 in deep at 6–10 in apart in rows 18–24 in wide; safflower needs a long growing season so early planting in cool soil is critical. The plant grows a large rosette in the vegetative phase, then bolts to form spiny, branching heads with brilliant orange or yellow florets. Harvest when the heads are dry and papery — cut and place in cloth bags to finish drying, then thresh by rubbing to release the seed. Safflower oil is prized for its high linoleic acid content and heat stability; the petals can also be used as a saffron substitute.

plant_note: Direct sow as early as 2–4 weeks before last frost; the long season requires early sowing and deep, loose soil.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in deeply dug, stone-free soil; salsify develops long taproots that fork and deform in rocky or compacted ground. Thin to 3–4 inches after germination and keep weed-free, as salsify's grass-like tops are easily overwhelmed early on. Roots are ready in autumn when they reach pencil thickness and finger length; flavor improves markedly after hard frost. Roots can overwinter in the ground and be harvested as needed through winter in mild climates, or dig all roots in late fall and store in damp sand.

plant_note: Sow as early as 2–4 weeks before last frost; scorzonera can also be sown in late summer for next-year roots, as it is a biennial in most climates.
directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in deeply loosened, rich soil; like salsify, roots fork in compacted ground. Scorzonera produces a black-skinned root with creamy white interior and a more refined flavor than salsify, often described as mildly nutty and sweet. Roots can be harvested in the first autumn when pencil-thick, but leaving them in the ground through winter and harvesting the following spring produces larger, sweeter roots. When harvesting, dig carefully as the roots are brittle and ooze a milky latex; peel under water to prevent discoloration.

plant_note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks after frost danger has passed once soil is thoroughly warm.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in moist starting mix, maintaining soil at 85°F for best germination results; this Capsicum chinense type is among the slower starters. Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil only after the garden is fully warm, and consider black plastic mulch to boost soil temperatures in cooler climates. The bonnet-shaped fruits ripen from green through yellow, orange, or red depending on the strain and carry a distinctive fruity aroma alongside intense heat. Use gloves when handling ripe fruits; they are essential in Caribbean jerk seasoning and hot sauces.

plant_note: Plant root cuttings (thongs) in early spring; plants establish slowly and should not be harvested until the second or third year.
directions: Plant root cuttings (thongs) 2–3 inches deep in deep, well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil with good lime content mimicking its native coastal habitat; sea kale is extremely drought and salt tolerant once established. The harvest is the blanched young shoots produced in early spring — cover crowns with a large pot or bucket to exclude light in late winter, and cut the pale, tender shoots when 6–8 inches tall. After harvesting the blanched shoots, remove the cover to allow the plant to photosynthesize and rebuild energy for the following year — do not blanch the same crown more than two consecutive years. The blanched shoots taste like a delicate cross between asparagus and cauliflower and have been a British garden delicacy since the 18th century.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm, moist starting mix and provide consistent warmth around 80°F for best germination results. Transplant into well-drained, fertile garden soil after the last frost, spacing plants to allow good airflow. Serranos are productive plants that benefit from light fertilization mid-season. Harvest small green fruits at 1–2 inches long for peak heat and crunch, or let them ripen to red for a slightly fruity flavor.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; sesame demands heat for germination and will rot in cool, wet soil.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in rows 18–24 in apart after soil reaches at least 70°F; sesame seed is very small, so mixing with dry sand aids even distribution and prevents clumping. Thin to 6–12 in once plants are 3 in tall and keep well-weeded until the canopy closes. The upright plants produce attractive tubular flowers along the stem that develop into rectangular seed capsules; the key challenge is timing harvest before 'shattering' — when ripe capsules split and scatter seed spontaneously. Cut entire stalks when the bottom capsules begin to yellow, invert plants into large paper bags, and allow to dry for a week indoors; the capsules then split easily and seed falls into the bag when shaken.

plant_note: Plant cloves 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; in mild climates, an autumn planting produces larger bulbs.
directions: Separate shallot clusters into individual cloves and plant each with the tip just at or slightly above the soil surface, 1 in deep, in fertile, well-drained soil. Each clove multiplies into a cluster of 6–12 new bulbs by season's end, so generous spacing pays off. Harvest when most of the green tops have toppled and turned papery. Dry clusters on screens in good airflow for 2–3 weeks and store in a cool, airy spot; well-cured shallots keep for 6 months or more.

plant_note: Sow as early as the ground is workable, typically 4–6 weeks before the last frost date.
directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep in rows 18 in apart and provide support for tall types. Consistent moisture from flowering through pod fill is critical; drought during this window sharply reduces yield. Harvest when pods are well rounded and the peas inside feel firm through the pod wall; sugar converts rapidly to starch after picking, so chill or eat immediately. Shell and freeze any surplus the same day for best sweetness.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes into warm soil.
directions: Sow 1/4 inch deep at 80°F in starting mix; shishito germinates somewhat faster than most peppers and grows into a productive, bushy plant. Harvest the thin-walled, wrinkled green pods at 2–4 inches long; roughly 1 in 10 pods will be surprisingly hot while the rest are mild, which is a characteristic of this variety rather than a flaw. Blistering or pan-roasting whole peppers (popped in a hot dry skillet until charred in spots) is the classic preparation. Production is heavy and consistent through summer; keep picking to prevent fruit from ripening fully to red, which slows new pod development.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; make a fall sowing 6–8 weeks before first frost for the best quality crop.
directions: Scatter seeds on prepared soil and cover lightly with 1/8 inch of soil; thin to 4–8 inches once seedlings are an inch tall and use thinnings in salads. Begin harvesting young shoot tips and leaves at 30 days by pinching the growing tip, which encourages the plant to branch and remain productive for several more weeks. The aromatic, slightly resinous leaves are a classic ingredient in Japanese hot pot and Chinese stir-fries, best harvested before the plant flowers. Flowers develop quickly in heat, so time the crop for cool weather or provide afternoon shade.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost or start indoors 6 weeks early; established clumps can also be divided in spring.
directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in well-prepared, deeply cultivated soil; skirret produces a cluster of multiple sweet, carrot-like roots that need loose ground to develop well. Keep well-watered through summer as drought stress causes woody, fibrous roots with an unpleasant core. Harvest in autumn after frost, washing and separating the finger-sized roots; any woody central strings are removed before eating. Divide clumps in early spring to propagate, replanting the outer sections and discarding the old center.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 75°F; a truly tropical vine requiring a long, hot summer.
directions: Nick the seed coat or soak seeds 24 hours before sowing 1/2 inch deep to improve germination rates in this heat-demanding tropical. Provide a tall, sturdy trellis or overhead structure — vines can exceed 12 feet and produce pendulous fruits that must hang freely to grow straight, reaching 1–3 feet in length. Harvest young fruits at 12–18 inches while skin is still soft and pale green-striped white; the tender young fruit is used like summer squash in South and Southeast Asian cooking. Overripe fruits develop an unpleasant bitter compound and turn orange-red, signaling they should be discarded rather than eaten.

plant_note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; a fall planting 8 weeks before first frost is equally productive in many climates.
directions: Sow seed 1 in deep as soon as the ground can be worked and provide twiggy brush or mesh netting for vines to climb. Pre-soaking seeds overnight can improve germination in cold soil. Harvest when pods are plump, fully filled, and glossy; snap peas pass their peak quickly in warm weather. Powdery mildew is common late in the season — choose resistant varieties when available.

plant_note: Sow 4–6 weeks before the last spring frost or in early autumn; cool weather produces the crispest, sweetest pods.
directions: Press seeds 1 in deep into cool, well-drained soil and erect support netting before the vines need it. Snow peas are harvested when pods are flat, bright green, and the seeds inside are barely visible; even slight bulging means the pod is overripe for stir-fry use. Keep pods picked daily during peak production to sustain cropping. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers, which produce lush foliage at the expense of pods.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches 65°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, compost-enriched hills, placing 3 seeds per hill and thinning to 1–2 plants. Give vines ample room to spread, or train onto a strong trellis and support heavy fruits with a sling. The rind turns from pale green to golden yellow when mature and will feel hard when pressed. Allow picked fruits to cure in a warm spot for 1–2 weeks before using or storing; they keep for several months in a cool, dry room.

plant_note: Sow 2–6 weeks before the first fall frost; spelt establishes before winter dormancy and resumes vigorous growth in early spring.
directions: Drill or broadcast seed at 80–100 lb per acre (approximately 2–3 oz per 100 sq ft) in fall to 1 in depth; spelt is taller than modern wheat and slightly more susceptible to lodging in rich soils, so avoid high-nitrogen amendments. It tolerates clay and compacted soils better than most small grains and has reasonable resistance to rust diseases. Harvest at hard dough stage in early summer by cutting, sheaving, and drying for a week before threshing; spelt retains a tough hull (spikelet) that requires a dedicated pearler or dehuller before milling. The resulting flour has a mild, nutty flavor and is widely used by those who tolerate ancient wheats better than modern varieties, though it does contain gluten.

plant_note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost in spring; also sow in fall 6–8 weeks before first frost for overwintering where winters are mild.
directions: Sow seed 1/2 in deep in fertile, slightly alkaline soil and keep evenly moist; germination is slow in warm soil above 70°F. Thin to 3–6 in and fertilize with nitrogen to encourage lush, dark-green growth. Begin harvesting outer leaves once plants have 6–8 leaves, or cut the whole plant about 1 in above the crown for regrowth. Bolting is triggered by long days and heat; choose slow-bolt varieties for extended spring harvests.

plant_note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil is warm.
directions: Plant seeds 1 inch deep in warm, loose, well-drained soil enriched with aged compost. Thin to one plant per hill and keep the bed consistently moist throughout the growing season. Monitor frequently for squash vine borers and squash bugs, which are the most serious pests. Harvest fruits at 6–8 inches long while the skin is still soft and pale yellow for the best flavor and texture.

plant_note: Compact icebox type suited to shorter seasons and small gardens.
directions: This compact icebox watermelon produces round 8 to 10 pound fruit with sweet red flesh on shorter vines. Start seed indoors or direct sow once soil is thoroughly warm, and use black mulch in cool regions. Water steadily during vine growth and fruit set, then ease off as melons ripen to intensify sweetness. Signs of ripeness include a yellowing ground spot and a dried tendril nearest the fruit. Because it matures relatively quickly, it succeeds even where summers are short.

plant_note: Direct-sow at or after last frost; oilseed sunflower varieties have smaller, black-hulled seeds with a higher oil percentage than confection types.
directions: Plant seed 1 in deep and 8–12 in apart in rows spaced 24–30 in; oilseed varieties like 'Peredovik' or 'Rostov' are grown more densely than confection types to reduce branching and produce a single large head per plant. Harvest when the back of the head turns yellow to brown and the seeds are hard — cut heads and hang upside-down in a covered, airy space to cure. Rub seeds out over a screen, then cold-press for oil or roast and use as a whole food. Birds are the main pest; protect ripening heads with netting or paper bags.

plant_note: Direct sow at last frost date once soil temperature reaches at least 55°F; sow in blocks of at least 4 rows for good pollination.
directions: Sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in blocks rather than single rows to ensure adequate wind pollination; block width should be at least 4 rows. Side-dress with nitrogen fertilizer when plants reach knee-height and again at tasseling to support high yields. Silks turn brown and dry about 20 days after they appear, signaling readiness; peel back the husk and pierce a kernel to check for milky juice as the ripeness test. Eat or preserve immediately after harvest for maximum sweetness, especially with modern supersweet varieties.

plant_note: Set out slips 2–4 weeks after last frost when soil is reliably warm; sweet potato vines are extremely cold-sensitive.
directions: Plant rooted slips 4 in deep in well-drained, loose soil on raised ridges or mounded rows to warm quickly; slips are propagated by sprouting cured roots in a warm, humid space 6–8 weeks before planting out. Resist fertilizing heavily with nitrogen, which favors vines over tubers. Harvest before the first frost by carefully lifting roots with a broad fork; any nicks will rot in storage. Cure freshly dug sweet potatoes at 85–90°F with high humidity for 10 days before storing at 55–60°F.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; also sow in late summer for a fall crop.
directions: Each chard seed is actually a cluster of seeds, so thin aggressively to 6–12 in once seedlings establish; sow 1/2 in deep. Swiss chard tolerates more summer heat than spinach, making it a valuable warm-gap green as long as irrigation is consistent. Harvest outer stalks when 8–10 in long, cutting cleanly at the base without disturbing the crown. Plants will produce continuously until heavy frost; mulching extends the season into early winter in temperate climates.

plant_note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed to at least 65°F.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and provide consistent heat of 80°F until seedlings emerge. Transplant to well-drained soil in the sunniest garden position, as Capsicum frutescens types thrive with maximum heat accumulation throughout the season. Distinctive erect fruits ripen from yellow-green through yellow and orange to red; harvest at the red stage for the highest capsaicin concentration. Plants are short-lived perennials in frost-free climates and can be overwintered in pots to produce a second heavy crop.

plant_note: Plant corms at or just after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; in zones 8–11 taro may stay in the ground year-round.
directions: Plant small corms or corm sections 2–3 inches deep in moist to wet, organically rich soil; taro grows naturally in waterlogged conditions and tolerates standing water at the margins of ponds and streams. Provide afternoon shade in the hottest climates to prevent leaf scorch, and keep the soil consistently moist throughout the growing season — drought stress greatly reduces corm size. Harvest when the large shield-shaped leaves begin to yellow and die back, typically 6–9 months after planting, carefully digging corms to avoid damage. The corms must be thoroughly cooked before eating to neutralize calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense mouth irritation in raw form; young leaves and stems are also edible when cooked.

plant_note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; excels as a fall crop sown 6–8 weeks before first frost, as light freezes sweeten the leaves.
directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep and thin to 6–12 inches for full rosettes; closer spacing produces baby greens for salad mix. Tatsoi forms a flat, ground-hugging rosette of dark spoon-shaped leaves and is remarkably cold-hardy, surviving temperatures into the low 20s°F under row cover. Harvest whole plants when 4–6 inches across or pick outer leaves progressively. Avoid planting in summer as heat causes immediate bolting.

plant_note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; teff tolerates both waterlogged and drought conditions better than most grain crops.
directions: Surface-broadcast seed — it is extremely small (poppy-seed sized) and must not be buried; mix seed with sand to achieve even distribution at roughly 2–3 lb per 1,000 sq ft, then press lightly with a roller or board for good soil contact.
